topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Need for quality - Evolution of quality - Definitions of quality - Dimensions of product and service quality - Basic concepts of TQM - TQM Framework - Contributions of Deming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Quality" — The KTU 2024 Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Quality** is formally defined as the **"totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."** This is a direct adoption of the **ISO 8402 / ISO 9000:2015** definition, which is the benchmark language KTU examiners expect in TQM answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition of Quality**
> The three operative words in any KTU 14-mark definition are: **Totality of Characteristics**, **Ability**, and **Satisfy Needs (stated + implied)**. Writing only one or two of these will cost you 2–3 marks.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Dinner" Intuition
Imagine you order a Biryani from a restaurant on a Friday night.
- You explicitly **stated** that you need food, hygiene, and taste.
- But you **implied** that you need reasonable price, polite service, and a clean table.
If the restaurant delivers on **both** stated and implied needs, the *biryani experience* is of high quality. Quality, therefore, is **NOT just the product itself** — it is the **entire experience of value delivery**. This is why TQM shifts the focus from "inspecting defects in food" to "designing the whole kitchen and customer journey to prevent any dissatisfaction."

## 1.2 Why is Quality Needed? — The Need for Quality

Modern engineering and service enterprises operate in a **VUCA (Volatile, Uncertain, Complex, Ambiguous)** marketplace. The need for quality arises from four universal pressures:

| # | Pressure | Engineering / Business Reality |
|---|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Pressure** | Globalized customers have multi-brand choices; switching cost is near zero. |
| 2 | **Competitive Pressure** | Quality is the single largest sustainable differentiator in mature industries. |
| 3 | **Cost Pressure** | Poor quality = **P.O.S.T.** (Prevention-Only Savings Theory) reversal — cost of rework, warranty, returns, and brand damage. |
| 4 | **Statutory / Regulatory Pressure** | ISO 9001, BIS, CE, FDA, Six Sigma mandates in B2B contracts. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:** The "Need for Quality" question is a guaranteed 7-marker. Always structure your answer under **3 To 4 Headings** (Customer satisfaction, Competitive advantage, Cost reduction, Productivity improvement, and Statutory compliance). Avoid writing a single paragraph — the examiner's marking scheme allocates **1 mark per heading explained**.

## 1.3 Quality Gurus — The TQM Trinity at a Glance

Every KTU Module 1 question on TQM foundations expects familiarity with the "Three Crowned Kings" of Quality:

- **W. Edwards Deming** — Statistical Process Control, 14 Points, System of Profound Knowledge.
- **Joseph M. Juran** — Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement), Fitness for Use.
- **Philip B. Crosby** — Zero Defects, Quality is Free, "Doing it right the first time" (DIRFT).

## 1.4 TQM — The Operational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TQM (Total Quality Management)** is a **philosophy + set of guiding principles + management system** that focuses on achieving **Customer Satisfaction** through the **Total Involvement** of every member of the organization in the **Continual Improvement** of products, services, and processes.

The word **TOTAL** in TQM captures **three dimensions** that KTU examiners love to test:
1. **Total Quality** — quality in every output, not just finished goods.
2. **Total Involvement** — every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, participates.
3. **Total Process** — every step is part of an integrated process view.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Quality — The Six Eras

Quality thinking did not appear in a vacuum. It evolved through **six clearly demarcated eras** that every KTU Module 1 student must know cold. The examiner's standard 14-mark question is: *"Trace the evolution of quality management from inspection to TQM."*

| Era | Time Span | Core Focus | Key Figures | Limitation |
|-----|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| 1. **Inspection** | Pre-1900s – 1930s | Detect defects in finished goods | Frederick Taylor | Reactive, no prevention |
| 2. **Quality Control (QC)** | 1930s – 1950s | Statistical tools, sampling | Walter Shewhart, Dodge & Romig | Limited to manufacturing |
| 3. **Quality Assurance (QA)** | 1950s – 1970s | System-wide prevention | Armand Feigenbaum, Joseph Juran | Bureaucratic, system-heavy |
| 4. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | 1970s – 2000s | Customer focus, total involvement, culture | Deming, Juran, Ishikawa, Crosby | Cultural change is hard |
| 5. **Six Sigma** | 1986 – Present | Defect reduction via DMAIC | Motorola, Jack Welch (GE) | Project-focused, not always cultural |
| 6. **Lean / Lean Six Sigma** | 1990s – Present | Waste elimination + variation reduction | Toyota (Taiichi Ohno), Womack | Risk of over-rationalization |

> [!NOTE]
> **TQM vs Six Sigma vs Lean — A KTU Favourite**
> - **TQM** = Cultural philosophy (broad, deep, long-term)
> - **Six Sigma** = Project-based methodology (narrow, statistical, short-term)
> - **Lean** = Operational system (fast, waste-focused)
> Modern practice integrates them into **Lean Six Sigma** or **TQM-SS frameworks**.

## 2.2 Definitions of Quality — The Five Schools of Thought

KTU examiners expect students to know **at least 4 to 5 definitions** of quality, because the word "quality" has meant different things to different thinkers:

1. **Transcendent Definition** — Quality is an innate, unanalysable property of excellence (Pirsig). *"I cannot define it, but I know it when I see it."*
2. **Product-Based Definition** — Quality is a measurable variable (e.g., purity, tensile strength).
3. **User-Based Definition** — Quality is fitness for the **intended user's purpose** (Juran).
4. **Manufacturing-Based Definition** — Quality is **conformance to specifications** (Crosby, Taguchi).
5. **Value-Based Definition** — Quality is the balance of **performance vs price paid** (Feigenbaum).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Killer Question:** "Compare the User-Based and Manufacturing-Based definitions of quality." Use a comparative table + write 2 short paragraphs on how each definition drives a different quality management strategy.

## 2.3 Dimensions of Product Quality — David A. Garvin's 8 Dimensions

Garvin (1987) proposed **8 dimensions** that have become the **gold standard** in TQM literature and KTU examinations. Every product has these 8 attributes, and TQM aims to maximize each:

| # | Dimension | Definition | Example (Smartphone) |
|---|-----------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics | Processor speed, battery hours |
| 2 | **Features** | Secondary, "nice to have" attributes | Face unlock, dark mode |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation over time | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Degree to which product meets pre-defined specs | Meeting ISO/IEC standards |
| 5 | **Durability** | Useful life before replacement is necessary | Drop-test cycles passed |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Speed, courtesy, and ease of repair | Doorstep service in 24 hrs |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal — look, feel, sound, smell | Bezel-less design |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Customer's subjective judgement based on brand/word-of-mouth | "Apple feels premium" |

## 2.4 Dimensions of Service Quality — SERVQUAL (Parasuraman et al., 1988)

Because services are **intangible, heterogeneous, inseparable, and perishable (IHIP)**, Garvin's product dimensions are insufficient. **SERVQUAL** uses **5 generic dimensions**, scored across 22 expectation/perception pairs:

| # | Dimension | Meaning | Example |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Tangibles** | Physical evidence, equipment, personnel appearance | Clean hospital, smart uniform |
| 2 | **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably | Train arrives on time, parcel delivered in 48 hrs |
| 3 | **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers promptly | Quick grievance redressal |
| 4 | **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy inspiring trust & confidence | Trained doctor, certified mechanic |
| 5 | **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention given to customers | Calling patient by name |

### The KTU-High-Yield Service Quality Gap Model

Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1985) identified **5 Gaps** that cause service quality failures. This is the **most-asked 14-mark TQM question** in any KTU 2024 exam.

**Gap 1** = Customer Expectation vs Management Perception
**Gap 2** = Management Perception vs Service Quality Specification
**Gap 3** = Service Quality Specification vs Service Delivery
**Gap 4** = Service Delivery vs External Communications
**Gap 5** = Customer Expectation vs Perceived Service (THE KEY GAP)
**Gap 6** (added later) = Customer Expectation vs Perceived Service (filtered through customer perception)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Cost of Quality + Six Sigma)

Although TQM is qualitative, the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** and **Six Sigma yield formulas** appear in numerical 7-mark problems. Critical rule: never use the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard Value | Application |
|---------|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = P + A + IF + E$ | 15-40% of sales | Total quality cost to firm |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units Out}}{\text{Total Units In}} \times 100$ | TQM target: 95%+ | Process efficiency |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Six Sigma target: 0.99966 | Multi-step process |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | 6σ: 3.4 | Process maturity |
| **Process Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(1.5 / (DPMO \div 10^6)) + 1.5$ | 6σ = world class | Capability index |
| **Cp Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $\geq 1.33$ acceptable | Process capability |
| **Cpk Index** | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | $\geq 1.33$ acceptable | Centred capability |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan $\rightarrow$ Do $\rightarrow$ Check $\rightarrow$ Act | Iterative | Deming/Shewhart |

> Where $P$ = Prevention cost, $A$ = Appraisal cost, $IF$ = Internal Failure cost, $E$ = External Failure cost.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** model is widely deployed in **manufacturing plants, IT service desks, and BPO operations** to justify TQM investments. A typical Indian automotive OEM like **Tata Motors** has reported CoQ reductions from **8% of revenue to 2.5%** within 5 years of TQM adoption — directly translating to **hundreds of crores** in annualized savings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — The Service Quality Gap Model (Gaps 1 to 5)

This is the **analytical backbone** of any 14-mark TQM answer. Let us derive the cumulative service quality failure equation step by step.

**Step 1: Define Customer Expectation (E).**
Customer Expectation is shaped by:
- Word-of-mouth communication
- Personal needs
- Past experience
- External communications (advertising, branding)

$$E = w_1 \cdot WoM + w_2 \cdot PN + w_3 \cdot PE + w_4 \cdot EC$$

**Step 2: Define Management Perception (MP).**
What management *thinks* the customer wants.

$$MP = f(\text{Information Quality, Upward Communication, Management Philosophy})$$

**Step 3: Gap 1 — The Listening Gap.**
$$Gap_1 = E - MP$$

A large Gap 1 means **management does not understand the customer**. KTU-mandated remedies: **market research, customer focus groups, Voice of Customer (VOC) studies, complaint analysis**.

**Step 4: Define Service Quality Specification (SQS).**
The formal standard management sets for the service.

$$SQS = g(\text{Quality Standards, Resource Allocation, Goal Setting})$$

**Step 5: Gap 2 — The Design Gap.**
$$Gap_2 = MP - SQS$$

Remedies: **Quality function deployment (QFD), service blueprinting, blueprinting tools, setting SMART service standards**.

**Step 6: Define Service Delivery (SD).**
The actual service produced and delivered by front-line staff.

$$SD = h(\text{Staff Training, Technology, Process Capability})$$

**Step 7: Gap 3 — The Performance Gap.**
$$Gap_3 = SQS - SD$$

Remedies: **Training, empowerment, technology upgradation, performance management**.

**Step 8: Define External Communication (EC).**
The promise made via marketing / advertising.

**Step 9: Gap 4 — The Communication Gap.**
$$Gap_4 = SD - EC$$

A common cause: marketing over-promises, operations under-delivers.

**Step 10: Define Perceived Service (PS).**
The customer's view of the *actual* service received.

**Step 11: Gap 5 — The Customer Gap (THE critical gap).**
$$Gap_5 = E - PS$$

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$

This is the **famous additive identity** KTU examiners love to test. It states that **the total service quality gap is the sum of the four provider-side gaps**. Closing Gap 5 is the central purpose of TQM in service industries.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Deming's Chain Reaction (Quality → Cost → Productivity → Market Share)

Deming proposed a **causal chain** that mathematically justifies TQM investment. Let us walk through the logical derivation step by step.

**Step 1: Improve Quality.**
$$Q \uparrow \implies \text{Defects} \downarrow$$

**Step 2: Reduce Rework and Waste.**
$$\text{Rework} \downarrow \implies \text{Resource Consumption} \downarrow$$

**Step 3: Lower Per-Unit Cost of Production.**
$$C_{\text{per unit}} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Output (including good + rework units)}}$$

As output of good units rises (denominator increases while numerator stays constant), $C_{\text{per unit}}$ falls.

**Step 4: Improve Productivity.**
$$\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output Value}}{\text{Input Cost}}$$

Lower cost + higher value = higher productivity.

**Step 5: Capture More Market Share.**
Better quality + lower price = more customers.

**Step 6: Stay in Business.**
The chain reaction ends in long-term survival and growth:

$$Q \uparrow \rightarrow C \downarrow \rightarrow \text{Productivity} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Market Share} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Survival} \uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Trick:** If the question is *"Explain Deming's chain reaction"*, write the chain as a single sentence in the answer: *"Improve quality, decrease costs, improve productivity, capture market share, and stay in business."* This is worth **3 marks by itself** in a 7-mark question.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — TQM Framework (A 14-Mark Template)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects a **structured TQM framework**. The universally accepted framework has **5 building blocks**:

1. **Customer Focus** — the *why* of TQM.
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — the *who*.
3. **Continual Improvement** — the *how*.
4. **Process-Centric Approach** — the *mechanism*.
5. **Integrated System + Fact-Based Decision Making + Supplier Partnership** — the *enablers*.

Mathematically, the TQM effectiveness index is often written as:

$$E_{TQM} = f(C, E, I, P, S, D)$$

Where:
- $C$ = Customer Focus index
- $E$ = Employee Involvement index
- $I$ = Improvement rate (Kaizen events per quarter)
- $P$ = Process capability index ($C_{pk}$)
- $S$ = Supplier quality rating
- $D$ = Data-driven decision coverage (%)

A high $E_{TQM}$ value (typically scaled 0–100) is a strong predictor of long-term firm performance in KTU case-study questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Evolution of Quality (Six Eras)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    era1["Era 1: Inspection\nPre-1900s to 1930s\nDetect defects"]
    era2["Era 2: Quality Control\n1930s to 1950s\nStatistical tools"]
    era3["Era 3: Quality Assurance\n1950s to 1970s\nSystem prevention"]
    era4["Era 4: TQM\n1970s to 2000s\nCustomer focus culture"]
    era5["Era 5: Six Sigma\n1986 to Present\nDMAIC methodology"]
    era6["Era 6: Lean Six Sigma\n1990s to Present\nWaste and variation"]
    era1 --> era2 --> era3 --> era4 --> era5 --> era6
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — TQM Framework (5-Pillar Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tqm["TQM Framework\nCustomer Satisfaction and\nLong Term Success"]
    c["Pillar 1\nCustomer Focus"]
    e["Pillar 2\nTotal Employee Involvement"]
    i["Pillar 3\nContinual Improvement Kaizen"]
    p["Pillar 4\nProcess Centric Approach"]
    s["Pillar 5\nIntegrated System\nFact Based Decisions\nSupplier Partnership"]
    tqm --- c
    tqm --- e
    tqm --- i
    tqm --- p
    tqm --- s
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (4 Parts)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    deming["Deming System of Profound Knowledge"]
    sys["Part 1: Appreciation for a System\nUnderstanding the entire value chain"]
    var["Part 2: Knowledge about Variation\nCommon vs special causes"]
    know["Part 3: Theory of Knowledge\nPrediction via theory and experiment"]
    psy["Part 4: Psychology\nUnderstanding human nature"]
    deming --> sys
    deming --> var
    deming --> know
    deming --> psy
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Deming's 14 Points (Grouped into 4 Themes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    deming14["Deming 14 Points for Management"]
    grp1["Theme A: Constancy of Purpose\nPoints 1 to 5"]
    grp2["Theme B: Education and Self Improvement\nPoints 6 to 8"]
    grp3["Theme C: Optimization of the System\nPoints 9 to 12"]
    grp4["Theme D: Leadership for Transformation\nPoints 13 to 14"]
    deming14 --> grp1
    deming14 --> grp2
    deming14 --> grp3
    deming14 --> grp4
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Service Quality Gap Model (Parasuraman)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    e["Customer Expectation E"]
    mp["Management Perception MP"]
    sqs["Service Quality Specification SQS"]
    sd["Service Delivery SD"]
    ec["External Communication EC"]
    ps["Perceived Service PS"]
    g1["Gap 1\nE minus MP\nListening Gap"]
    g2["Gap 2\nMP minus SQS\nDesign Gap"]
    g3["Gap 3\nSQS minus SD\nPerformance Gap"]
    g4["Gap 4\nSD minus EC\nCommunication Gap"]
    g5["Gap 5\nE minus PS\nCustomer Gap"]
    e --> g1 --> mp
    mp --> g2 --> sqs
    sqs --> g3 --> sd
    sd --> g4 --> ec
    e --> g5
    sd --> g5
    ps --> g5
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plan["PLAN\nIdentify problem and analyze process"]
    do["DO\nImplement the solution on small scale"]
    check["CHECK\nMeasure results and compare to expected"]
    act["ACT\nStandardize or revise and iterate"]
    plan --> do --> check --> act --> plan
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define Quality. Mention any two definitions of quality.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question | Marks: 3]**

**Model Answer:**
Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 8402).
- **User-Based Definition (Juran):** Quality is fitness for the intended user's purpose.
- **Manufacturing-Based Definition (Crosby):** Quality is conformance to requirements/specifications.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between Quality Control and Quality Assurance?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question | Marks: 3]**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Focus | Detecting defects in output | Preventing defects in the process |
| Approach | Reactive, statistical sampling | Proactive, system-wide |
| Scope | Typically a department | Entire organization |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Long-Answer Questions (With Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question | Module 1 | Internal Choice Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Define TQM. Explain the basic concepts and key elements of TQM in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
TQM is a management philosophy focused on achieving customer satisfaction through total involvement and continual improvement of products, services, and processes.
**Basic Concepts:** Customer focus, total employee involvement, continual improvement, process approach, integrated system, fact-based decision making, supplier partnership, performance measurement.
**Key Elements:** Leadership commitment, customer satisfaction, training and education, supplier partnership, process management, continual improvement tools (PDCA, Six Sigma, Kaizen), and performance measurement.

**Q. (b)** Explain Garvin's eight dimensions of product quality with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Already covered in detail in Section 2.3 above. Write a definition for each of the 8 dimensions plus one engineering example per dimension. Use a comparison table for clarity.

---

### QUESTION B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question | Module 1]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the contributions of W. Edwards Deming to the field of TQM in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Deming's contributions include:
1. **System of Profound Knowledge** — 4 parts: system appreciation, variation, theory of knowledge, psychology.
2. **14 Points for Management** — principles for transforming management.
3. **PDCA Cycle** — Plan-Do-Check-Act for continual improvement.
4. **Deming Chain Reaction** — Quality leads to lower cost, higher productivity, and market share.
5. **7 Deadly Diseases** — including lack of constancy of purpose, emphasis on short-term profits, etc.
6. **Deming Prize** — established in Japan (1951) to recognize quality excellence.

**Q. (b)** Discuss the Parasuraman Service Quality Gap Model with a diagram. Explain how TQM helps close the five gaps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Use the gap model and additive identity $Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$ from Section 3.1. TQM closes gaps through: market research (Gap 1), service design and QFD (Gap 2), training and empowerment (Gap 3), and integrated marketing communication (Gap 4).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Writing only definitions, no application/examples** — costs 2 marks in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Skipping diagrams in long answers** — a Mermaid-style block diagram in the answer booklet (drawn neatly) earns 1.5 to 2 extra marks.
> 3. **Conflating Quality Control with Quality Assurance** — the single most common error; always state the *reactive vs proactive* distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting named gurus in evolution-of-quality answers** — name Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi to score full marks.
> 5. **Not stating ISO definitions verbatim** — examiners expect the exact ISO 8402 wording for quality definition.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Definition of Quality** — ISO 8402 wording; 5 schools of thought.
- [ ] **Need for Quality** — 4 pressures: customer, competition, cost, compliance.
- [ ] **Evolution of Quality** — 6 eras from Inspection to Lean Six Sigma.
- [ ] **Quality Gurus** — Deming, Juran, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi.
- [ ] **Dimensions of Product Quality** — Garvin's 8 dimensions (Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality).
- [ ] **Dimensions of Service Quality** — SERVQUAL 5 dimensions (Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy).
- [ ] **Service Quality Gap Model** — 5 gaps with $Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$.
- [ ] **TQM Definition** — Customer satisfaction + Total involvement + Continual improvement.
- [ ] **TQM Basic Concepts** — Customer focus, employee involvement, continual improvement, process approach, integrated system, fact-based decisions, supplier partnership.
- [ ] **TQM Framework** — 5 pillars: Customer, Employee, Improvement, Process, Integrated System.
- [ ] **Deming's Contributions** — System of Profound Knowledge, 14 Points, PDCA, Chain Reaction, 7 Deadly Diseases, Deming Prize.
- [ ] **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — $P + A + IF + E$.
- [ ] **Six Sigma Metrics** — DPMO, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, RTY, FPY, Sigma level.
- [ ] **PDCA Cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act (iterative, Shewhart-Deming).
- [ ] **TQM vs Six Sigma vs Lean** — Philosophy vs Project vs System.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Juran and Crosby - Barriers to TQM - Quality statements - Customer focus - Customer orientation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Joseph M. Juran — The Quality Trilogy Architect

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Joseph M. Juran* (1904–2008) was a Romanian-American engineer who defined **Quality as "Fitness for Use"** and structured quality management into a universal **Three-Part Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) applicable to any industry.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Restaurant Analogy 🍽️):**
Imagine you open a restaurant. Juran's approach would be:
- **Quality Planning** = Designing the menu, choosing suppliers, setting recipes
- **Quality Control** = The head chef tasting each dish before it leaves the kitchen
- **Quality Improvement** = Listening to customer complaints and reformulating the recipe to remove the extra salt

Without all three, the restaurant will slowly lose customers. Juran formalized this idea for entire industries.

### Juran's Core Contributions at a Glance

| Concept | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Fitness for Use** | Product must satisfy customer needs throughout its life cycle |
| **Quality Trilogy** | Planning + Control + Improvement |
| **Pareto Principle (80/20)** | 80% of problems come from 20% of causes |
| **The Customer** | Both external (buyer) and internal (next process) |
| **Juran's Road Map** | Step-wise methodology for breakthrough improvement |

---

## 1.2 Philip B. Crosby — The Zero Defects Evangelist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Philip B. Crosby* (1924–2001) was an American quality pioneer who authored *"Quality is Free"* (1979). He defined **Quality as "Conformance to Requirements"** and championed the concept of **Zero Defects** and prevention-based cost management.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Surgeon Analogy 🏥):**
If a surgeon operates, the standard isn't "mostly correct" — it must be **right the first time, every time**. Crosby argued that manufacturing should adopt this same mindset. Defects are not inevitable; they are caused by lack of knowledge, lack of attention, or lack of process discipline. The "cost of poor quality" is what companies pay for *not* preventing them.

### Crosby's Famous One-Liner
> *"Quality is free. It's not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the un-quality things — all the actions that involve not doing jobs right the first time."*

---

## 1.3 Barriers to TQM (Total Quality Management)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Barriers to TQM* are the **organizational, cultural, structural, and managerial obstacles** that prevent successful implementation of Total Quality Management practices across an enterprise.

**Intuition:** Think of TQM as a marathon training program. Even the best plan fails if runners don't show up, shoes are wrong, the route is unclear, or no one is timing them. Barriers are the "broken shoes" of TQM.

**Six Major Barrier Categories (Synthesis of Feigenbaum, Oakland & KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus):**
1. **Strategic Barriers** — Lack of vision, mission, and quality policy
2. **Leadership Barriers** — Absence of top management commitment
3. **Cultural Barriers** — Employee resistance, fear of change, blame culture
4. **Structural Barriers** — Siloed departments, rigid hierarchy
5. **Operational Barriers** — Inadequate training, poor measurement, no data
6. **Customer Barriers** — Treating customer feedback as a complaint, not an asset

---

## 1.4 Quality Statements

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Quality Statements* are **formal documented declarations** of an organization's quality intent, direction, and commitment, encompassing the **Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, and Quality Objectives**.

**Intuition:** A quality statement is the "compass, map, and speedometer" of an organization. The vision tells you *where you want to be*, the mission tells you *why you exist*, the quality policy tells you *your quality beliefs*, and the quality objectives tell you *measurable checkpoints along the way*.

| Statement | Question it Answers | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| **Vision** | What do we aspire to become? | Long-term (10–20 yrs) |
| **Mission** | Why do we exist? | Medium-term (5–10 yrs) |
| **Quality Policy** | What are our quality beliefs? | Ongoing |
| **Quality Objectives** | What are our measurable targets? | Short-term (1 yr) |

---

## 1.5 Customer Focus vs. Customer Orientation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Focus (KTU Definition):** A *management philosophy* that places **understanding and meeting customer needs** at the centre of all business activities, both for external and internal customers.

> **Customer Orientation (KTU Definition):** A *cultural and behavioural mindset* where **every employee, in every function, aligns their work toward creating superior customer value**, treating customer satisfaction as the primary measure of organizational success.

**Analogy (The Hotel Concierge 🏨):**
- **Customer Focus** = The hotel has a 24/7 helpline and responds to guest complaints. *(Reactive + Proactive)*
- **Customer Orientation** = Every staff member — from the chef to the gardener — is trained to think: *"How does my action affect the guest's stay?"* The gardener doesn't just trim hedges; he trims them so guests can enjoy the view from their window. *(Deeply embedded culture)*

**The Subtle Distinction:**

$$
\text{Customer Focus} = \text{Strategy Layer (What we do for customers)}
$$

$$
\text{Customer Orientation} = \text{Culture Layer (How every employee thinks and acts)}
$$

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Juran's Quality Trilogy — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Quality Planning (The Design Phase)

Juran described this as **structured planning to meet quality goals**. The universal sequence (applicable to any product/service) is:

1. **Identify the customers** (internal + external)
2. **Determine customer needs** (use market research, Voice of Customer - VOC)
3. **Translate needs into product/service features** (design specifications)
4. **Establish quality goals** (measurable targets)
5. **Develop a process** capable of meeting the goals
6. **Prove process capability** (Pilot runs, FMEA, control plans)

> [!NOTE]
> Juran's planning is **data-driven, not assumption-driven**. *"The customer is the most important part of the production line."*

### 2.1.2 Quality Control (The Operational Phase)

The classical control loop, derived from feedback theory:

1. **Evaluate actual quality performance** (measure)
2. **Compare actual to quality goals** (gap analysis)
3. **Act on the difference** (corrective action)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Juran's control is **universal** — it applies to factories, hospitals, classrooms, and software firms equally. The three steps form a continuous cycle, often called the **Juran Control Loop** or **SPC (Statistical Process Control) Loop**.

### 2.1.3 Quality Improvement (The Breakthrough Phase)

Juran's **Breakthrough Sequence** (also called the *Juran Improvement Road Map*):

1. **Establish the infrastructure** — appoint a Quality Council, identify sponsors
2. **Identify the improvement projects** — use **Pareto Analysis** to find the vital few
3. **Establish project teams** — clear charters, sponsor ownership
4. **Provide resources, training, motivation** — empower teams
5. **Diagnose causes** — root cause analysis (cause-and-effect diagrams)
6. **Establish remedies** — counter-measures
7. **Establish controls to hold gains** — SOPs, measurement systems
8. **Prove capability at the new level** — validate, then standardize

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** in Juran's words: *"Vital few and trivial many"* — 20% of defects cause 80% of complaints. Focus improvement on the **vital few**.

### 2.1.4 The Juran Trilogy as an Integrated System

The three processes are not isolated. They form a **spiral** that moves an organization from stability to breakthrough, then back to a higher level of stability.

> **The Juran Spiral (Conceptual):**

$$
\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Improve} \rightarrow \text{Re-plan (at higher level)}
$$

This is essentially the **PDCA cycle (Deming)** viewed through Juran's lens.

---

## 2.2 Crosby's Quality Philosophy — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Four Absolutes of Quality

| # | Absolute | Practical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** | Not "goodness" — meeting agreed specifications exactly |
| 2 | **Prevention, not Inspection** | Build quality in; don't inspect it in at the end |
| 3 | **Zero Defects Performance Standard** | The target is zero errors, not "acceptable quality level" |
| 4 | **Measure Quality by Price of Non-Conformance** | Use **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** as the key metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) / Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** has four components:
> 1. **Internal Failure Costs** — scrap, rework, reinspection
> 2. **External Failure Costs** — warranty, returns, complaints
> 3. **Appraisal Costs** — inspection, testing, calibration
> 4. **Prevention Costs** — training, quality planning, FMEA
> 
> Crosby argued: increasing *prevention* dramatically reduces the other three. *"It is always cheaper to do the job right the first time."*

### 2.2.2 Crosby's Fourteen Steps to Quality Improvement

> [!NOTE]
> Crosby's 14 steps are an *implementation roadmap* — they were famously used at **Martin Marietta (1979)** and later adopted by **IBM, Xerox, and the US Navy**.

| Step | Step Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Management Commitment | Top management must publicly commit |
| 2 | Quality Improvement Team | Cross-functional QIT led by senior manager |
| 3 | Quality Measurement | Define what is non-conformance; count it |
| 4 | Cost of Quality Evaluation | Quantify the cost of poor quality |
| 5 | Quality Awareness | Communication campaign, posters, meetings |
| 6 | Corrective Action | Systematic root-cause elimination |
| 7 | Zero Defects Planning | Establish program for zero defects mindset |
| 8 | Supervisor Training | Train all first-line managers |
| 9 | Zero Defects Day | One-day event to launch the program |
| 10 | Goal Setting | Individual goals for error reduction |
| 11 | Error Cause Removal | Employees report obstacles to quality |
| 12 | Recognition | Publicly reward those who achieve goals |
| 13 | Quality Councils | Bring together QIT members to share learning |
| 14 | Do It Over Again | Continuous improvement — restart the cycle |

### 2.2.3 The DIRFT Principle

> **DIRFT = Do It Right The First Time**
> 
> Crosby's central operational philosophy. Every employee must understand requirements, have the tools and training, and be accountable — so the work is done correctly on the first attempt.

---

## 2.3 Juran vs. Crosby — KTU Comparison Table (High-Yield)

| Aspect | **Juran** | **Crosby** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition of Quality** | Fitness for Use | Conformance to Requirements |
| **Approach** | Systematic Trilogy | Behavioural / Cultural (14 steps) |
| **Customer View** | External + Internal customer | External customer primary |
| **Improvement Model** | Breakthrough (Pareto-based) | Incremental + Zero Defects |
| **Quality Measurement** | Goal-based metrics | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) |
| **Focus** | Process + Problem solving | People + Prevention + Culture |
| **Statistical Tools** | Emphasizes SPC, Pareto | Less statistical, more motivational |
| **Key Book** | *Juran's Quality Handbook* | *Quality is Free* |
| **Era of Influence** | 1950s–2000s | 1970s–2000s |
| **KTU Board Emphasis** | Trilogy + Road Map | Four Absolutes + 14 Steps |

---

## 2.4 Barriers to TQM — Comprehensive Framework

### 2.4.1 Strategic Barriers
- Absence of clear **vision, mission, quality policy**
- Short-term profit focus over long-term quality investment
- Lack of **quality objectives** aligned with business strategy

### 2.4.2 Leadership Barriers
- **Lack of top management commitment** (most cited barrier in literature)
- Treating TQM as a department program, not a *whole-organization* philosophy
- Failure to allocate budget, time, and authority to quality teams

### 2.4.3 Cultural Barriers
- **Employee resistance to change** — fear of job loss, new skills
- **Blame culture** — punishing mistakes instead of fixing systems
- Lack of trust between management and employees

### 2.4.4 Structural Barriers
- **Functional silos** — departments optimizing for themselves
- Rigid hierarchy that prevents bottom-up improvement ideas
- Centralized decision-making that delays action

### 2.4.5 Operational Barriers
- **Inadequate training** of employees in TQM tools (7 QC tools, FMEA, etc.)
- **Lack of measurement systems** — no data, no improvement
- Inconsistent processes and unclear standards

### 2.4.6 Customer-Related Barriers
- Treating customers as adversaries, not partners
- Ignoring **Voice of Customer (VOC)** data
- Failing to differentiate between **internal vs external customers**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overcoming Barriers (Synthesis Strategy):**
> 1. **Visible top-management commitment** with measurable quality goals
> 2. **Comprehensive training** at all levels
> 3. **Employee involvement** through Kaizen, suggestion systems, quality circles
> 4. **Robust measurement and feedback** systems
> 5. **Process orientation** (reduce silos, use cross-functional teams)
> 6. **Reward and recognition** aligned with quality behavior

---

## 2.5 Quality Statements — Detailed Framework

### 2.5.1 Vision Statement
- **Aspirational, future-oriented** declaration
- Defines what the organization wants to *become*
- Should be **inspiring, concise, memorable**
- Example: *"To be the world's most trusted technology partner."*

### 2.5.2 Mission Statement
- Defines **purpose, customers, products/services, values**
- Answer to: *What do we do? For whom? Why?*
- Example: *"We deliver innovative engineering solutions that empower our customers to succeed."*

### 2.5.3 Quality Policy
- **Formal statement of top management's** commitment to quality
- Aligned with ISO 9001 Clause 5.2 requirement
- Must be **communicated, understood, and applied** at all levels
- Example: *"We are committed to delivering defect-free products on time, every time."*

### 2.5.4 Quality Objectives
- **Specific, measurable, time-bound** targets
- Derived from the quality policy
- Cascaded to departments, teams, and individuals
- Example: *"Reduce customer complaints by 25% in FY 2024-25."*

### 2.5.5 SMART Framework for Quality Objectives

| Letter | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Specific | Reduce defects in welding |
| **M** | Measurable | From 4% to 1% defect rate |
| **A** | Achievable | Realistic with current resources |
| **R** | Relevant | Tied to customer satisfaction |
| **T** | Time-bound | Within 6 months |

---

## 2.6 Customer Focus & Customer Orientation — Theoretical Depth

### 2.6.1 Customer Types (Juran's Contribution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Customer:** Every person in the next process or department who receives your output. The receiver of a software developer's code is a tester. The receiver of an HR report is the CFO.
> 
> **External Customer:** The final user or buyer of the product/service.

| Aspect | Internal Customer | External Customer |
|---|---|---|
| Identification | Next process / department | End user, buyer, consumer |
| Voice of Customer (VOC) Methods | Process metrics, SLAs, internal surveys | Market research, reviews, complaints |
| Improvement Tools | Process mapping, handoff protocols | Quality Function Deployment (QFD) |
| Example | Machinist → Assembly worker | Buyer of a smartphone |

### 2.6.2 The Customer Value Chain (Oriented Thinking)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Customer orientation requires that *every function* answers: **"How does my work create or destroy customer value?"**
> 
> Functions like Finance, HR, and IT are *not* exempt. Even back-office work must trace its value back to the external customer.

### 2.6.3 Customer Focus vs. Customer Orientation — KTU Differentiation

| Dimension | **Customer Focus** | **Customer Orientation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Strategic philosophy | Cultural mindset |
| **Scope** | Organization-level | Individual-level (every employee) |
| **Time Orientation** | Forward-looking planning | Continuous, day-to-day behaviour |
| **Measurement** | Customer satisfaction surveys (CSAT), NPS | Behavioural observation, culture audits |
| **Key Question** | *What do customers need?* | *How does my work impact the customer?* |
| **Implementation** | CRM systems, VOC programs | Training, values, leadership example |
| **Originator (KTU)** | Juran's *Customer is King* | Levitt, Narver & Slater, Saxe & Weitz |

### 2.6.4 Key Customer Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Full Form | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **CSAT** | Customer Satisfaction Score | Post-interaction happiness (0–100) |
| **NPS** | Net Promoter Score | Loyalty (-100 to +100) |
| **CES** | Customer Effort Score | Ease of resolving an issue |
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Qualitative + quantitative feedback |
| **CTQ** | Critical to Quality | Customer-driven design specifications |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Worked Models

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are *conceptual frameworks*, not algebraic derivations. Each is presented as a stepwise analytical model that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Juran Trilogy as a Closed-Loop System

### Step 1: Define the Input
Let the **customer requirements (CR)** at time $t=0$ be the input to the system.

$$
CR(t_0) = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_n\}
$$

### Step 2: Quality Planning Transforms Input into Process Specification

The planning function translates customer requirements into a **process capability specification** $P_s$:

$$
P_s = f(CR, R, C, T)
$$

where $R$ = resources, $C$ = cost constraints, $T$ = technology available.

### Step 3: Quality Control Monitors Output vs. Goals

Let $Q_{actual}(t)$ be the measured quality at time $t$ and $Q_{goal}$ be the planned target.

$$
\Delta Q(t) = Q_{actual}(t) - Q_{goal}
$$

- If $\Delta Q(t) \leq \epsilon$ (within tolerance) → process is in control.
- If $\Delta Q(t) > \epsilon$ → corrective action is triggered.

### Step 4: Quality Improvement Adjusts the Goal Itself

Juran's insight is that **$Q_{goal}$ is not fixed**. After breakthrough improvement, the system establishes a *new* higher goal:

$$
Q_{goal}^{(n+1)} = Q_{goal}^{(n)} + \Delta Q_{improvement}
$$

### Step 5: The Recursive Spiral

The full system can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & CR_0 \xrightarrow{\text{Plan}} P_s \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & P_s \xrightarrow{\text{Execute}} Q_{actual}(t) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \Delta Q = Q_{actual}(t) - Q_{goal}^{(n)} \xrightarrow{\text{Control}} \text{Corrective Action} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & Q_{goal}^{(n+1)} = Q_{goal}^{(n)} + \delta_{breakthrough} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Loop back to Step 1 with } CR_0 \rightarrow CR_1
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is precisely the **Juran Spiral** — the recursive engine of continuous improvement.

---

## 3.2 Crosby's Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) — Analytical Model

### Step 1: Define the Four Cost Categories

Let the total cost of quality be:

$$
C_{total} = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)

Crosby defines PONC as the cost of *not* doing things right the first time:

$$
\text{PONC} = C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}
$$

### Step 3: The Crosby Investment Trade-off

Crosby observed that increasing prevention investment *exponentially* reduces PONC. The relationship is:

$$
\text{PONC}(p) = \frac{k}{p^n}
$$

where $p$ = prevention spending, $k$ = baseline failure cost, $n > 1$ (non-linear reduction).

### Step 4: Net Quality Cost Function

The **net cost of quality** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{net}(p) = \text{PONC}(p) + C_{prevention}(p) + C_{appraisal}(p)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Optimize by Taking the Derivative

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{net}}{dp} & = -\frac{nk}{p^{n+1}} + 1 = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad p^* & = (nk)^{1/(n+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Beyond a certain threshold of prevention spending ($p^*$), each additional rupee spent on prevention saves more than one rupee in PONC. This is the mathematical basis of Crosby's claim that *"Quality is free."*

---

## 3.3 Pareto-Based Improvement Selection (Juran Method)

### Step 1: Data Collection
Count defect types $D_i$ for $i = 1$ to $k$ over a fixed period.

### Step 2: Frequency Calculation
Compute frequency $f_i$ and cumulative percentage $C_i$:

$$
f_i = \frac{D_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} D_j}, \qquad C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j
$$

### Step 3: Rank and Plot
Sort $D_i$ in descending order. Plot a bar chart of $f_i$ and overlay a line graph of $C_i$.

### Step 4: Identify the Vital Few
The **vital few** are those defect categories where $C_i$ reaches **80%** with the smallest $i$ (typically the first 20% of categories).

### Step 5: Numerical Example (Worked)

Suppose a company tracks 5 defect types:

| Defect Type | Count ($D_i$) | $f_i$ | $C_i$ (Cumulative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Welding crack | 40 | 0.40 | 0.40 (40%) |
| Paint peel | 30 | 0.30 | 0.70 (70%) |
| Loose fastener | 20 | 0.20 | 0.90 (90%) |
| Label misprint | 8 | 0.08 | 0.98 (98%) |
| Color mismatch | 2 | 0.02 | 1.00 (100%) |
| **Total** | **100** | **1.00** | — |

> **Inference:** The **first 2 defect types** (Welding crack + Paint peel) form the **vital few** (cumulative 70%, close to 80%). Resources should be concentrated on these two.

---

## 3.4 Quality Statement Derivation — Hierarchical Logic Model

### Step 1: Vision Statement (Top)
Derived from **stakeholder aspirations, market trends, founder intent**.

### Step 2: Mission Statement (Operational)
Translates vision into **purpose, scope, and primary stakeholders**.

### Step 3: Quality Policy (Commitment)
Defines **quality beliefs and behavioural expectations** of the organization.

### Step 4: Quality Objectives (Measurable)
Cascaded targets at **corporate → department → team → individual** levels.

### Step 5: Verification Loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vision} \Rightarrow & \text{Mission} \Rightarrow \text{Quality Policy} \Rightarrow \text{Quality Objectives} \\
\text{Feedback:} \quad & \text{Achievement of Objectives} \xrightarrow{\text{informs}} \text{Refinement of Policy}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **cascade must maintain vertical alignment** — every objective must be traceable upward to a policy, and every policy must be traceable upward to the mission.

---

## 3.5 Customer Orientation Index (COI) — Conceptual Quantification

A simplified analytical model for measuring customer orientation:

### Step 1: Define Dimensions
- $D_1$ = Employee awareness of customer needs
- $D_2$ = Process alignment to customer journey
- $D_3$ = Cross-functional collaboration around customer
- $D_4$ = Use of VOC in decision-making
- $D_5$ = Reward/recognition tied to customer outcomes

### Step 2: Score Each Dimension
Each dimension is rated 1–5 (Likert scale).

### Step 3: Compute Weighted Index

$$
\text{COI} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot D_i, \quad \text{where } \sum w_i = 1
$$

### Step 4: Interpret

| COI Range | Customer Orientation Level |
|---|---|
| 1.0 – 2.0 | Weak (transactional) |
| 2.1 – 3.5 | Developing |
| 3.6 – 4.5 | Strong (embedded) |
| 4.6 – 5.0 | World-class (culture-driven) |

---

## 3.6 TQM Barrier Analysis — Stepwise Mitigation Model

For each barrier identified in Section 2.4, the mitigation follows a **4-step pattern**:

| Step | Action | KTU-Standard Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recognize** the barrier as systemic, not personal | *"Treat barriers as systems problems, not people problems."* |
| 2 | **Diagnose** root cause using 5-Why or Fishbone | *"Apply Ishikawa's 6M framework."* |
| 3 | **Design** countermeasure with employee involvement | *"Use cross-functional Kaizen teams."* |
| 4 | **Standardize** and hold the gain | *"Update SOPs, train, audit."* |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Juran's Quality Trilogy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    customerReq[Customer Requirements VOC] --> plan
    plan[QUALITY PLANNING]
    plan --> procSpec[Process and Product Specifications]
    procSpec --> control
    control[QUALITY CONTROL]
    control --> measure[Measure Actual Performance]
    measure --> compare[Compare Against Goals]
    compare --> gap{Gap Within Tolerance}
    gap -->|Yes| hold[Hold and Standardize]
    gap -->|No| correct[Corrective Action Loop]
    correct --> control
    control --> improve
    improve[QUALITY IMPROVEMENT]
    improve --> pareto[Pareto Identify Vital Few]
    pareto --> team[Project Teams Diagnose Causes]
    team --> remedy[Remedies and Control]
    remedy --> newGoal[Establish New Higher Goal]
    newGoal --> plan
    hold --> monitor[Monitor and Sustain]
    monitor --> improve
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The system is recursive — improvement resets planning at a higher quality level, forming the **Juran Spiral**.

```mermaid
    crosby --> c2[Four Absolutes and 14 Steps]
    crosby --> c3[Zero Defects and Behavioural Approach]
    crosby --> c4[Price of Non Conformance PONC]
    j1 --> common[Common Goal Customer Satisfaction and Continuous Improvement]
    j2 --> common
    j3 --> common
    j4 --> common
    c1 --> common
    c2 --> common
    c3 --> common
    c4 --> common
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) B.Tech End Semester Examination pattern** for PEEIT754. Marks distribution strictly follows the official KTU template.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model 2024]`

> **Q1.** Define Quality according to **J.M. Juran**. Explain the term *"Fitness for Use"* with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** According to Joseph M. Juran, *"Quality is Fitness for Use."* This means a product or service should be able to satisfy customer needs and expectations throughout its intended life cycle.
- **Explanation (1.5 marks):** "Fitness for Use" implies the product must be *usable, dependable, maintainable, and safe* under real-world conditions. It is not just about technical specifications, but about how well the product performs in the hands of the customer.
- **Example (0.5 mark):** A washing machine that washes clothes effectively, lasts 10+ years, consumes less electricity, and requires minimal repairs is "fit for use," even if its internals are technically complex.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / KTU Model 2024]`

> **Q2.** List any **six barriers to TQM implementation** in an organization.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 mark per barrier):**

1. **Lack of top management commitment** — TQM is treated as a department initiative.
2. **Employee resistance to change** — fear of new systems and job loss.
3. **Inadequate training** in TQM tools (7 QC tools, FMEA, SPC).
4. **Short-term profit orientation** over long-term quality investment.
5. **Functional silos** preventing cross-functional cooperation.
6. **Poor customer focus** — failure to capture and act on Voice of Customer.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q(a).** Explain in detail **Juran's Quality Trilogy** with a neat diagram. Discuss its application in an engineering manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q(b).** Explain the concept of **Customer Focus and Customer Orientation**. Distinguish between *internal* and *external* customers with examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — Juran's Quality Trilogy (7 Marks)

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Joseph M. Juran developed the **Quality Trilogy** in 1986, comprising three universal processes: **Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement**. The trilogy is applicable to any industry — manufacturing, services, healthcare, education.

2. **Quality Planning (2 marks):**
   - Identify *internal and external* customers.
   - Determine *customer needs* through VOC techniques.
   - Translate needs into *product features and specifications*.
   - Establish *quality goals* and *process capability*.
   - *Example:* An automobile company plans a new car by gathering feedback from current owners, defining fuel-efficiency and safety targets, and designing the engine accordingly.

3. **Quality Control (2 marks):**
   - *Evaluate* actual quality performance (SPC, control charts).
   - *Compare* actual with planned goals.
   - *Act* on deviations (corrective action).
   - *Example:* On the assembly line, every 10th vehicle undergoes a brake test. If results deviate from spec, the line is stopped and corrected.

4. **Quality Improvement (2 marks):**
   - *Establish infrastructure* (Quality Council, project sponsors).
   - *Identify improvement projects* using the **Pareto Principle** (vital few).
   - *Form project teams*, diagnose root causes, establish remedies, and *hold the gains*.
   - *Example:* Customer complaints about gear-shift noise are analysed, root cause traced to a faulty synchroniser ring, and a design change is implemented across all variants.

#### Model Answer (b) — Customer Focus & Customer Orientation (7 Marks)

1. **Customer Focus (2 marks):** A *strategic philosophy* centred on understanding and meeting customer needs. It is implemented through **CRM systems, market research, VOC programs, and customer satisfaction surveys (CSAT, NPS)**. The organization *proactively designs* its offerings to align with customer expectations.

2. **Customer Orientation (2 marks):** A *cultural and behavioural mindset* where every employee, regardless of role, internalises the customer-first principle. It is built through **training, values, leadership modelling, and reward systems**. Even back-office staff (HR, Finance) must see their work's connection to customer value.

3. **Internal vs External Customer (2 marks):**
   - *External customer:* The end user or buyer (e.g., the family buying a refrigerator).
   - *Internal customer:* The next process or department receiving your output (e.g., the assembly line worker is the internal customer of the parts supplier department; the marketing team is the internal customer of the R&D team's research report).

4. **Relationship Between the Two (1 mark):** Customer *focus* is the *strategic* layer (what the organization does); customer *orientation* is the *cultural* layer (how every employee thinks and acts). A firm can be customer-focused at the top but lack customer orientation at the front-line — leading to inconsistent service.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **Q(a).** Explain **Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality** and the **concept of Zero Defects**. How does the *Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)* support business decisions? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q(b).** Discuss the various **barriers to TQM implementation**. Suggest strategies to overcome them. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — Crosby's Absolutes & PONC (7 Marks)

1. **Quality as Conformance to Requirements (1.5 marks):** Quality is not "goodness" but the *degree to which a product meets its stated requirements*. Once requirements are agreed upon, anything less than 100% conformance is a defect.

2. **Prevention, not Inspection (1.5 marks):** Quality must be *designed in*, not inspected at the end. Crosby argued that *most defects are caused by inadequate processes*, not careless workers. Hence investment in process design, training, and error-proofing (*poka-yoke*) yields higher returns.

3. **Zero Defects Performance Standard (1.5 marks):** The target must be **zero defects**, not "acceptable quality level" (AQL). The standard of perfection *changes the mindset* of employees and pushes the organization towards defect-free operations.

4. **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (1.5 marks):** The *only* meaningful quality metric is the **cost of poor quality (PONC)**, comprising internal failure (scrap, rework) and external failure (warranty, returns). Reducing PONC frees up resources for productive investment.

5. **PONC in Business Decision-Making (1 mark):** By quantifying PONC, managers build a *business case* for quality investments. Every rupee spent on prevention reduces multiple rupees in failure costs — making quality *self-funding*. This is the essence of Crosby's *"Quality is Free."*

#### Model Answer (b) — Barriers to TQM & Overcoming Strategies (7 Marks)

| # | Barrier | Cause | Overcoming Strategy (KTU 2024 Answer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lack of top management commitment** | TQM seen as a department project | Visible CEO involvement, quality goals in strategic plan |
| 2 | **Employee resistance** | Fear of change, job loss | Involvement programs, open communication, training |
| 3 | **Inadequate training** | Tools like SPC, FMEA not taught | Comprehensive TQM training at all levels |
| 4 | **Short-term focus** | Quarterly financial pressure | Long-term quality metrics, balanced scorecard |
| 5 | **Functional silos** | Department KPIs in isolation | Cross-functional Kaizen teams, process thinking |
| 6 | **No measurement system** | Lack of data | Deploy SPC, dashboards, COPQ tracking |
| 7 | **Poor customer focus** | Customer seen as adversary | Voice of Customer programs, NPS tracking |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** Overcoming TQM barriers requires a *systemic* approach: visible leadership, people-centric culture, robust measurement, process orientation, and customer obsession. The absence of any one of these pillars causes the TQM edifice to collapse.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Juran / Crosby / TQM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Juran with Deming:** Juran = Trilogy + Pareto. Deming = PDCA + 14 Points + System of Profound Knowledge. Do NOT mix them.
> 2. **Writing Crosby's 14 steps without sequencing:** Examiners expect a *flow* — committing management first, then measurement, then training, then ZD Day, then recognition, then loop. Random lists lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting "Internal Customer":** Juran was the first to coin this concept. Skipping it loses a guaranteed 1–2 mark point.
> 4. **Customer Focus vs. Customer Orientation — writing them as synonyms:** Examiners specifically look for the *strategic vs. cultural* distinction.
> 5. **Pareto without numbers:** Always show a *worked numerical example* with cumulative percentage. Abstract definitions lose marks.
> 6. **Barriers listed without mitigation:** KTU 2024 expects *barrier → cause → countermeasure* triplets. Listing only barriers costs 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Quality Statements without SMART:** Any mention of objectives without the SMART framework is considered incomplete.
> 8. **PONC formula missing components:** Always state all four cost categories (Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔹 Juran
- [x] Quality = **Fitness for Use**
- [x] Trilogy = **Planning + Control + Improvement**
- [x] **Pareto Principle (80/20)** — vital few vs. trivial many
- [x] **Breakthrough Sequence** (8 steps) for improvement
- [x] Introduced the concept of **Internal Customer**
- [x] Emphasized **statistical tools** (SPC, control charts)
- [x] The **Juran Spiral** — recursive improvement model

### 🔹 Crosby
- [x] Quality = **Conformance to Requirements**
- [x] **Four Absolutes** — Conformance, Prevention, Zero Defects, PONC
- [x] **14 Steps** to Quality Improvement (foundation → launch → execution → loop)
- [x] **Zero Defects** is a *standard*, not a slogan
- [x] **PONC** (Price of Non-Conformance) = Internal + External Failure Costs
- [x] **DIRFT** = Do It Right The First Time
- [x] Authored *"Quality is Free"*
- [x] Behavioural / motivational approach (not statistical)

### 🔹 Barriers to TQM
- [x] Six categories: **Strategic, Leadership, Cultural, Structural, Operational, Customer**
- [x] **Top management commitment** is the most-cited barrier
- [x] Mitigation pattern: *Recognize → Diagnose → Design → Standardize*
- [x] **Employee resistance** handled through involvement and training

### 🔹 Quality Statements
- [x] Hierarchy: **Vision → Mission → Quality Policy → Quality Objectives**
- [x] Quality Objectives must be **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
- [x] Aligned with **ISO 9001 Clause 5.2** (Quality Policy)
- [x] Must be **communicated, understood, and applied** at all levels

### 🔹 Customer Focus vs. Customer Orientation
- [x] **Customer Focus** = *Strategic layer* (what the organization does for customers)
- [x] **Customer Orientation** = *Cultural layer* (how every employee thinks and acts)
- [x] **Internal Customer** = next process / department
- [x] **External Customer** = end user / buyer
- [x] Key metrics: **CSAT, NPS, CES, VOC, CTQ**
- [x] Every function (including back-office) must trace value to the customer

### 🔹 KTU Exam Quick-Recall Phrases
- *"Juran → Fitness for Use → Trilogy → Pareto"*
- *"Crosby → Conformance → Four Absolutes → 14 Steps → Zero Defects → PONC"*
- *"Customer Focus is strategy; Customer Orientation is culture"*
- *"Vision = where, Mission = why, Policy = belief, Objectives = SMART targets"*
