topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Customer satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Satisfaction** is a strategic performance metric in Total Quality Management (TQM) that quantifies the degree to which a product or service delivered by an organization meets or exceeds the **expectations, needs, and requirements** of its customers. It represents the customer's post-purchase emotional and rational evaluation of the value received relative to the value expected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Adopt verbatim for 2-mark questions):**
> *"Customer Satisfaction is the perception of the customer regarding the extent to which a supplier's products or services have fulfilled his/her needs, expectations, and requirements, as measured against competing alternatives and prior experience."*

In the **TQM philosophy**, customer satisfaction is treated as the **ultimate outcome variable** of every quality initiative. It is the dependent variable against which all process improvements, defect reductions, and quality investments are ultimately justified. KTU emphasizes two foundational principles by quality gurus:

- **Juran's Quality Trinity**: Customer satisfaction is the link between the *Quality Planning* → *Quality Control* → *Quality Improvement* cycle.
- **Deming's Chain Reaction**: *Improve quality → Decrease costs → Improve productivity → Capture market → Stay in business → Create jobs*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you walk into a **restaurant** on a Friday evening. Before arriving, you have certain **expectations** — a clean table, warm food, polite service, and a fair bill. These expectations are shaped by reviews, price, brand, and past visits.

Now consider what actually happens:
- If the food is **better** than expected → You are **delighted** (high satisfaction).
- If the food **matches** expectations → You are **satisfied** (adequate satisfaction).
- If the food is **worse** than expected → You are **dissatisfied** (low satisfaction / complaint).

**Customer Satisfaction = Perceived Performance − Expected Performance**

This is the same logic Deming, Juran, and Ishikawa used in Japanese manufacturing — but applied universally to every transaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:**
> Satisfaction is **not the same as quality**. Quality is a *producer-side* concept (conformance to specifications). Satisfaction is a *customer-side* perception (fulfillment of expectations). The two are linked but distinct.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Measures (Bold Constants)

The three globally standardized customer satisfaction metrics are:

| Metric | Full Form | Unit | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CSAT** | Customer Satisfaction Score | **Percentage (%)** | **1 to 5** (or 1–7) |
| **NPS** | Net Promoter Score | **Index (–100 to +100)** | **0 to 10** |
| **CES** | Customer Effort Score | **Score (1–7)** | **1 = Very Hard, 7 = Very Easy** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> Dr. Claes Fornell's **American Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI)** is the **gold standard** national-level satisfaction benchmark and is frequently asked in KTU Module 1 questions. ACSI ranges from **0 to 100**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Levels of Customer Satisfaction (Juran's Framework)

According to **Joseph M. Juran**, customer satisfaction exists at three escalating levels:

1. **Basic Satisfaction (Threshold):** The product/service must at least *meet* expectations to be acceptable. Failure at this level results in customer defection.
2. **Performance Satisfaction:** The product/service *exceeds* baseline expectations and produces a positive emotional response.
3. **Excitement Satisfaction (Delight):** The product/service *dramatically surpasses* expectations, creating customer loyalty, advocacy, and word-of-mouth promotion (Kano Model "Wow" features).

---

## 1.5 Determinants of Customer Satisfaction — The "Expectation Disconfirmation" Model

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Oliver–Swan–Tormala Expectation-Disconfirmation Model (1981)**, which is the most frequently asked framework in board examinations.

The five core determinants are:

1. **Customer Expectations** (driven by prior experience, word-of-mouth, brand reputation).
2. **Perceived Performance** (subjective evaluation of the actual product/service).
3. **Disconfirmation Gap** = Perceived Performance − Expectation.
4. **Affective Response** (emotional reaction — satisfaction or dissatisfaction).
5. **Behavioral Intention** (repurchase, loyalty, complaint, or switching).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:**
> Many students confuse *customer expectations* with *customer requirements*. They are different. **Requirements** are *what the customer says they want*; **Expectations** are *what the customer believes they will get*. Quality professionals must address both.

---

## 1.6 Importance of Customer Satisfaction in TQM (Why KTU Teaches This First)

In the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Total Quality Management curriculum**, customer satisfaction is positioned as **Module 1's foundational concept** because:

- It is the **primary output** of the TQM system.
- It is the **single most important competitive differentiator** in mature markets.
- It is the **leading indicator** of long-term profitability, customer lifetime value (CLV), and shareholder returns.
- The **Heskett–Sasser–Schlesinger Service-Profit Chain** mathematically links satisfaction → loyalty → profit.

---

## 1.7 Customer vs. Consumer vs. Client — Critical Distinction

KTU examiners frequently test this distinction for 2 marks:

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer** | The entity that *pays* for the product/service. | A retail shopper. |
| **Consumer** | The entity that *uses* the product/service. | A child eating the cereal. |
| **Client** | A long-term *trusted* customer under a service relationship. | A law firm's retainer. |

> [!WARNING]
> In B2B (Business-to-Business) contexts, the *customer* and *consumer* are often the **same entity** (e.g., a manufacturing firm buying industrial equipment). In B2C (Business-to-Consumer) contexts, they are often **different** (e.g., parents buying the cereal, child eating it).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Customer Satisfaction Formation Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a **structured analytical breakdown** of how satisfaction is *actually formed* in the customer's mind. We use the **Expectation-Disconfirmation Paradigm (EDP)** as the canonical model.

### Step 1 — Pre-Purchase Expectation Formation
Before purchase, the customer develops **expectations** based on:
- **Personal needs** (intrinsic motivation).
- **Past experience** with the same product/service.
- **External communications** (advertising, sales, word-of-mouth).
- **Competitive alternatives** considered.

$$\text{Expectation} = f(\text{Past Experience, WOM, Marketing, Alternatives})$$

### Step 2 — Purchase & Consumption
The customer acquires and uses the product/service. Actual performance is observed through the **lens of subjective perception**, not objective engineering specifications.

### Step 3 — Post-Consumption Evaluation
The customer compares the **perceived performance** (subjective) with the **prior expectation** (subjective). The gap is called the **Disconfirmation Gap**.

$$\Delta_{\text{disconfirmation}} = P_{\text{perceived}} - E_{\text{expectation}}$$

### Step 4 — Affective Response (Satisfaction Outcome)
- If $\Delta > 0$ → **Positive Disconfirmation** → Satisfaction / Delight.
- If $\Delta = 0$ → **Simple Confirmation** → Neutral satisfaction.
- If $\Delta < 0$ → **Negative Disconfirmation** → Dissatisfaction.

### Step 5 — Post-Satisfaction Behavioral Outcome
The affective response drives:
- **Loyalty** (positive disconfirmation).
- **Complaint** (negative disconfirmation).
- **Switching** (severe negative disconfirmation).
- **Word-of-Mouth Advocacy** (delight).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 6 formulas are sufficient to answer any numerical question on Customer Satisfaction in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Disconfirmation Gap** | $\Delta = P - E$ | Arbitrary (–∞ to +∞) | Core EDP model. |
| 2 | **CSAT Score** | $CSAT = \dfrac{N_{s}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | **% (0 to 100)** | % of customers rating ≥ 4 on 1–5 scale. |
| 3 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | **Index (–100 to +100)** | Detractors subtract from Promoters. |
| 4 | **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $CES = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}{n}$ | **Score (1 to 7)** | Lower = better; industry leader < 3. |
| 5 | **ACSI Weighting Formula** | $ACSI = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ | **Index (0 to 100)** | Weighted average of latent variables. |
| 6 | **Service-Profit Chain** | $\text{Profit} \propto L \propto S$ | Qualitative | Loyalty $(L)$ driven by Satisfaction $(S)$. |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table, we used $\dfrac{N_{s}}{N_{t}}$ instead of vertical pipes to keep the markdown table parser safe. KTU examiners accept either the `ds/d_t` ratio notation or the `\\vert`-based absolute value notation.

---

## 2.3 The Kano Model — Mapping Satisfaction to Features (Engineering Utility)

Professor **Noriaki Kano's** 1984 model classifies product/service features into **5 categories** based on their impact on customer satisfaction. This is **frequently asked for 7-mark sub-questions**.

| Feature Type | If Present | If Absent | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | No change in satisfaction | Severe dissatisfaction | Power button works on a phone. |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Higher satisfaction | Lower satisfaction | Battery life, speed. |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | High satisfaction (delight) | No dissatisfaction | Free gift in delivery. |
| **Indifferent** | No effect | No effect | Color of internal screws. |
| **Reverse** | Dissatisfaction | Satisfaction | Over-complicated menu. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):**
> Software companies use the Kano Model during **requirements elicitation** in Agile projects. Hardware firms use it during **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** — the "House of Quality" matrix maps customer *wants* to engineering *specs*. This is **directly assessed** in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 2.4 The Service-Profit Chain (Heskett, Sasser, Schlesinger, 1994)

This **causal chain** is the most powerful business logic for justifying TQM investment to senior management. KTU expects students to **draw the chain** in 7-mark answers.

$$\boxed{\text{Internal Quality} \rightarrow \text{Employee Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Employee Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Employee Productivity} \rightarrow \text{Service Value} \rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Customer Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Revenue Growth} \rightarrow \text{Profit Growth}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always start and end your Service-Profit Chain answer with the same variables: Internal Quality (input) and Profit Growth (output). The middle 7 links are mandatory.

---

## 2.5 Strategic Significance — Why Customer Satisfaction Drives Engineering Excellence

In modern engineering firms (e.g., **Siemens, Bosch, Tata, Infosys**), customer satisfaction drives:
- **Product Design Decisions** (DFMEA priorities are weighted by customer-perceived severity).
- **Tolerance Selection** (tighter tolerances for customer-visible features).
- **Supply Chain Choices** (supplier selection uses CSAT audits).
- **Lean Six Sigma Project Selection** (Black Belts select projects with the highest customer impact).

---

## 2.6 Customer Satisfaction vs. Service Quality vs. Product Quality

| Dimension | Customer Satisfaction | Service Quality | Product Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orientation** | Outcome-based | Process-based | Conformance-based |
| **Time Frame** | Post-purchase evaluation | During service delivery | Pre-shipment inspection |
| **Subject** | Customer perception | Customer perception + process metrics | Engineering specifications |
| **Measurement** | CSAT, NPS, CES | SERVQUAL (5 dimensions) | Cp, Cpk, defect rates |
| **Owner** | Marketing / CX team | Operations / Service team | Engineering / QA team |

> [!WARNING]
> **SERVQUAL** (Parasuraman–Zeithaml–Berry, 1988) has **5 dimensions**: *Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy*. KTU questions often require listing all five — incomplete answers lose **3 marks** out of 7.

---

## 2.7 Customer Loyalty as a *Consequence* of Satisfaction (Loyalty Cascade)

Customer satisfaction and customer loyalty are **correlated but not identical**. KTU examiners frequently test this nuance.

$$\text{Customer Loyalty} = f(\text{Satisfaction, Switching Cost, Trust, Habit})$$

- **Satisfied but not loyal:** Customer is open to better offers from competitors.
- **Loyal but not satisfied:** Customer stays due to lock-in, contracts, or lack of alternatives (false loyalty).
- **Loyal AND satisfied:** True advocacy and long-term value.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Expectation-Disconfirmation Satisfaction Function

We will derive the **canonical satisfaction function** used in academic TQM literature. This is the most mathematically rigorous answer to a 7-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $E$ = Customer's pre-purchase **expectation** (a scalar, typically 0 to 10).
- $P$ = Customer's **perceived performance** after consumption (same scale).
- $S$ = Customer **satisfaction** (output, same scale).
- $\alpha$ = **Sensitivity coefficient** (how strongly the customer reacts to disconfirmation).
- $\beta$ = **Baseline satisfaction** floor (satisfaction at zero disconfirmation).

### Step 2 — Form the Disconfirmation Gap

The **disconfirmation** is defined as the algebraic difference between perceived performance and expectation.

$$\Delta = P - E$$

### Step 3 — Construct the Linear Satisfaction Function

We assume a linear relationship between satisfaction and disconfirmation, anchored by a baseline floor.

$$S = \beta + \alpha \cdot \Delta$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Disconfirmation Definition

$$S = \beta + \alpha \cdot (P - E)$$

### Step 5 — Expand and Rearrange

$$\boxed{S = \beta + \alpha P - \alpha E}$$

This is the **fundamental EDP equation**. Interpretation:

- If $\alpha > 0$ (typical customer), higher $P$ increases $S$ and higher $E$ decreases $S$.
- If $\alpha < 0$ (contrarian customer), the relationship inverts.
- $\beta$ represents **inherent baseline satisfaction** (e.g., brand trust).

### Step 6 — Three Special Cases (Board Exam Favourite)

**Case A — Simple Confirmation** ($P = E$):

$$S = \beta + \alpha(0) = \beta$$

So satisfaction equals the baseline only when performance matches expectations.

**Case B — Positive Disconfirmation (Delight)** ($P = E + k$):

$$S = \beta + \alpha k \quad \text{where } k > 0$$

**Case C — Negative Disconfirmation (Dissatisfaction)** ($P = E - k$):

$$S = \beta - \alpha k \quad \text{where } k > 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:**
> In practice, $\alpha$ is often **asymmetric**. Customers react *more strongly* to negative disconfirmation than to positive disconfirmation. This is called the **negativity bias** ($-\alpha_{\text{neg}} > +\alpha_{\text{pos}}$). Mentioning this in a 14-mark answer earns **2 extra marks** (current KTU scheme).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — CSAT Score Calculation

A retail outlet collected 250 customer responses on a 1-to-5 satisfaction scale.

| Rating | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Count** | 10 | 20 | 50 | 100 | 70 |

**Problem:** Calculate the CSAT score if only ratings of 4 and 5 are considered "satisfied."

### Step 1 — Identify the Satisfied Customers

Ratings of 4 and 5 qualify.

$$N_{s} = 100 + 70 = 170$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Total Respondents

$$N_{t} = 10 + 20 + 50 + 100 + 70 = 250$$

### Step 3 — Apply the CSAT Formula

$$CSAT = \frac{N_{s}}{N_{t}} \times 100$$

$$CSAT = \frac{170}{250} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{CSAT = 68\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 68% of customers are "satisfied" (ratings 4 or 5). A CSAT of **≥ 80%** is considered world-class. Below **60%** indicates a serious quality problem.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Net Promoter Score (NPS) Calculation

A SaaS company surveyed 1,000 customers on the 0-to-10 scale.

| Category | Score Range | Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Promoters** | 9–10 | 520 |
| **Passives** | 7–8 | 300 |
| **Detractors** | 0–6 | 180 |

### Step 1 — Verify Total

$$520 + 300 + 180 = 1,000 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Calculate % Promoters

$$\%P = \frac{520}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 52\%$$

### Step 3 — Calculate % Detractors

$$\%D = \frac{180}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 18\%$$

### Step 4 — Apply the NPS Formula

$$NPS = \%P - \%D$$

$$NPS = 52 - 18$$

$$\boxed{NPS = 34}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation (Fred Reichheld, 2003):**
> - NPS > **50** → World-class (Apple, Tesla, USAA).
> - NPS between **0 and 50** → Good.
> - NPS < **0** → Critical problem; majority are detractors.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Customer Effort Score (CES) Mean

A telecom company received 400 service complaints. Customers rated effort on a 1-to-7 scale (1 = very hard, 7 = very easy). The frequency distribution is:

| Rating | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Count** | 30 | 50 | 80 | 90 | 70 | 50 | 30 |

### Step 1 — Compute the Weighted Sum

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7} R_i \cdot f_i = (1)(30) + (2)(50) + (3)(80) + (4)(90) + (5)(70) + (6)(50) + (7)(30)$$

$$= 30 + 100 + 240 + 360 + 350 + 300 + 210$$

$$= 1{,}590$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Responses

$$n = 30 + 50 + 80 + 90 + 70 + 50 + 30 = 400$$

### Step 3 — Apply the CES Formula

$$CES = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{7} R_i \cdot f_i}{n}$$

$$CES = \frac{1{,}590}{400}$$

$$\boxed{CES = 3.975 \approx 3.98}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A CES of **3.98** indicates customers found the service "moderately difficult." Industry best practice (per CEB/Gartner) is CES ≤ **3.0**. High CES is the **#1 predictor of churn** in service industries.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — ACSI Weighted Index Calculation

The American Customer Satisfaction Index uses three latent variables with weights:

| Latent Variable | Weight $w_i$ | Sub-score $s_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality (Q)** | 0.40 | 82 |
| **Value (V)** | 0.30 | 75 |
| **Expectation (E)** | 0.30 | 88 |

### Step 1 — State the ACSI Formula

$$ACSI = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot s_i$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$ACSI = (0.40)(82) + (0.30)(75) + (0.30)(88)$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Term

$$\text{Quality Contribution} = 32.80$$
$$\text{Value Contribution} = 22.50$$
$$\text{Expectation Contribution} = 26.40$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Contributions

$$ACSI = 32.80 + 22.50 + 26.40$$

$$\boxed{ACSI = 81.70}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An ACSI of 81.70 is above the U.S. national average (which fluctuates around 76–78). This indicates the company outperforms the average in customer perception.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Satisfaction Models Compared

| Model | Author(s) | Year | Core Logic | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Expectation-Disconfirmation** | Oliver | 1980 | $S = f(P - E)$ | Most frequently asked. |
| **Normative Comparison** | Festinger | 1954 | Compare with *other* customers. | Mentioned in 14-mark answers. |
| **Equity Theory** | Adams | 1965 | Fairness of input/output ratio. | Service recovery questions. |
| **Attribution Theory** | Weiner | 1985 | Cause of satisfaction (locus, stability). | Service failure analysis. |
| **Kano Model** | Kano | 1984 | Non-linear feature-satisfaction mapping. | Product design questions. |
| **SERVQUAL** | Parasuraman et al. | 1988 | Gap between expectation & perception of 5 service dimensions. | Service quality questions. |
| **ACSI** | Fornell | 1994 | Weighted latent variable index. | National-level benchmarking. |
| **Service-Profit Chain** | Heskett et al. | 1994 | Causal chain from internal quality to profit. | Strategic management questions. |

---

## 3.7 The Five Customer Satisfaction Gaps (SERVQUAL Gap Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gap analysis is the most tested 14-mark topic in KTU Module 1.** The 5 gaps are:

1. **Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap:** Customer expectation vs. Management perception of expectation.
2. **Gap 2 — Standards Gap:** Management perception vs. Service quality specifications.
3. **Gap 3 — Delivery Gap:** Service specifications vs. Actual service delivered.
4. **Gap 4 — Communication Gap:** Actual delivery vs. External communications.
5. **Gap 5 — Perceived Service Gap:** Expected service vs. Perceived service (the *final* satisfaction gap).

The **mathematical closure** of all 5 gaps:

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define Customer Satisfaction. List any TWO standard metrics used to measure it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Customer Satisfaction is the customer's post-purchase perception of the extent to which a product or service has met or exceeded their expectations, needs, and requirements. It reflects the gap between perceived performance and prior expectation.

**Two Metrics (1 mark):**
1. **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score)** — measured as a percentage of customers rating 4 or 5 on a 1–5 scale.
2. **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** — calculated as (% Promoters – % Detractors) on a 0–10 scale.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often define "quality" instead of "satisfaction." Quality is a producer-side concept; satisfaction is a customer-side perception. Definition must reference *customer perception of value received vs. value expected*.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Distinguish between Customer Expectation and Customer Requirement with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Customer Requirement | Customer Expectation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | What the customer explicitly states they need. | What the customer *believes* they will receive. |
| **Origin** | Expressed needs (stated). | Formed from experience, WOM, brand, alternatives. |
| **Nature** | Objective and measurable. | Subjective and perceptual. |
| **Example** | "I need a phone with a 5000 mAh battery." | "I expect this phone to last a full day." (1 mark each) |

The two are linked: *requirements* feed into the *specifications* the firm designs, while *expectations* determine the *satisfaction* outcome after use. (1 mark for the conclusion.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions for Module 1 carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two sub-options. Each sub-question is typically **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the Expectation-Disconfirmation Paradigm (EDP) of customer satisfaction. Discuss its three possible outcomes with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Introduction (2 marks):**
The Expectation-Disconfirmation Paradigm (EDP) was formalized by **Richard L. Oliver (1980)** and is the most widely accepted model in TQM literature. It states that customer satisfaction is a function of the **disconfirmation gap** between *perceived performance* and *pre-purchase expectation*.

Mathematically:

$$S = f(P - E)$$

where $P$ = perceived performance, $E$ = pre-purchase expectation, and $S$ = resulting satisfaction.

**Step 2 — Three Outcomes (3 marks):**

| Outcome | Condition | Customer Reaction | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Disconfirmation** | $P > E$ | Delight / High satisfaction | A budget hotel offering a free upgrade. |
| **Simple Confirmation** | $P = E$ | Neutral satisfaction | A ₹50 coffee tasting exactly as expected. |
| **Negative Disconfirmation** | $P < E$ | Dissatisfaction / Complaint | A flight arriving 3 hours late without compensation. |

**Step 3 — Application in TQM (2 marks):**
TQM practices such as **Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, and QFD** aim to *consistently* deliver $P \geq E$ to avoid negative disconfirmation. Strategic delight comes from *occasionally* achieving $P >> E$ (Kano's "Attractive Features").

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the Service-Profit Chain. Why is internal service quality the starting point of this chain?**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — The Chain (5 marks):**

The Service-Profit Chain (Heskett, Sasser, Schlesinger, 1994) establishes a direct, quantifiable link between the quality of internal operations and external financial outcomes:

$$\boxed{\text{Internal Quality} \rightarrow \text{Employee Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Employee Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Employee Productivity} \rightarrow \text{Service Value} \rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Customer Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Revenue Growth} \rightarrow \text{Profit Growth}}$$

*(Award 0.5 marks per link if drawn correctly. 5 marks for all 9 links.)*

**Step 2 — Why Internal Quality is the Starting Point (2 marks):**
1. **Upstream causality:** External customer satisfaction is *impossible* without competent, motivated, and loyal employees. A dissatisfied employee cannot deliver a satisfied customer.
2. **Self-reinforcing loop:** High internal quality → satisfied employees → high productivity → better service value → satisfied customers → revenue growth → more resources for internal quality.
3. **TQM alignment:** This matches Deming's principle that *"quality begins with the people who make the product."*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the Kano Model of customer satisfaction. List its FIVE categories of features with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (2 marks):**
The **Kano Model**, developed by Professor **Noriaki Kano** (Tokyo Rika University, 1984), classifies product/service features into categories based on their *non-linear* ability to create or destroy customer satisfaction. Unlike the EDP model, Kano acknowledges that **not all features contribute equally** to satisfaction.

**Step 2 — Five Categories (5 marks — 1 mark per category with example):**

| Category | If Present | If Absent | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Must-Be (Basic)** | No extra satisfaction | Severe dissatisfaction | Airbags in a car. |
| **2. One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Higher satisfaction | Lower satisfaction | Fuel efficiency of the car. |
| **3. Attractive (Excitement)** | High delight | No dissatisfaction | Heated seats in a budget car. |
| **4. Indifferent** | No effect | No effect | Logo placement on rear door. |
| **5. Reverse** | Dissatisfaction | Higher satisfaction | Overly complex car infotainment. |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: What is the SERVQUAL model? Explain its FIVE dimensions and the FIVE customer gaps.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — SERVQUAL Introduction (1 mark):**
**SERVQUAL** (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry, 1988) is a 22-item instrument for measuring **service quality** as the gap between customer *expectations* and *perceptions*. It identifies **5 dimensions** and **5 gaps**.

**Step 2 — Five Dimensions (3 marks — 0.6 per dimension):**

1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers.

**Step 3 — Five Gaps (3 marks — 0.6 per gap):**

1. **Gap 1:** Customer expectations vs. Management perception of those expectations.
2. **Gap 2:** Management perception vs. Service quality specifications.
3. **Gap 3:** Service specifications vs. Actual service delivery.
4. **Gap 4:** Actual delivery vs. External communications.
5. **Gap 5:** Expected service vs. Perceived service (the final satisfaction gap).

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting Gap 4** is the most common reason students lose 1.5 marks. Gap 4 is the *communication* gap (e.g., advertising promising more than what is delivered).
> 2. **Conflating the dimensions with the gaps** results in a 0 for that part. Dimensions are *attributes of service*; gaps are *discrepancies* between expectation and delivery.

---

## KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout (Module 1)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Customer Satisfaction Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Quality with Satisfaction:** Quality is conformance to specs (producer view). Satisfaction is perception vs. expectation (customer view). KTU explicitly tests this distinction.
> 2. **Missing a Kano Category:** Forgetting the *Indifferent* or *Reverse* categories in the Kano model costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting Negativity Bias in EDP:** Failing to mention that $\alpha$ is asymmetric (negative disconfirmation has a larger effect than positive) loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Mathematical Closure in SERVQUAL:** Not writing $Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Incomplete Service-Profit Chain:** Missing any of the 9 links results in proportional deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Revise Before Exam**

- [x] **Customer Satisfaction** = $f(P - E)$ (Perceived Performance – Expectation).
- [x] Three outcomes: **Positive (Delight), Simple (Neutral), Negative (Dissatisfaction)**.
- [x] **CSAT** = $(N_s / N_t) \times 100$ (in %).
- [x] **NPS** = $\%P - \%D$ (–100 to +100); > 50 = World-class.
- [x] **CES** = $\sum(R_i \cdot f_i) / n$ (1 to 7); < 3 = Best-in-class.
- [x] **ACSI** (Fornell, 1994) is the national benchmark (0–100).
- [x] **EDP** by Oliver (1980) is the foundational satisfaction model.
- [x] **Kano Model** has 5 categories: Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, Reverse.
- [x] **SERVQUAL** has **5 dimensions**: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- [x] **SERVQUAL** has **5 gaps**; $Gap_5$ is the final satisfaction gap.
- [x] **Service-Profit Chain** has **9 links**, starting with Internal Quality and ending with Profit Growth.
- [x] **Negativity Bias:** $\alpha_{\text{negative}} > \alpha_{\text{positive}}$ (customers remember bad experiences more).
- [x] **Customer ≠ Consumer ≠ Client**: customer pays, consumer uses, client has long-term relationship.
- [x] **Three levels of satisfaction** (Juran): Basic → Performance → Excitement/Delight.
- [x] **Quality and satisfaction are linked but distinct**: Quality is producer-side; Satisfaction is customer-side.
- [x] **CSAT > 80%** = World-class; **NPS > 50** = World-class; **CES < 3** = World-class.
- [x] **True loyalty** requires both satisfaction *and* trust; lock-in loyalty is *false loyalty*.
- [x] KTU Module 1 = 15–20% of total marks; customer satisfaction is the **most asked** topic.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEEIT754 MODULE 1: CUSTOMER SATISFACTION**

topics.title: Customer complaints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Customer complaints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Customer Complaint** is a formal expression of dissatisfaction or grievance raised by a customer against a product, service, or process, where the customer explicitly communicates the gap between their *expected quality* and the *perceived quality* actually delivered. In the Total Quality Management (TQM) framework, customer complaints are treated as **critical quality feedback data points** that drive continuous improvement (Kaizen), corrective action, and preventive action (CAPA).

Mathematically, a complaint represents a gap that can be expressed through the classic TQM quality equation:

$$Q_{complaint} = f(E - P)$$

Where:
- $Q_{complaint}$ = Magnitude/Severity of the customer complaint
- $E$ = Customer Expectation (the standard the customer anticipated)
- $P$ = Perceived Performance (the actual quality received)
- $f$ = Function defining the customer's reaction curve

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> Whenever $(E - P) > 0$, a quality gap exists. If this gap crosses the customer's **Zone of Tolerance**, the dissatisfaction is voiced as a complaint. This is the foundation of the **SERVQUAL Gap Model** by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Scenario**
> Imagine you walk into a premium restaurant expecting a 5-star dining experience (Expectation $E$). You ordered a *Butter Chicken* but received a cold, under-spiced, late-delivered dish (Performance $P$). You immediately call the waiter and complain. This complaint is **golden feedback** — the restaurant can use it to:
> - Retrain the kitchen staff (Corrective Action)
> - Modify the spice recipe (Preventive Action)
> - Offer you a free dessert to recover trust (Service Recovery)
> - Update the menu to reflect accurate preparation time (Continuous Improvement)

In TQM philosophy, **every complaint is a free consulting report** given by the customer. The company that ignores it pays later in the form of customer defection; the company that embraces it gains a competitive edge.

### 1.3 Importance of Customer Complaints in TQM

| Strategic Role | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Voice of Customer (VoC) | Direct, unfiltered market feedback |
| Defect Detection | Reveals hidden process failures |
| Cost of Quality (CoQ) | Source of *external failure cost* data |
| Loyalty Driver | Properly resolved complaints build trust |
| Innovation Trigger | Highlights unmet needs and gaps |
| Benchmarking Data | Compares competitor offerings implicitly |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Customer Complaints

TQM literature (Juran, Deming, Ishikawa) classifies complaints along three primary dimensions:

**A. Based on Nature of the Problem**
- **Product Complaints** — Defects, breakage, wrong specifications, packaging failures
- **Service Complaints** — Late delivery, rude staff, billing errors, poor after-sales support
- **Process Complaints** — Long waiting time, complex procedures, repeated form submissions

**B. Based on Severity Level**
- **Critical Complaints** — Safety hazards, legal violations, financial loss (Priority 1)
- **Major Complaints** — Functional failure of product/service (Priority 2)
- **Minor Complaints** — Cosmetic issues, minor inconvenience (Priority 3)

**C. Based on Source of Dissatisfaction**
- **Attitudinal Complaints** — About behavior, communication, empathy
- **Technical Complaints** — About engineering, design, specifications
- **Transactional Complaints** — About pricing, billing, refund, return policies

### 2.2 The Five Gap Model of Service Quality (SERVQUAL)

This is the **single most important framework** for KTU exams on this topic. Proposed by Parasuraman et al. (1985, 1988):

- **Gap 1**: Management Perception Gap — What management thinks customers want vs. what customers actually want
- **Gap 2**: Service Quality Specification Gap — Management perception vs. service standards set
- **Gap 3**: Service Performance Gap — Service standards vs. actual service delivered
- **Gap 4**: Communication Gap — External communication vs. actual service delivered
- **Gap 5**: Expected Service vs. Perceived Service Gap — The **ultimate complaint-generating gap**

$$Gap_5 = f(Gap_1, Gap_2, Gap_3, Gap_4)$$

### 2.3 Customer Complaint Handling Process (8-Stage TQM Model)

1. **Receipt of Complaint** — Log via CRM, email, hotline, social media
2. **Acknowledgement** — Immediate response within 24 hours (SLA standard)
3. **Classification** — Categorize by type, severity, department affected
4. **Investigation** — Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys, Fishbone/Ishikawa diagram)
5. **Resolution** — Corrective action, refund, replacement, apology
6. **Communication** — Inform customer of resolution timeline and outcome
7. **Verification** — Follow-up call/email to confirm satisfaction
8. **Documentation & Learning** — Feed into FMEA, CAPA, and knowledge base

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complaint Rate | $CR = \dfrac{N_{c}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | Frequency of complaints per total transactions | Percentage (%) |
| Customer Effort Score (CES) | $CES = 1 + \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}{n \times 7} \times 6$ | Effort needed to resolve complaint | Scale 1-7 |
| Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | Loyalty after complaint handling | -100 to +100 |
| First Contact Resolution | $FCR = \dfrac{N_{fcr}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of complaint handling | Percentage (%) |
| Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) | $CSAT = \dfrac{\sum S_i}{n \times S_{max}} \times 100$ | Post-resolution satisfaction | Percentage (%) |
| Service Recovery Paradox | $SRP_{index} = \dfrac{L_{after}}{L_{before}}$ | Loyalty boost after recovery | Ratio > 1 |
| Complaint Aging Index | $CAI = \dfrac{D_{actual} - D_{target}}{D_{target}}$ | Delay in complaint closure | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Exam Tip**
> In KTU valuation, examiners reward students who **define variables before substituting**. Always write: *"Let $N_c$ be the number of complaints and $N_t$ be the total number of transactions."*

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing and IT industries, complaint data feeds directly into:
- **Six Sigma DMAIC** projects (Define phase identifies complaints)
- **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule identifies top 20% complaints causing 80% dissatisfaction)
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** to prevent recurrence
- **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 10.2** — Nonconformity and Corrective Action requirements

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Aggregate Service Quality Gap (Gap 5)

The SERVQUAL Gap 5 is mathematically derived as the cumulative effect of Gaps 1 through 4.

**Step 1**: Define each individual gap as the difference between two perceptual states.

$$\begin{aligned}
Gap_1 &= E_c - P_m \\
Gap_2 &= P_m - S_s \\
Gap_3 &= S_s - D_s \\
Gap_4 &= D_s - E_c
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $E_c$ = Expected service (customer expectation)
- $P_m$ = Perceived service expectation (management's perception)
- $S_s$ = Service specification (standard set by management)
- $D_s$ = Delivered service (actual performance)
- $E_c$ (in Gap 4) = External communication promise

**Step 2**: Recognize that Gap 5 (the complaint-generating gap) is:

$$Gap_5 = E_c - D_s$$

**Step 3**: Add and subtract the intermediate variables to express Gap 5 in terms of Gaps 1, 2, 3:

$$\begin{aligned}
Gap_5 &= E_c - D_s \\
      &= (E_c - P_m) + (P_m - S_s) + (S_s - D_s) \\
      &= Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Incorporate Gap 4 (the communication gap) for the complete model:

$$\begin{aligned}
Gap_5 &= Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4 \\
      &= (E_c - P_m) + (P_m - S_s) + (S_s - D_s) + (D_s - C_p)
\end{aligned}$$

Where $C_p$ is the externally communicated promise. This shows that **every complaint is traceable to a measurable upstream gap**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Complaint Resolution Cost vs. Defection Cost

A common KTU 14-mark question asks students to justify the investment in complaint management. Let us derive the break-even analysis.

**Step 1**: Define the variables
- $C_r$ = Cost of resolving one complaint (refund, replacement, agent time, shipping)
- $C_d$ = Cost of losing one customer (lost lifetime value $LTV$ minus any salvaged revenue)
- $N$ = Number of complaints per year
- $R$ = Resolution effectiveness ratio (fraction of complaints retained)

**Step 2**: Write the total cost equation for two scenarios

$$\begin{aligned}
Cost_{ignore} &= N \times (1 - R_0) \times C_d \\
Cost_{resolve} &= N \times C_r + N \times (1 - R) \times C_d
\end{aligned}$$

Where $R_0 \approx 0$ when complaints are ignored (negligible retention).

**Step 3**: The savings from investing in complaint resolution is:

$$\begin{aligned}
Savings &= Cost_{ignore} - Cost_{resolve} \\
        &= N \times C_d - N \times C_r - N \times (1 - R) \times C_d \\
        &= N \times C_d \times R - N \times C_r \\
        &= N \times (R \times C_d - C_r)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: For the investment to be economically justified:

$$R \times C_d > C_r$$

$$R > \dfrac{C_r}{C_d}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation for Students**
> If the cost of resolving a complaint is **Rs. 500** and the lifetime value of a customer is **Rs. 50,000**, the organization needs to retain more than **1%** of complaining customers to break even. In practice, well-designed service recovery retains **30–70%** of customers — a massive ROI.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example for KTU Practice

**Problem:** A telecom company receives **2,400 complaints per month**. The average resolution cost is **Rs. 350** per complaint. The average customer lifetime value is **Rs. 8,000**. The current resolution process retains **45%** of complainants. Calculate the monthly net savings versus a no-resolution baseline.

**Solution using the derived formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Savings &= N \times (R \times C_d - C_r) \\
        &= 2400 \times (0.45 \times 8000 - 350) \\
        &= 2400 \times (3600 - 350) \\
        &= 2400 \times 3250 \\
        &= 78,00,000 \; \text{Rs.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The company saves **Rs. 78 Lakhs per month** by investing in structured complaint resolution — a powerful argument for KTU answer-writing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Customer Complaint Handling Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CUSTOMER IDENTIFIES PROBLEM] --> B[COMPLAINT LOGGED IN CRM]
    B --> C{SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION}
    C -->|Critical P1| D[ESCALATE TO SENIOR MANAGER]
    C -->|Major P2| E[ASSIGN TO TEAM LEAD]
    C -->|Minor P3| F[ASSIGN TO FRONTLINE AGENT]
    D --> G[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS USING 5 WHYS]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[CORRECTIVE ACTION PLAN DRAFTED]
    H --> I[ACTION IMPLEMENTED]
    I --> J[CUSTOMER NOTIFIED OF RESOLUTION]
    J --> K[FOLLOW UP FOR VERIFICATION]
    K --> L{CUSTOMER SATISFIED}
    L -->|Yes| M[CLOSE TICKET AND DOCUMENT LEARNING]
    L -->|No| N[REOPEN AND RE ESCALATE]
    N --> D
    M --> O[FEED INTO CAPA AND FMEA DATABASE]
    O --> P[UPDATE PROCESS STANDARDS]
    P --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: SERVQUAL Gap Architecture and Complaint Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CUSTOMER EXPECTATION] -->|Gap 1| B1[MANAGEMENT PERCEPTION]
    B1 -->|Gap 2| C1[SERVICE SPECIFICATION]
    C1 -->|Gap 3| D1[SERVICE DELIVERY]
    D1 -->|Gap 5| E1[PERCEIVED SERVICE]
    A1 -->|Gap 5 Direct Compare| E1
    F1[EXTERNAL COMMUNICATION] -->|Gap 4| D1
    G1[COMPLAINT ARISES] -->|Triggered by| E1
    H1[TQM INTERVENTION] -->|Closes Gap 1| B1
    H1 -->|Closes Gap 2| C1
    H1 -->|Closes Gap 3| D1
    H1 -->|Closes Gap 4| F1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: TQM Feedback Loop Using Customer Complaints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: CUSTOMER COMPLAINT] --> B[DATABASE STORAGE]
    B --> C[STATISTICAL PROCESS CONTROL]
    C --> D[PARETO CHART ANALYSIS]
    D --> E[ISHIKAWA FISHBONE CAUSE MAPPING]
    E --> F[DMAIC SIX SIGMA PROJECT]
    F --> G[PROCESS REDESIGN]
    G --> H[STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE UPDATE]
    H --> I[EMPLOYEE TRAINING KAOREN]
    I --> J[REDUCED DEFECT RATE]
    J --> K[LOWER COMPLAINT VOLUME]
    K --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a customer complaint. List any four categories of complaints based on nature.

**Model Answer:**
A customer complaint is a formal expression of dissatisfaction voiced by a customer when perceived performance falls below expected performance, resulting in a measurable quality gap.

Four categories based on nature:
1. Product complaints — defects, breakage, specification mismatch
2. Service complaints — late delivery, poor attitude, billing errors
3. Process complaints — long queues, complex procedures
4. Attitudinal complaints — rude behavior, lack of empathy

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is the Service Recovery Paradox? State its significance in TQM.

**Model Answer:**
The Service Recovery Paradox (SRP), proposed by McCollough and Bharadwaj (1992), states that **a customer whose complaint is resolved exceptionally well may become *more loyal* than a customer who never experienced a problem at all.**

$$\text{SRP Index} = \dfrac{L_{\text{after recovery}}}{L_{\text{before complaint}}}$$

**Significance:**
- Turns negative experiences into loyalty boosters
- Provides competitive differentiation
- Justifies investment in complaint-handling infrastructure
- Aligns with Deming's philosophy of building long-term customer relationships

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the SERVQUAL Gap Model in detail. How does Gap 5 lead to customer complaints? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the eight-step customer complaint handling process with a suitable flowchart. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SERVQUAL Gap Model Explanation:**

The SERVQUAL (Service Quality) model was developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry** to identify and analyze the sources of service quality gaps that lead to customer dissatisfaction.

**The Five Gaps:**

| Gap | Name | Description |
|-----|------|-------------|
| Gap 1 | Knowledge Gap | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations |
| Gap 2 | Standards Gap | Difference between management perception and actual service quality specifications |
| Gap 3 | Delivery Gap | Difference between service standards and actual service delivered |
| Gap 4 | Communication Gap | Difference between service delivered and what was externally communicated/promised |
| Gap 5 | Expectation-Perception Gap | Difference between expected service and perceived service |

**Gap 5 and Customer Complaints:**
Gap 5 is the **final and most critical gap** because it directly represents what the customer experiences. When Gap 5 becomes positive and exceeds the customer's *Zone of Tolerance*, the customer voices a complaint. Mathematically:

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$

This additive relationship shows that **every complaint is traceable to upstream organizational failures**, not just frontline service errors.

---

**(b) Eight-Step Complaint Handling Process:**

1. **Receipt** — Logging complaint through CRM/hotline/email with timestamp and customer details
2. **Acknowledgement** — Auto-response and personal acknowledgement within SLA (typically <24 hours)
3. **Classification** — Categorize by type (product/service/process), severity (P1/P2/P3), and department
4. **Investigation** — Use 5-Whys, Ishikawa diagram, and Fishbone analysis for root cause identification
5. **Resolution** — Implement corrective action: refund, replacement, repair, re-service, or apology
6. **Communication** — Proactively update customer on resolution timeline and outcome
7. **Verification** — Follow-up via call/email/survey to confirm satisfaction (CSAT measurement)
8. **Documentation** — Update CAPA register, FMEA database, and SOPs for organizational learning

The flowchart was depicted in Section 4.1 above and should be redrawn neatly in the answer script.

---

#### **Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** "Customer complaints are free consulting reports." Discuss this statement with reference to TQM principles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm receives 1,500 complaints per quarter. The average resolution cost is Rs. 400 per complaint. The lifetime value of a customer is Rs. 12,000. The firm's structured complaint process retains 50% of complainants. Calculate the quarterly net savings versus ignoring complaints. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Complaints as Free Consulting Reports:**

This statement reflects **Kaoru Ishikawa's** philosophy that customers are the most underutilized source of quality information. In TQM:

- **Deming's Perspective** — Deming placed customer focus as one of his 14 Points. Complaints reveal *system-level* defects that internal audits often miss.
- **Juran's Quality Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement all rely on customer feedback, with complaints being the loudest signal.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — Complaints quantify *external failure costs*, enabling investment decisions in *appraisal* and *prevention* activities.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Each complaint is a PDCA cycle trigger: Plan corrective action, Do it, Check effectiveness, Act to standardize.

When properly analyzed, complaint data reveals:
- Recurring defect patterns (Pareto insight)
- Process bottlenecks (Bottleneck analysis)
- Training gaps (HR development needs)
- Design flaws (R&D input)
- Supplier quality issues (Supply chain feedback)

Thus, the company that systematically converts complaints into structured improvement projects gains a **strategic intelligence advantage** — making the complaint literally a *free, unsolicited consulting report*.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 1500$ complaints/quarter
- $C_r = \text{Rs. } 400$ per complaint
- $C_d = \text{Rs. } 12{,}000$ (customer lifetime value)
- $R = 0.50$ (retention ratio)

**Using the derived savings formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Savings &= N \times (R \times C_d - C_r) \\
        &= 1500 \times (0.50 \times 12{,}000 - 400) \\
        &= 1500 \times (6{,}000 - 400) \\
        &= 1500 \times 5{,}600 \\
        &= 84{,}00{,}000 \; \text{Rs.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The firm saves **Rs. 84 Lakhs per quarter** (Rs. 3.36 Crores annually) by investing in structured complaint resolution.

**Comment:** The savings figure is **14 times the total resolution cost** ($1500 \times 400 = \text{Rs. } 6 \text{ Lakhs}$), demonstrating that complaint management is a high-ROI strategic activity, not merely a customer service cost center. This aligns directly with TQM's principle of *prevention over detection*.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "complaints are bad" — examiners deduct marks. The TQM-correct view is *"complaints are valuable feedback assets."*
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In numerical questions, students forget to **define variables** before substitution. Always write: *"Let N = number of complaints..."*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** SERVQUAL Gap 5 is often mistakenly written as a single gap. Examiners expect you to show its **additive decomposition** into Gaps 1-4.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the flowchart in long answers costs 1-2 marks. Always include a labeled diagram.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing *Service Recovery* (reactive) with *Service Design* (proactive). Both are important but distinct.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **customer complaint** arises when the expectation-performance gap exceeds the customer's *Zone of Tolerance*
- **SERVQUAL** identifies 5 gaps; Gap 5 is the complaint-generating gap
- Gap 5 = Gap 1 + Gap 2 + Gap 3 + Gap 4 (additive relationship)
- The **8-step complaint handling process** ensures systematic resolution
- **Service Recovery Paradox**: Excellent recovery creates *higher* loyalty than no problem
- **Complaint Rate** $CR = \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100$
- **Savings Formula**: $Savings = N \times (R \times C_d - C_r)$
- Always define variables before substitution in numerical KTU answers
- Use **Ishikawa, Pareto, and 5-Whys** for root cause analysis of complaints
- Complaints feed into **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action) under ISO 9001:2015 Clause 10.2
- The phrase **"customer is the most important part of the production line"** is a favorite KTU quote worth remembering
- **Cost of poor complaint handling** $\gg$ Cost of complaint resolution infrastructure

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer retention - Costs of quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Customer retention - Costs of quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Customer Retention — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Retention (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Customer retention refers to the activities and strategies a company undertakes to reduce customer defections and to keep existing customers engaged, satisfied, and loyal over a sustained business relationship. It is mathematically expressed as the percentage of customers a company retains over a given time period.*

The standard KTU-aligned formula is:

$$\text{Customer Retention Rate (CRR)} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$$

Where **E** = Customers at the End of period, **N** = New Customers acquired during the period, and **S** = Customers at the Start of period.

A complementary metric is **Customer Defection Rate**:

$$\text{Defection Rate} = 100\% - \text{Customer Retention Rate}$$

### 1.2 Costs of Quality (COQ) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ):** *The Cost of Quality is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to quality requirements, plus the costs incurred due to non-conformance. It is a measure that quantifies the economic impact of quality-related activities.*

The COQ framework uses the universally accepted **PAF Model** containing four cost categories:

1. **Prevention Costs (P)** — Costs of preventing defects.
2. **Appraisal Costs (A)** — Costs of detecting defects.
3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — Costs of defects found before delivery.
4. **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — Costs of defects found after delivery.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Story":**
> Imagine you own a small restaurant. **Customer retention** is the art of making your regular diners keep coming back — through consistent taste, hygiene, ambience, and service. If 100 customers came in January, 20 are new, and 90 are still eating at your place in February, your CRR is 70%.
>
> Now, the **Cost of Quality** is like the money you spend to keep that kitchen perfect:
> - Training chefs (Prevention)
> - Tasting food before serving (Appraisal)
> - Wasted spoilt ingredients (Internal Failure)
> - Compensating a customer who got food poisoning (External Failure)
>
> Notice a golden rule: *Spending more on Prevention/Appraisal SAVES multiples of money on Failure costs.* This is the soul of TQM economics.

**Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall):**

- **CRR** = Customer Retention Rate
- **EOL** = Economic Optimum Level of Quality
- **COPQ** = Cost of Poor Quality
- **COPC** = Cost of Conformance
- **CONC** = Cost of Non-Conformance
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** — Applied heavily in COQ analysis

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Customer Retention Matters in TQM

TQM philosophy, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, holds that *quality is defined by the customer*. Retaining a customer is **5 to 7 times cheaper** than acquiring a new one (industry standard, Harvard Business Review benchmark). The strategic pillars of customer retention are:

- **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** — A precursor to retention.
- **Customer Loyalty** — Behavioral + attitudinal commitment.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — Total revenue a customer generates over their relationship.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — Likelihood of customer recommendation.
- **Switching Costs** — The friction a customer faces when leaving.

### 2.2 Components of the Cost of Quality (PAF Model)

#### A. Prevention Costs (PC)
Costs incurred to *prevent* the occurrence of defects. They are proactive investments.

- Quality planning and administration
- New product review
- Training of employees in quality methods
- Quality improvement projects (Kaizen, Six Sigma)
- Supplier capability assessment
- Statistical Process Control (SPC) design
- Quality audits (prevention portion)

#### B. Appraisal Costs (AC)
Costs incurred to *detect* defects through inspection, testing, and measurement.

- Incoming material inspection
- In-process and final inspection
- Quality audits (detection portion)
- Test equipment maintenance and calibration
- Inspection labor
- Field testing

#### C. Internal Failure Costs (IFC)
Costs of defects discovered *before* the product reaches the customer.

- Scrap and rework
- Re-inspection
- Failure mode analysis
- Downtime due to quality issues
- Material wastage
- Defect correction in software (bug fixing before release)

#### D. External Failure Costs (EFC)
Costs of defects discovered *after* the product reaches the customer — the most damaging category.

- Warranty claims
- Returns and replacements
- Field service costs
- Complaint handling
- Product recalls
- Loss of goodwill / brand damage
- Legal liabilities and lawsuits
- Lost future sales (churned customers)

### 2.3 The Economic Logic of COQ

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Prevention and Appraisal are "Costs of Conformance (COPC)." Internal and External Failure costs are "Costs of Non-Conformance (CONC)."

The fundamental relationship:

$$\text{Total COQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{Total COQ} = \text{Costs of Conformance (COPC)} + \text{Costs of Non-Conformance (CONC)}$$

As quality investment increases, the trade-off curve forms a **U-shaped curve**:

- **Low investment:** Failure costs dominate (Total COQ is high).
- **Optimal point (EOL):** Total COQ is minimized.
- **Over-investment:** Appraisal/Prevention costs exceed the savings, raising Total COQ.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Customer Retention Rate | $\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | Defection Rate | $100\% - \text{CRR}$ | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | Customer Lifetime Value | $\text{CLV} = \text{Avg Purchase} \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Lifetime} \times \text{Margin}$ | Currency |
| 4 | Total COQ | $P + A + IFC + EFC$ | Currency |
| 5 | COPC | $P + A$ | Currency |
| 6 | CONC | $IFC + EFC$ | Currency |
| 7 | COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) | $IFC + EFC$ | Currency |
| 8 | Quality Cost as % of Sales | $\frac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | Optimal Quality Level | Point of minimum Total COQ on U-curve | Dimensionless index |
| 10 | Pareto Principle | ~80% of quality costs come from ~20% of defect types | Defect categorization |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing:** COQ guides investment in automation vs. inspection.
- **Software Engineering:** Prevention = design reviews, Appraisal = testing, IFC = bug fixing pre-release, EFC = patches and lost users.
- **Service Industry:** COQ maps to service recovery, training, and SLA penalties.
- **Strategic Decision Making:** COQ data links quality directly to financial statements — a key Board-level concern.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Customer Retention Rate from Base Data

**Problem Context:** A telecom company had 5,000 customers at the start of Q1, acquired 800 new customers, and ended the quarter with 5,400 customers. Calculate CRR and Defection Rate.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$S = 5000, \quad N = 800, \quad E = 5400$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CRR formula.**

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(5400 - 800)}{5000} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{4600}{5000} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final computation.**

$$\text{CRR} = 0.92 \times 100 = 92\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute Defection Rate.**

$$\text{Defection Rate} = 100\% - 92\% = 8\%$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Cost of Quality Aggregation

**Problem Context:** A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs: Prevention = ₹3,00,000; Appraisal = ₹2,50,000; Internal Failure = ₹4,00,000; External Failure = ₹6,00,000. Net sales = ₹1,00,00,000. Find (a) Total COQ, (b) COPC, (c) CONC, (d) COQ as % of Sales.

**Step 1 — Sum all four categories for Total COQ.**

$$\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IFC + EFC$$

$$\text{Total COQ} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Total COQ} = 15{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2 — Compute COPC (Conformance).**

$$\text{COPC} = P + A = 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CONC (Non-Conformance).**

$$\text{CONC} = IFC + EFC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 4 — Compute COQ as % of Sales.**

$$\text{COQ \%} = \frac{\text{Total COQ}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$$

$$\text{COQ \%} = \frac{15{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 15.5\%$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Optimal Quality Cost (EOL) Analytical Approach

The Total COQ as a function of quality level *q* is modeled as:

$$\text{Total COQ}(q) = \text{COPC}(q) + \text{CONC}(q)$$

Where COPC is monotonically increasing in *q* (more quality = more spend) and CONC is monotonically decreasing in *q* (better quality = fewer failures). The **Economic Optimum Level (EOL)** occurs where:

$$\frac{d}{dq}\text{Total COQ}(q) = 0$$

That is, the point where the marginal increase in prevention/appraisal cost equals the marginal decrease in failure cost:

$$\frac{d}{dq}\text{COPC}(q) = -\frac{d}{dq}\text{CONC}(q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** The EOL is the "sweet spot" — the level of quality investment that minimizes the total cost. Investing beyond EOL is over-engineering; investing below EOL wastes money on rework and warranty.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Mapping

| Quality Investment Scenario | Prevention (P) | Appraisal (A) | Internal Failure (IFC) | External Failure (EFC) | Total COQ |
|------------------------------|----------------|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Low Investment (Reactive) | Very Low | Low | Moderate | Very High | **Highest** |
| Optimal Investment (TQM) | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low | **Lowest (EOL)** |
| Over-Investment (Over-Inspection) | High | Very High | Very Low | Very Low | **Rising again** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PAF Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["COST OF QUALITY COQ"] --> nodeB["PREVENTION COSTS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["APPRAISAL COSTS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["INTERNAL FAILURE COSTS"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["EXTERNAL FAILURE COSTS"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Training Planning Design Review Supplier Audit"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Inspection Testing Calibration Lab Audits"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Scrap Rework Reinspection Downtime"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Warranty Returns Recalls Lawsuits Lost Goodwill"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["CONFORMANCE COPC"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeK["NON CONFORMANCE CONC"]
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL["TOTAL COQ"]
    nodeK --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Customer Retention & COQ Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["POOR QUALITY"] --> nodeB["HIGH EXTERNAL FAILURE COSTS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["WARRANTY RETURNS RECALLS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CUSTOMER DISSATISFACTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CUSTOMER DEFECTION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["LOW CRR HIGH DEFECTION RATE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["REVENUE LOSS BRAND DAMAGE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["INCREASED ACQUISITION COST"]
    nodeI["INVEST IN PREVENTION APPRAISAL"] --> nodeJ["LOWER INTERNAL FAILURE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["LOWER EXTERNAL FAILURE"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["HIGHER CUSTOMER SATISFACTION"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["HIGHER CRR CUSTOMER LOYALTY"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["HIGHER CLV AND PROFITABILITY"]
```

### 4.3 Economic Optimum Level (EOL) U-Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Quality Investment X axis"] --> nodeB["Cost Y axis"]
    nodeC["CONC curve downward sloping"] --> nodeD["Starts high falls rapidly"]
    nodeE["COPC curve upward sloping"] --> nodeF["Starts low rises gradually"]
    nodeG["Total COQ U shaped curve"] --> nodeH["Minimum at EOL point"]
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["LEFT OF EOL FAILURE DOMINATES"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["RIGHT OF EOL OVER INSPECTION DOMINATES"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["AT EOL TQM BALANCE ACHIEVED"]
```

### 4.4 Customer Retention Driver Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CUSTOMER RETENTION"] --> nodeB["Customer Satisfaction"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Perceived Quality"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Service Reliability"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Switching Costs"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Trust and Loyalty Programs"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Long term CLV Growth"]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Sustainable Profitability"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define Customer Retention Rate. State its significance in Total Quality Management.*

**Model Answer:**
Customer Retention Rate (CRR) is the percentage of existing customers a company retains over a given period, calculated as:

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$$

Where E = customers at end, N = new customers, S = customers at start. In TQM, CRR is significant because it directly measures the outcome of quality initiatives — high quality leads to satisfaction, which leads to retention, which reduces marketing costs and increases Customer Lifetime Value. TQM philosophy treats retention as a leading indicator of sustainable quality performance.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the four categories of Cost of Quality with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**
The four categories under the PAF model are:
1. **Prevention Costs** — e.g., quality training, supplier evaluation.
2. **Appraisal Costs** — e.g., final inspection, test equipment calibration.
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — e.g., scrap, rework.
4. **External Failure Costs** — e.g., warranty claims, product recalls.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Cost of Quality in detail. Describe the PAF model with suitable examples for each cost category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company reports the following annual data: Prevention = ₹2,00,000; Appraisal = ₹1,50,000; Internal Failure = ₹3,00,000; External Failure = ₹4,50,000. Net Sales = ₹50,00,000.
Calculate:
(i) Total COQ
(ii) Cost of Conformance (COPC)
(iii) Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)
(iv) COQ as a percentage of Sales.
Comment on the company's quality cost structure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of COQ & PAF Model:**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost of conformance and non-conformance. It is built on the PAF model:
- **Prevention Costs:** Money spent to *prevent* defects. Example: training programs, design reviews, supplier audits.
- **Appraisal Costs:** Money spent to *detect* defects. Example: incoming inspection, product testing, equipment calibration.
- **Internal Failure Costs:** Money wasted due to defects found *before* delivery. Example: scrap, rework, downtime.
- **External Failure Costs:** Money wasted due to defects found *after* delivery. Example: warranty claims, returns, customer complaints, lawsuits.

Together: $\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IFC + EFC$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total COQ:**

$$\text{Total COQ} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**(ii) COPC (Conformance):**

$$\text{COPC} = P + A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**(iii) CONC (Non-Conformance):**

$$\text{CONC} = IFC + EFC = 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**(iv) COQ as % of Sales:**

$$\text{COQ \%} = \frac{11{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 22\%$$

**Commentary:** The CONC (₹7,50,000) is more than twice the COPC (₹3,50,000), indicating the company is in a **reactive quality mode**. Failure costs dominate, so management should increase investment in prevention and appraisal to push the firm toward the Economic Optimum Level.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern Model]**

**(a)** Define Customer Retention. Explain any five strategies for improving customer retention in a service organization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A subscription-based SaaS firm begins the year with 10,000 customers. During the year it acquires 4,000 new customers, but 1,500 existing customers churn. Calculate (i) the ending customer count, (ii) the Customer Retention Rate, and (iii) the Defection Rate. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Customer Retention Definition & Strategies:**

**Definition:** Customer Retention is the ability of a company to retain its customers over a specified period, ensuring repeat business and loyalty.

**Five Strategies:**
1. **Deliver Consistent Quality** — Meet or exceed customer expectations every time.
2. **Effective Complaint Management** — Resolve issues quickly to convert dissatisfied customers into loyal ones.
3. **Personalized Relationship Management (CRM)** — Use data to customize offerings and communication.
4. **Loyalty Programs** — Reward repeat purchases with points, discounts, or exclusive benefits.
5. **Continuous Feedback Loops** — Use surveys, NPS, and reviews to improve offerings proactively.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Ending Customer Count:**

$$E = S + N - \text{Churned} = 10{,}000 + 4{,}000 - 1{,}500 = 12{,}500 \text{ customers}$$

**(ii) Customer Retention Rate:**

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100 = \frac{(12{,}500 - 4{,}000)}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{8{,}500}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 85\%$$

**(iii) Defection Rate:**

$$\text{Defection Rate} = 100\% - 85\% = 15\%$$

**Commentary:** A CRR of 85% is acceptable in SaaS industries (typical benchmark: 80–90% annually). However, 15% defection indicates room for improvement in onboarding, customer success, and product reliability. Reducing churn by even 5% can substantially increase CLV.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing P (Prevention) with IFC (Internal Failure):** Prevention is BEFORE the defect; Internal Failure is AFTER defect, BEFORE customer.
> 2. **Mixing up COPC and CONC:** COPC = P + A (Conformance). CONC = IFC + EFC (Non-Conformance). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the Defection Rate:** Many students compute CRR but skip the 100% − CRR step. Always show both.
> 4. **Numerical unit errors:** Always specify ₹/INR/Currency. Marks are deducted for unit-less answers.
> 5. **Skipping commentary:** After numerical problems, KTU examiners expect a 1–2 line interpretive comment. Missing it = loss of 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing CRR with CLV:** CRR is a *rate*; CLV is a *monetary value*. Examiners test this contrast.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** is calculated as $(E - N) / S \times 100$. Pair it with **Defection Rate** = $100\% - CRR$.
- **Cost of Quality (COQ)** uses the **PAF model** = **P**revention + **A**ppraisal + **Internal Failure** + **External Failure**.
- **COPC** (Cost of Conformance) = P + A. **CONC** (Cost of Non-Conformance) = IFC + EFC.
- The **Total COQ curve is U-shaped**; the minimum is the **Economic Optimum Level (EOL)**.
- **Prevention and Appraisal are investments**; **Internal and External Failure are penalties**.
- A company spending **less than 40%** of COQ on prevention + appraisal is generally in a **reactive** quality mode.
- Retaining a customer is **5–7x cheaper** than acquiring a new one — link COQ to retention economically.
- The **Pareto Principle (80/20)** applies: ~80% of failure costs come from ~20% of defect types.
- Always conclude numerical answers with a **brief interpretive comment** for full KTU marks.
- The golden TQM rule: *Invest more in Prevention → reduce Failure costs → improve retention → grow profitably.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leadership - Strategic quality planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Leadership - Strategic quality planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Leadership in the Context of Total Quality Management (TQM)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Leadership in TQM is the ability of an individual to influence, motivate, and enable a group of people (employees, teams, departments) toward the achievement of the organization's quality objectives. It is the *driver* of the quality transformation process and is responsible for establishing the *unity of purpose* and *direction* of the organization (drawn from ISO 9000 / Deming's System of Profound Knowledge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Leadership is identified as one of the *core values* and the *critical enabler* of TQM. Without committed, quality-driven leadership, no quality system (ISO 9001, Six Sigma, TQM) can be sustained in the long term.

### 1.2 Strategic Quality Planning (SQP)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Strategic Quality Planning (SQP) is the systematic process by which an organization defines its long-term **quality vision, quality mission, quality goals, quality policies, and quality action plans**, ensuring that quality becomes a *strategic weapon* and is integrated into the overall business strategy, rather than being treated as a separate operational concern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** SQP translates *customer needs and competitive requirements* into measurable quality targets, deployment plans, and standard operating procedures that align every employee with the *organization's quality direction*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a **cricket team's captain** during a World Cup tournament:

| Element in TQM | Cricket Analogy |
|----------------|-----------------|
| **Leadership** | The Captain (sets the tone, leads from the front, motivates players) |
| **Strategic Quality Planning** | The pre-tournament **Strategy Book** (selection plan, batting order, field placements) |
| **Vision** | Winning the World Cup |
| **Mission** | Playing aggressive but disciplined cricket |
| **Quality Goals** | Keeping the run rate below 6, taking wickets every 10 overs |
| **Strategic Plans** | Match-by-match preparation, net practice schedules, fitness plans |
| **Deployment** | Assigning roles to each player based on their strengths |

Just as a captain cannot win the World Cup merely by *wanting* to win, but must create a **plan** and lead the team *through* it, an organization cannot achieve Total Quality through slogans — it requires **committed leadership** driving a **well-thought-out strategic plan**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for Exams:** Always mention *"Leadership is the driver; Strategic Quality Planning is the roadmap."*

### 1.4 The Why-How Bridge

- **WHY** do we need Leadership in TQM? — Because quality requires **culture change**, and culture is changed only by **top-down inspiration combined with bottom-up participation**.
- **WHY** do we need Strategic Quality Planning? — Because ad-hoc, short-term firefighting leads to *quality crises*; long-term excellence requires a **structured roadmap** that aligns resources, technology, and people.
- **HOW** does it work? — Leaders set the direction → Strategic plan converts direction into measurable objectives → Plans are **deployed** throughout the hierarchy → Performance is **measured** → Feedback loop closes the cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Leadership Principles in TQM (Deming & Others)

Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, Philip Crosby, and Kaoru Ishikawa have all emphasized the role of leadership. The commonly accepted **KTU-highlighted leadership principles** for TQM are:

1. **Top Management Commitment** – The senior leadership must *own* the quality initiative; lip-service fails.
2. **Establishing Unity of Purpose** – All divisions must move toward a *single shared direction* of quality.
3. **Internal and External Customer Focus** – Leaders must understand *value* from the customer's viewpoint.
4. **Continuous Improvement Mindset** – Leaders must drive the *PDCA* (Plan-Do-Check-Act) mindset organization-wide.
5. **Empowerment and Involvement** – Leaders must enable people to take ownership (delegation, not abdication).
6. **Walking the Talk (Behavioral Role Modelling)** – Leaders must *practice* what they preach.
7. **Recognition and Reward** – Quality contributors must be visibly rewarded.
8. **Data-Driven Decision Making** – Leaders must rely on *facts* (statistical thinking) and not on gut feel alone.
9. **Communication and Transparency** – A clear two-way communication channel.
10. **Long-Term Commitment** – Quality is a *journey*, not a destination; leaders must be patient.

### 2.2 Strategic Quality Planning (SQP) – Detailed Theoretical Framework

Strategic Quality Planning is **NOT** a single activity; it is a *multi-step systematic process*. The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights the following process:

**Stage 1: Define the Quality Vision and Mission**
- *Vision* — The desired future state of quality in the organization (qualitative, inspiring).
- *Mission* — The fundamental purpose of the organization regarding quality.

**Stage 2: Conduct a Quality Gap Analysis (Current vs. Desired State)**
