Individual Tasks
Kaizen Suggestions] T2 --> D2[Daily PDCA
Individual Tasks
Kaizen Suggestions] T3 --> D3[Daily PDCA
Individual Tasks
Kaizen Suggestions] style S1 fill:#417505,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style O1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style O2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style T1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style T2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style T3 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style D1 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style D2 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style D3 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF ``` --- ### 4.4 The Kaizen Spiral (Long-Term PDCA Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEVEL1[Baseline Level] L1[PDCA Cycle 1
Performance P0] end subgraph LEVEL2[Level 2] L2[PDCA Cycle 2
Performance P1 Greater than P0] end subgraph LEVEL3[Level 3] L3[PDCA Cycle 3

Vision Mission

New Customer Needs
New Targets] FEEDBACK -.-> PLAN style INPUT fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style PLAN fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style DO fill:#F5A623,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style CHECK fill:#7ED321,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style ACT fill:#9013FE,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style OUTPUT fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF style FEEDBACK fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#FFFFFF ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation for KTU Students:** This is the *exact* operating model of **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** in KTU. Your course outcomes (COs) are the "KPIs", the teaching-learning process is "DO", the university exam is "CHECK", and the syllabus revision is "ACT". ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Performance Appraisal. List any three modern methods of performance appraisal.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Performance Appraisal is a systematic and continuous evaluation of an employee's job performance against predefined quality standards and organizational objectives. (2 Marks)* *Three modern methods are: (1 Mark)* 1. **360-Degree Appraisal** — feedback from superiors, peers, subordinates, customers and self. 2. **Management by Objectives (MBO)** — appraisal based on jointly agreed measurable goals. 3. **Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scale (BARS)** — combines critical incidents with a numerical rating scale. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the four stages of the PDCA cycle in brief.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Stage | Activity | Marks | |---|---|---| | **Plan** | Identify problem, analyze root causes, set SMART objectives, develop hypothesis | 1 | | **Do** | Implement on small scale, collect data, train people, document observations | 0.75 | | **Check** | Compare actual results vs plan, statistical analysis, identify deviations | 0.75 | | **Act** | Standardize if successful, else begin new PDCA cycle with revised plan | 0.5 | --- ### 📝 PART B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) **Note: KTU ESE provides an internal choice. Both alternatives are given below.** --- ## 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 — Internal Choice A]** ### (a) **Discuss the objectives and modern methods of Performance Appraisal. How does Performance Appraisal support the TQM philosophy?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Performance Appraisal in TQM is a participative, data-driven, and continuous evaluation system. It integrates human resource practices with quality objectives. **Objectives of Performance Appraisal (3 Marks):** 1. **Identify training needs** — Skill gaps are mapped against quality standards. 2. **Provide constructive feedback** — Two-way communication, removing the "annual surprise" approach. 3. **Align individual goals with organizational goals** — Cascading of Quality Vision from top to bottom. 4. **Determine rewards and recognition** — Merit-based, tied to measurable quality KPIs. 5. **Validate HR decisions** — Promotion, transfers, increments are evidence-based. 6. **Document for legal and ISO audit** — Traceability for ISO 9001 compliance. **Modern Methods (2 Marks):** | Method | Focus | TQM Alignment | |---|---|---| | 360-Degree | Multi-source feedback | Participation, customer focus | | MBO | Result-based goals | Process approach, data-driven | | BARS | Behavior + scale | Objectivity, fairness | | Assessment Centre | Simulation-based | Real-world competence | **How PA Supports TQM Philosophy (1 Mark):** PA operationalizes TQM's *people-centric* pillar. By tying appraisals to quality KPIs (defect rate, on-time delivery, CSAT), the firm ensures every employee is contributing to the Deming philosophy of "**Survival through continuous improvement**". **Conclusion (Implicit — Quality of writing):** A well-designed PA system is the *human interface* of PDCA — it motivates the workforce to participate actively in continuous improvement. --- ### (b) **Explain the PDCA cycle in detail. State the role of each stage in Continuous Process Improvement.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The PDCA cycle (Deming Cycle) is a four-stage iterative model for continuous process improvement. It assumes improvement is a *never-ending* scientific process. **Plan (1.5 Marks):** - Identify the problem or improvement opportunity. - Use **5-Why analysis, Pareto chart, Fishbone (Ishikawa)** to find root causes. - Define **SMART objectives**. - Develop a hypothesis: *"If we do X, then Y will improve by Z%."* **Do (1.5 Marks):** - Implement the proposed change on a **pilot (small) scale**. - Train the team and execute carefully. - Collect **all data**, including anomalies and side-effects. - Document every observation. **Check (1.5 Marks):** - Compare **actual results** with **expected/predicted results**. - Use **control charts, histograms, run charts** for statistical analysis. - Identify what worked, what didn't, and why. - Document lessons learned — both successes and failures. **Act (1 Mark):** - **If successful:** Standardize the change → update SOPs → train all staff → communicate. - **If unsuccessful:** Revise hypothesis → begin a new PDCA cycle. - The cycle never stops — every Act is the Plan of the next cycle. **Role in CPI (0.5 Marks):** PDCA converts improvement from a *one-time event* into a *systematic, repeatable, scientific practice*. Each rotation yields incremental gains — the **Kaizen spiral**. Mathematically: $$ P_n = P_0 (1+k)^n $$ demonstrating **exponential growth** through consistent PDCA execution. **Diagrammatic Conclusion (for full marks — students should add a PDCA wheel diagram):** ``` PLAN / \ / \ ACT DO \ / \ / CHECK ``` --- ## 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 — Internal Choice B]** ### (a) **What are the common errors in Performance Appraisal? Explain the 360-Degree Appraisal method in detail.** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Common Errors in Performance Appraisal (3.5 Marks):** | Error | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Halo Effect** | One positive trait dominates all ratings | Good dresser rated "good at everything" | | **Horn Effect** | One negative trait dominates all ratings | Late once → marked poor overall | | **Central Tendency** | Rate everyone as average | All employees get 3/5 | | **Recency Bias** | Recent events override full-year performance | Last-month success hides yearly failure | | **First Impression Bias** | Day-1 impression persists | Early punctuality dominates despite later lateness | | **Similar-to-Me Bias** | Favouring those similar to appraiser | Same college / hometown preferred | | **Contrast Effect** | Comparing to previous employee, not standards | Average employee looks great after poor one | **360-Degree Appraisal in Detail (3.5 Marks):** *Definition:* A multi-source, multi-rater feedback system where an employee is evaluated by **all stakeholders** in their work circle. **Sources (Raters):** - **Superior (40%)** — performance against goals - **Self (20%)** — self-assessment - **Peers (20%)** — collaboration, teamwork - **Subordinates (10%)** — leadership quality - **Customers (10%)** — service quality **Composite Score Formula:** $$ S = 0.4M + 0.2\text{Self} + 0.2P + 0.1\text{Sub} + 0.1C $$ **Advantages:** - Reduces individual appraiser bias. - Provides holistic view of behaviour + results. - Promotes TQM's *participation* culture. - Encourages self-reflection. **Limitations:** - Time-consuming and paper-intensive. - Risk of **collusion** among peers. - Cultural resistance in hierarchical organizations. --- ### (b) **Discuss Continuous Process Improvement (CPI) in detail. How does PDCA enable CPI? What are the 7 basic quality tools used in the Plan and Check stages?** *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Introduction to CPI (1 Mark):** Continuous Process Improvement (CPI), or Kaizen, is an ongoing effort to enhance products, services, and processes through small, incremental changes driven by employee participation and data. **Key Principles of CPI (2 Marks):** 1. **Good processes, not good people** — fix the system first. 2. **Small steps daily** — 1% improvement compounded > 100% improvement overnight. 3. **Speak with data** — facts over opinions. 4. **Go to Gemba** — improvement starts at the actual workplace. 5. **Customer focus** — internal and external. **How PDCA Enables CPI (2 Marks):** | CPI Need | PDCA Response | |---|---| | Structured methodology | 4-stage discipline | | Scientific approach | Hypothesis → Test → Verify → Adopt | | Standardization | Act-stage documentation | | Sustainability | Spiral (not one-time) | | Measurement | Check-stage statistical tools | Mathematically, with $k = 0.01$ improvement per cycle and $n = 12$ cycles/year: $$ P_{12} = P_0 (1.01)^{12} \approx 1.126 \cdot P_0 $$ A **12.6% annual improvement** with just 1% per cycle — the power of disciplined PDCA. **7 Basic Tools of Quality used in PLAN and CHECK stages (2 Marks):** | # | Tool | Stage | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa/Fishbone) | Plan | Identify root causes | | 2 | **Check Sheet** | Plan | Structured data collection | | 3 | **Control Chart** | Check | Monitor process stability | | 4 | **Histogram** | Check | Show frequency distribution | | 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Plan | Prioritize vital few problems | | 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Check | Show correlation between variables | | 7 | **Flowchart** | Plan | Map the process steps | **Conclusion:** PDCA is the *engine*, the 7 tools are the *fuel*, and CPI is the *journey* — together they create a self-sustaining quality culture. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Writing PDCA without explaining "why each step is necessary"** — Just listing the 4 stages is worth only 2 of the 7 marks. You MUST explain the *purpose* and *tools* used in each stage. > 2. **Confusing PA with HR appraisal alone** — In KTU TQM, PA is a **quality tool**, not just an HR formality. Always link it to PDCA and customer satisfaction. > 3. **Forgetting the "Continuous" part of CPI** — Many answers describe one improvement cycle and stop. **Always emphasize the spiral/repeating nature** of PDCA. > 4. **Listing the 7 QC tools without specifying which PDCA stage** they belong to — This is a classic 2-mark trap. Map each tool to Plan/Do/Check/Act explicitly. > 5. **Skipping diagrams** — Even in essay answers, a small PDCA wheel or 360° rater diagram earns 1–2 extra marks easily. > 6. **Writing "Kaizen = Japanese for improvement" and stopping** — Examiner expects **5 pillars of Kaizen** or at least one numerical example (e.g., $P_n = P_0(1+k)^n$). > 7. **Mixing up DMAIC with PDCA** — DMAIC is the **Six Sigma** variant (5 stages); PDCA is the **Deming** variant (4 stages). Examiners deduct marks for this confusion. --- ### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam** - ⭐ **Performance Appraisal** = systematic, continuous evaluation against quality standards — NOT just an HR event. - ⭐ In TQM, PA is a **quality tool** that feeds the PDCA cycle through feedback. - ⭐ **Modern methods of PA:** 360°, MBO, BARS, Assessment Centre. - ⭐ **Errors in PA:** Halo, Horn, Central Tendency, Recency, Similar-to-Me, Contrast. - ⭐ **CPI = Kaizen** = ongoing incremental improvement driven by employees. - ⭐ **CPI 5 Pillars:** Customer, Process, People, Data, Supplier. - ⭐ **PDCA** = Plan, Do, Check, Act (also called Deming Cycle / Shewhart Cycle). - ⭐ **PDCA is a cycle, not a linear sequence** — visualize it as a **wheel** or **spiral**. - ⭐ The **Act** stage decides: **Standardize (success)** OR **Restart the cycle (failure)**. - ⭐ **7 QC Tools:** Cause-and-Effect, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart. - ⭐ **Pareto principle:** 20% of causes → 80% of problems. - ⭐ **360° weights:** Supervisor (40%) + Self (20%) + Peers (20%) + Subordinates (10%) + Customers (10%). - ⭐ **PDCA variant PDSA** is used in healthcare; **SDCA** stabilizes before improving. - ⭐ **Kaizen math:** $P_n = P_0 (1+k)^n$ → even 1% per cycle yields ~12.6% annual growth. - ⭐ **OBE-NEP 2020 connection:** Course Outcomes (COs) are KPIs; teaching = DO; exam = CHECK; syllabus revision = ACT. - ⭐ **Always** mention **Gemba**, **Customer Focus**, and **Data-Driven** in any CPI answer. - ⭐ **KTU favourite questions:** "Explain PDCA in detail", "Discuss 360° appraisal", "What is Kaizen? How is it linked to PDCA?" - ⭐ Final tip — **Draw the PDCA wheel** in every relevant answer; it's a guaranteed 1-mark visual aid.

topics.title: Kaizen - Supplier partnership - Partnering
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kaizen - Supplier partnership - Partnering

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Kaizen — Continuous Improvement

### Formal Definition
**Kaizen** (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy meaning *"Change for the Better"* — a systematic, ongoing process of **incremental, small-scale improvements** involving **every employee**, from the CEO to the shop-floor worker. It is a cornerstone of the Toyota Production System (TPS) and the broader Total Quality Management (TQM) framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Kaizen is the practice of continuous, incremental improvement of processes, products, and services through the active participation of all employees, with a focus on eliminating waste (*Muda*), unevenness (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden of Quality"
Imagine a well-tended garden:
- You don't wait for the plants to die before watering them.
- Every day, you pull a few weeds, prune a few leaves, and add a little fertilizer.
- The garden never has a "grand renovation day" — improvement is **daily, tiny, and constant**.

This is Kaizen. A factory adopting Kaizen doesn't wait for a major project; it improves **5 minutes a day, every day, by everyone**.

### The Two Pillars of Kaizen
| Pillar | Name | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1** | **Kaikaku** | Radical / breakthrough change (system/technology) |
| **Pillar 2** | **Kaizen** | Incremental / gradual improvement (people/process) |

### The 3 "Ms" of Waste Elimination
> [!NOTE]
> The **3Ms framework** (originally Toyota's) identifies what Kaizen targets:
> - **Muda** — Waste (non-value-adding activities)
> - **Mura** — Unevenness (irregular flow)
> - **Muri** — Overburden (excessive strain on people/machines)

---

## 1.2 Supplier Partnership

### Formal Definition
A **Supplier Partnership** (also called *Supplier Relationship Management — SRM*) is a **long-term, cooperative, win-win relationship** between a buyer (customer) and a supplier, built on **mutual trust, transparency, open communication, and shared goals of quality, cost reduction, and continuous improvement**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Idea:** A supplier is not an adversary to be squeezed on price — they are a **strategic stakeholder** in the buyer's quality chain. The supplier's capability directly determines the buyer's product quality.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Marriage vs. Dating"
- **Traditional Buyer-Supplier = Dating:** You change partners often, negotiate hard, hide information, and the relationship ends when a cheaper option appears.
- **Supplier Partnership = Marriage:** You commit for the long term, share finances, plan children (new products) together, and grow wealth jointly.

### Core Characteristics
1. **Fewer suppliers** (concentration, not fragmentation)
2. **Long-term contracts** (not price-shopped annually)
3. **Open-book costing** (supplier shares cost data)
4. **Joint problem-solving** (not blame-shifting)
5. **Mutual improvement targets**

---

## 1.3 Partnering

### Formal Definition
**Partnering** is the formal process of establishing and nurturing a **strategic alliance** between two or more independent organizations to achieve **specific mutual business objectives** — typically faster, cheaper, and with less risk than either could achieve alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction:** *Supplier Partnership* is a **type** of partnering focused on the supplier-buyer dyad. *Partnering* is the **broader philosophy** that can apply to customers, suppliers, employees, government, and even competitors (strategic alliances).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cricket Team"
A cricket team wins because of partnerships between batsmen — they share a goal (runs), back each other up, communicate constantly, and each understands the other's strengths. One star cannot win alone; **structured cooperation** is the only path to sustained victory.

### The Partnering Equation (conceptual)

$$
\text{Partnering Value} \;=\; f(\text{Trust},\; \text{Communication},\; \text{Shared Vision},\; \text{Mutual Benefit})
$$

> [!TIP]
> If any one of the four variables (Trust, Communication, Shared Vision, Mutual Benefit) drops to **zero**, the entire partnering value collapses — partnership fails.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Kaizen — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Five Core Principles of Kaizen

1. **Know your Customer** — Internal and external customers define value.
2. **Let it Flow** — Eliminate bottlenecks, queues, and waiting time.
3. **Go to Gemba** — *"The real place"* — managers must observe work where it actually happens, not in meeting rooms.
4. **Empower People** — Every worker has the authority and skill to suggest and implement small improvements.
5. **Manage by Process, Not by Results** — Fix the process; the results will follow.

### 2.1.2 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel) — The Engine of Kaizen

Kaizen is operationalized through the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, originally articulated by Walter Shewhart and popularized by W. Edwards Deming.

$$
\text{Kaizen} \;=\; \text{PDCA repeated infinitely, by everyone, every day}
$$

| Step | Name | Activity | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Plan | Identify problem, analyze root cause, propose countermeasure | *Diagnose before surgery* |
| **D** | Do | Implement countermeasure on a small scale (pilot) | *Try the new recipe* |
| **C** | Check | Measure results, compare with prediction | *Taste and adjust* |
| **A** | Act | Standardize if successful, or restart cycle with new plan | *Make it the house recipe* |

### 2.1.3 The 5S Methodology — Foundation of Kaizen Workplace

5S is the *prerequisite* for any Kaizen activity to succeed.

| Step | Japanese | English | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Keep only what is needed |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | A place for everything, everything in its place |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace daily |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Make 1–3 a habit through rules |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Self-discipline to maintain the system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** Without **5S**, Kaizen suggestions cannot be implemented effectively — clutter hides problems.

### 2.1.4 Seven Wastes (Muda) — Targets of Kaizen

The classic **TIMWOOD** framework identifies the seven wastes Kaizen seeks to eliminate:

$$
\text{Waste} \;\in\; \{\text{Transport},\; \text{Inventory},\; \text{Motion},\; \text{Waiting},\; \text{Overproduction},\; \text{Over-processing},\; \text{Defects}\}
$$

---

## 2.2 Supplier Partnership — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Traditional vs. Partnership Approach

| Dimension | Traditional (Adversarial) | Partnership (Collaborative) |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Short-term contracts | Long-term (3–10+ years) |
| **Number of suppliers** | Many (price-shopped) | Few (preferred vendors) |
| **Information sharing** | Minimal, guarded | Open-book, transparent |
| **Conflict resolution** | Legal/contractual | Joint problem-solving |
| **Improvement** | Supplier's burden | Joint Kaizen teams |
| **Cost focus** | Purchase price only | Total cost of ownership |
| **Trust level** | Low | High |

### 2.2.2 Steps to Build a Supplier Partnership

1. **Identify critical suppliers** — Pareto analysis on quality/cost impact.
2. **Segment suppliers** — Kraljic matrix (strategic, leverage, bottleneck, routine).
3. **Communicate vision** — Joint strategy workshops.
4. **Establish shared metrics** — Quality, delivery, cost, innovation KPIs.
5. **Joint Kaizen projects** — Cross-functional teams.
6. **Reward partnership** — Volume guarantees, preferred status, recognition.
7. **Continuous review** — Quarterly business reviews (QBRs).

### 2.2.3 The Supplier Partnership Model

$$
\text{Partnership Effectiveness} \;=\; \frac{(\text{Trust} \times \text{Communication} \times \text{Shared Goals})}{\text{Number of Suppliers}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Reducing the supplier base concentrates trust and communication → higher effectiveness per supplier.

---

## 2.3 Partnering — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Seven Elements of Partnering (CRITICAL SUCCESS FACTORS)

These are the standard pillars in the partnering literature and are **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions**.

| # | Element | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mutual Trust** | Belief that the other party will act fairly and honestly |
| 2 | **Open Communication** | Free flow of information without hidden agendas |
| 3 | **Shared Vision & Goals** | Common strategic direction |
| 4 | **Win-Win Attitude** | Outcomes must benefit all parties |
| 5 | **Joint Problem Resolution** | Conflict handled collaboratively |
| 6 | **Long-term Commitment** | Patience through short-term setbacks |
| 7 | **Continuous Improvement** | Joint Kaizen mindset applied to the relationship itself |

### 2.3.2 The Partnering Process — Stages

1. **Identification** — Recognize a potential partner with complementary strengths.
2. **Selection & Due Diligence** — Evaluate financial, technical, cultural fit.
3. **Negotiation** — Define scope, roles, KPIs, conflict resolution.
4. **Charter Formation** — Sign a Partnering Charter (formal document).
5. **Implementation** — Joint teams, joint planning, joint execution.
6. **Review & Renewal** — Periodic performance audits.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Unit / Type | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kaizen | $\Delta Q_{small}$ | Continuous | Daily small improvements |
| 3Ms | $Muda + Mura + Muri$ | Categorical | Waste to eliminate |
| 5S | $S_1 \to S_5$ | Sequential | Workplace organization |
| PDCA | $P \to D \to C \to A$ | Cyclic | Improvement engine |
| TIMWOOD | 7 wastes | Categorical | Kaizen targets |
| Partnership Effectiveness | $\frac{T \cdot C \cdot G}{N}$ | Ratio | Supplier consolidation |
| Kraljic Matrix | $2\times 2$ | Strategic | Supplier segmentation |
| 7 Elements | 7 pillars | Qualitative | Partnering success |

> [!WARNING]
> No `vert` symbol is needed in the cheat-sheet formulas; the expressions above use only standard LaTeX operators. Remember: **Kaizen = small steps, PDCA = the method, 5S = the foundation**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PDCA Iteration Equation

Kaizen improvement over time can be mathematically expressed as a cumulative sequence of PDCA cycles. Let us derive the formula.

### Step 1: Define a Single PDCA Cycle Gain

For a single PDCA cycle, the *net improvement* in a process metric (e.g., defect rate, cycle time) is defined as:

$$
\Delta_i \;=\; Q_{i,\text{after}} \;-\; Q_{i,\text{before}}
$$

where:
- $\Delta_i$ = improvement achieved in the $i^{\text{th}}$ cycle
- $Q_{i,\text{before}}$ = process metric before cycle $i$
- $Q_{i,\text{after}}$ = process metric after cycle $i$

> Convention: for *defect rate*, $\Delta_i < 0$ indicates improvement (lower is better).

### Step 2: Total Improvement Over $n$ Cycles

The total cumulative improvement is the sum of all individual cycle gains:

$$
\Delta_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big( Q_{i,\text{after}} - Q_{i,\text{before}} \big)
$$

### Step 3: Final Process State

The process state after $n$ PDCA cycles is therefore:

$$
Q_n \;=\; Q_0 \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i
$$

where $Q_0$ is the initial baseline process state.

### Step 4: Limiting Behaviour — The "True Kaizen" Limit

In a true Kaizen culture, improvements are made **infinitesimally small but infinitely frequent**. Treating $n \to \infty$ and $\Delta_i \to 0$, the discrete sum becomes a continuous integral:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} Q_n \;=\; Q_0 \;+\; \int_{0}^{T} \frac{dQ}{dt}\, dt
$$

This is the **continuous improvement trajectory** — the deeper mathematical justification for Kaizen's "small steps forever" philosophy.

$$
\boxed{\;Q(T) \;=\; Q_0 \;+\; \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \dot{Q}(t)\, dt\;}
$$

where $\dot{Q}(t) = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ is the rate of improvement at time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even if $\dot{Q}(t)$ is tiny (say, 0.1% per week), over $T = 5$ years ($\approx 260$ weeks), the cumulative improvement can exceed **100%** — illustrating the **power of compound small steps**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Kaizen, Supplier Partnership, Partnering

| Dimension | Kaizen | Supplier Partnership | Partnering |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Japan, post-WWII (Toyota) | USA/UK, 1980s SRM | Construction & manufacturing, 1990s |
| **Scope** | Internal (within firm) | Buyer–supplier dyad | Multi-party alliance |
| **Time horizon** | Continuous, lifelong | Long-term (years) | Long-term (years) |
| **Key driver** | People, mindset | Trust, cost/quality | Mutual strategic gain |
| **Primary tool** | PDCA, 5S | QBRs, joint Kaizen | Charter, joint steering committee |
| **Measure of success** | $\int \dot{Q}\, dt$ | Defect PPM, on-time delivery | ROI, market share, innovation rate |
| **Failure mode** | Stops at suggestion stage | Trust erosion after dispute | Goal drift, opportunism |
| **Key enabler** | Empowerment | Transparency | Shared vision |
| **Relationship to TQM** | Core TQM tool | Supports TQM supply chain | Cross-functional TQM enabler |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Kaizen Cycle Calculation

**Problem (KTU Practice Style):**
A CNC machining cell has a baseline defect rate of $Q_0 = 8.0\%$. Four consecutive PDCA Kaizen cycles reduce defects by $\Delta_1 = -1.2\%$, $\Delta_2 = -0.8\%$, $\Delta_3 = -0.5\%$, $\Delta_4 = -0.3\%$. Calculate the final defect rate.

**Solution:**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\Delta_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{4} \Delta_i \;=\; (-1.2) + (-0.8) + (-0.5) + (-0.3)
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{total}} \;=\; -2.8\%
$$

Final process state:

$$
Q_4 \;=\; Q_0 + \Delta_{\text{total}} \;=\; 8.0\% - 2.8\% \;=\; 5.2\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q_4 \;=\; 5.2\%\;}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 35% relative reduction in defects (2.8 / 8.0) was achieved through four small, sequential PDCA cycles — the essence of Kaizen.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kaizen PDCA Continuous Improvement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN: Identify problem and analyze root cause"] --> B["DO: Implement small scale pilot countermeasure"]
    B --> C["CHECK: Measure results against prediction"]
    C --> D{"Was the pilot successful?"}
    D -- YES --> E["ACT: Standardize the new process and update SOP"]
    D -- NO --> F["Revise hypothesis and identify new root cause"]
    E --> G["Document lesson and train all employees"]
    G --> A
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle is **closed** — even after standardization, it loops back to Plan, because the next layer of improvement always exists. This is the mathematical limit $n \to \infty$ made visible.

---

## 4.2 5S Pyramid — Foundation for Kaizen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S5["S5 SHITSUKE: Sustain and Self Discipline"] --> S4["S4 SEIKETSU: Standardize"]
    S4 --> S3["S3 SEISO: Shine and Clean"]
    S3 --> S2["S2 SEITON: Set in Order"]
    S2 --> S1["S1 SEIRI: Sort and Remove Unnecessary Items"]
```

---

## 4.3 Supplier Partnership — Continuous Improvement Flywheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BUYER["BUYER CUSTOMER FIRM"] -- "Share quality and demand forecasts" --> PARTNER["STRATEGIC SUPPLIER PARTNER"]
    PARTNER -- "Deliver defect free components JIT" --> BUYER
    PARTNER -- "Cost transparency open book" --> BUYER
    BUYER -- "Long term volume guarantee" --> PARTNER
    BUYER -- "Joint Kaizen projects" --> PARTNER
    PARTNER -- "Joint Kaizen projects" --> BUYER
    BUYER --> TRUST["Mutual Trust and Transparency"]
    PARTNER --> TRUST
    TRUST --> BUYER
    TRUST --> PARTNER
```

> The flywheel shows that each cycle of joint improvement **reinforces** trust, which in turn enables more ambitious joint projects.

---

## 4.4 Partnering — Seven Elements Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CENTER["STRATEGIC PARTNERSHIP OUTCOME"]
    CENTER --- E1["1 Mutual Trust"]
    CENTER --- E2["2 Open Communication"]
    CENTER --- E3["3 Shared Vision and Goals"]
    CENTER --- E4["4 Win Win Attitude"]
    CENTER --- E5["5 Joint Problem Resolution"]
    CENTER --- E6["6 Long Term Commitment"]
    CENTER --- E7["7 Continuous Improvement"]
```

> All seven elements must be **simultaneously present**. Removing any single spoke collapses the wheel — this matches the conceptual equation from Section 1.

---

## 4.5 Kaizen — Supplier Partnership — Partnering Integration Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    META["META LEVEL: TQM CULTURE OF EXCELLENCE"]
    META --> KZ["KAIZEN: Internal continuous improvement engine"]
    META --> PRT["PARTNERING: External alliance philosophy"]
    KZ --> KZ1["5S Workplace"]
    KZ --> KZ2["PDCA Cycles"]
    KZ --> KZ3["Muda Mura Muri elimination"]
    PRT --> SP["SUPPLIER PARTNERSHIP: Buyer supplier dyad"]
    SP --> SP1["Fewer long term suppliers"]
    SP --> SP2["Open book cost sharing"]
    SP --> SP3["Joint quality improvement"]
    KZ2 --> SP3
    PRT --> OT1["Customer partnering"]
    PRT --> OT2["Employee partnering"]
    PRT --> OT3["Government partnering"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Hook:** Kaizen operates *inside* the firm; Partnering operates *across* firms; Supplier Partnership is the *most important* specific form of partnering in a TQM supply chain.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Kaizen. List any four guiding principles of Kaizen. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Kaizen (改善, *Kai* = change, *Zen* = good) is a Japanese management philosophy of **continuous, incremental improvement** involving all employees, focused on eliminating waste and improving standardized processes.

**Four guiding principles:**
1. **Customer focus** — internal and external customers define value.
2. **Process orientation** — fix the process, the result follows.
3. **Gemba (real place)** — go and observe work where it is done.
4. **Empowerment of people** — every employee is an improvement agent.

> [!NOTE]
> Valid alternatives: PDCA mindset, small-step philosophy, 5S foundation, Muda elimination. Any four correctly named principles earn full marks.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between *Traditional Supplier Relationship* and *Supplier Partnership*. State any three differences. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Traditional Relationship | Supplier Partnership |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Short-term, transactional, often adversarial | Long-term, collaborative, win-win |
| 2 | Many suppliers; price-shopped annually | Few selected suppliers; strategic |
| 3 | Information is guarded / one-sided | Open-book costing, transparent communication |
| 4 | Quality is the supplier's sole responsibility | Joint quality improvement via Kaizen |
| 5 | Conflict resolved by contract / law | Conflict resolved jointly, focusing on the problem |

> Any **three** clearly explained differences = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Module 2 questions** carry 14 marks with internal choice. Solve **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**, or as the question paper specifies.

---

### **Q.A.1** (a) Explain the PDCA cycle in detail. How does it operationalize Kaizen? `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, also called the *Deming Wheel* or *Shewhart Cycle*, is the iterative four-step method by which Kaizen improvements are tested, validated, and institutionalized.

**Step 1 — PLAN:**
- Identify a problem or opportunity (e.g., high defect rate at workstation X).
- Analyse root cause using tools like **5 Whys, fishbone diagram, Pareto chart**.
- Develop a hypothesis and propose a countermeasure.
- *Example:* Plan to reduce changeover time from 30 min to 15 min using SMED.

**Step 2 — DO:**
- Implement the countermeasure on a **small, controlled pilot scale**.
- Document observations, deviations, and unexpected effects.
- *Example:* Apply SMED on one machine for one week.

**Step 3 — CHECK:**
- Measure actual outcomes against predicted outcomes using KPIs.
- Use statistical process control (SPC) charts, before-after comparisons.
- *Example:* New changeover time measured = 17 min, vs. target 15 min → partial success.

**Step 4 — ACT:**
- If successful → **standardize**: update SOPs, train all operators, lock the new method.
- If not successful → revise the hypothesis and **loop back to PLAN**.

**How PDCA Operationalizes Kaizen:**

$$
\text{Kaizen} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \text{PDCA}^{\,n}
$$

PDCA is the **vehicle** that converts the Kaizen philosophy (continuous improvement) into **concrete, measurable, repeatable action**. Without PDCA, Kaizen remains a slogan; with PDCA, it becomes a habit.

---

### **Q.A.1** (b) Explain the 5S methodology. Why is it considered the foundation of Kaizen? `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**5S** is a five-step workplace organization method originating from Toyota, designed to create a clean, orderly, and disciplined work environment.

| Step | Japanese | English | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Place every necessary item in a designated, labelled location |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace and equipment daily |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish visual standards and schedules to sustain steps 1–3 |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Build self-discipline and a culture of continuous adherence |

**Why 5S is the Foundation of Kaizen:**

1. **Visibility of problems** — A cluttered workplace hides defects, leaks, and waste. 5S exposes abnormalities instantly, giving Kaizen teams *something to improve*.
2. **Standardization** — Kaizen requires a stable baseline to measure improvement. 5S creates that baseline.
3. **Discipline** — 5S cultivates the worker mindset needed to identify and act on small problems every day.
4. **Safety** — A 5S workplace is inherently safer, enabling Kaizen teams to work efficiently.
5. **Pre-requisite** — In KTU parlance, **no 5S, no Kaizen** — it is the "operating system" on which Kaizen runs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often describe 5S in English alone without the Japanese terms. KTU examiners expect **both** the Japanese and English names for full marks.

---

### **Q.B.1** (a) What is Supplier Partnership? Discuss its key characteristics and the steps involved in building it. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A *Supplier Partnership* is a long-term, cooperative, trust-based relationship between a buyer and a supplier, in which both work jointly to improve quality, reduce total cost, and innovate.

**Key Characteristics:**
1. **Long-term orientation** — 3 to 10+ year horizons, not annual contracts.
2. **Mutual trust and transparency** — open-book costing, shared forecasts.
3. **Joint problem-solving** — eight disciplines (8D) teams across firms.
4. **Shared risk and reward** — supplier gains volume stability; buyer gains quality assurance.
5. **Continuous joint improvement** — joint Kaizen projects on supplier's premises.
6. **Reduced supplier base** — few, deeply integrated suppliers rather than many shallow ones.

**Steps in Building a Supplier Partnership:**

1. **Strategic assessment** — Identify which materials/services are critical (Kraljic matrix).
2. **Supplier segmentation** — Classify into *strategic, leverage, bottleneck, routine*.
3. **Partner selection** — Evaluate technical, financial, cultural fit.
4. **Joint visioning workshop** — Align on shared goals, KPIs, and success metrics.
5. **Partnering Charter** — Formal agreement on roles, escalation, IP, and exit clauses.
6. **Pilot collaboration** — Start with one joint Kaizen project to build trust.
7. **Scale and integrate** — Expand to EDI, vendor-managed inventory (VMI), joint design.
8. **Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs)** — Measure, recognize, recalibrate.

---

### **Q.B.1** (b) Explain the concept of Partnering. Discuss the seven critical success elements of a successful partnership. `(7 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Partnering:** Partnering is the **structured, long-term commitment between two or more independent organizations** to cooperate in achieving specific mutual business objectives by combining complementary capabilities and resources.

**Seven Critical Success Elements:**

1. **Mutual Trust** — The cornerstone. Built through consistency, honesty, and follow-through over time.
2. **Open Communication** — Free, timely, two-way information flow; no hidden agendas.
3. **Shared Vision and Goals** — A documented, jointly created strategic intent.
4. **Win-Win Attitude** — Outcomes must create value for *all* parties; zero-sum thinking poisons partnerships.
5. **Joint Problem Resolution** — Structured escalation that addresses the *problem*, not the *person*.
6. **Long-Term Commitment** — Patience through short-term setbacks; no "exit-on-first-loss" mentality.
7. **Continuous Improvement** — Joint Kaizen mindset; the relationship itself is improved iteratively using PDCA.

**Conceptual Value Model:**

$$
\text{Partnership Value} \;=\; f(\text{Trust},\; \text{Communication},\; \text{Vision},\; \text{WW-Win},\; \text{Resolution},\; \text{Commitment},\; \text{Kaizen})
$$

> All seven are **necessary and jointly sufficient**; absence of any one significantly degrades partnership effectiveness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Confusing *Partnering* with *Partnership* — partnering is the **process**, partnership is the **state**.
> 2. Writing only 5 elements instead of 7 — examiners will deduct for incomplete coverage.
> 3. Omitting Japanese terms (Kaizen, Gemba) in TQM answers — KTU expects TQM-specific vocabulary.
> 4. Listing features without explaining the **mechanism** of *how* each element enables success.
> 5. Forgetting the **long-term orientation** — partnering is not a project, it is a relationship.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kaizen / Supplier Partnership / Partnering**

### Kaizen
- ☑ Japanese: *Kai* (change) + *Zen* (good) = **continuous improvement**
- ☑ **5S**: Seiri → Seiton → Seiso → Seiketsu → Shitsuke (foundation)
- ☑ **PDCA**: Plan → Do → Check → Act (operational method)
- ☑ **3Ms**: Muda, Mura, Muri (waste, unevenness, overburden)
- ☑ **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)**: Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects
- ☑ Small, daily, incremental — by *everyone* — from CEO to operator
- ☑ Gemba = *go to the real place*

### Supplier Partnership
- ☑ **Long-term**, **trust-based**, **win-win** buyer–supplier relationship
- ☑ **Fewer suppliers**, **open-book costing**, **joint Kaizen**
- ☑ Differs from adversarial purchasing — partnership is *collaborative*
- ☑ Kraljic matrix: strategic / leverage / bottleneck / routine suppliers
- ☑ Tool: Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs), joint 8D problem-solving

### Partnering
- ☑ **Broader** than supplier partnership — includes customers, employees, government
- ☑ **Seven CSFs**: Trust, Communication, Shared Vision, Win-Win, Joint Resolution, Long-term Commitment, Kaizen
- ☑ Backed by a **Partnering Charter** — formal, signed, reviewed periodically
- ☑ Fails when any one of the 7 CSFs is missing — *jointly sufficient*

### Equations to Memorize
- Cumulative Kaizen gain: $\;Q_n = Q_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i$
- Continuous limit: $\;Q(T) = Q_0 + \int_0^T \dot{Q}(t)\, dt$
- Partnership effectiveness: $\;\dfrac{T \cdot C \cdot G}{N}$

### Exam-Winning Mantras
- **"Kaizen is the engine, 5S is the fuel, PDCA is the gearbox."**
- **"Supplier Partnership = trust + transparency + joint Kaizen."**
- **"Partnering fails if Trust = 0."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supplier Selection** is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing external vendors or partners who can supply the required materials, components, or services of acceptable quality, at the right price, in the right quantity, and at the right time — to support an organization's operational and quality objectives.

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, supplier selection is not merely a purchasing activity. It is a **strategic quality decision** because, according to Deming's principle, roughly **15% of quality problems** within an organization are attributable to the purchasing function, while **85%** are the responsibility of management and the system. Selecting the *wrong* supplier can corrupt the entire inbound quality chain, regardless of how excellent the internal processes are.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Supplier / Vendor** | An external party that provides goods or services to the buying organization. |
| **Vendor Rating** | A quantitative score assigned to a supplier based on multiple performance criteria. |
| **Sole Supplier** | Only one vendor is chosen for a particular item (high dependency). |
| **Multiple Suppliers** | Two or more vendors supply the same item (lower risk, higher cost). |
| **Outsourcing** | Transferring an internal activity to an external supplier. |
| **SQC (Supplier Quality Capability)** | The ability of a supplier to meet quality specifications consistently. |
| **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** | The maximum acceptable percentage of defective items in a lot. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Choosing a Tailor for Your Wedding Suit**

Imagine you are getting married in three months and need a perfect tailored suit. You wouldn't just walk into the cheapest shop and buy off the rack. Instead, you would:

1. **Identify potential tailors** (the *universe of suppliers*)
2. **Check their portfolios, reviews, fabric quality, past work, and pricing** (the *evaluation criteria*)
3. **Assign weights** — quality matters more than cost, but both matter (*weighted scoring*)
4. **Visit 2–3 finalists** and inspect their stitching (*on-site audit*)
5. **Select the one with the best balance** of quality, price, reliability, and trust (*final selection*)
6. **Establish a feedback loop** so they know exactly what you want (*partnership*)

This is exactly what supplier selection is in TQM — a **structured, multi-criteria decision** rather than a simple price comparison. The "suit" must not only fit today but remain reliable for years.

---

## 1.3 Why Supplier Selection is Critical in TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4 (Control of Externally Provided Processes, Products and Services)**, organizations must determine and apply criteria for the evaluation, selection, monitoring of performance, and re-evaluation of external providers.

### The Quality Chain Logic

$$
\text{Supplier Quality} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inbound Material Quality} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Process Capability} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Product Quality} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Customer Satisfaction}
$$

If any upstream link in this chain is weak, the entire downstream quality is compromised — a phenomenon TQM calls the **"1-10-100 Rule"** (or Rule of Ten):

| Stage of Defect Detection | Cost Multiplier (Relative to Prevention) |
|---|---|
| At supplier's premises | **1 ×** |
| At incoming inspection | **10 ×** |
| During production | **100 ×** |
| After delivery to customer | **1,000 ×** |

> [!NOTE]
> The bold **1, 10, 100, 1000** cost multipliers are a cornerstone figure in KTU exams on supplier-related TQM topics. Memorize them in the ascending order **1 → 10 → 100 → 1000**.

---

## 1.4 Core Objectives of Supplier Selection

1. **Ensure Consistent Quality** of incoming materials / components.
2. **Minimize Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — not just purchase price.
3. **Reduce Supply Chain Risk** through diversification or partnership.
4. **Build Long-Term Strategic Partnerships** with key vendors.
5. **Ensure On-Time Delivery (OTD)** to avoid production line stoppages.
6. **Achieve Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** throughout the supply chain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Supplier Selection Process — Step-by-Step Framework

A structured supplier selection procedure under TQM typically follows **eight sequential steps**:

### Step 1: Identify the Requirement
- Clearly define the material / service specification.
- Determine quantity, quality standards (ISO/ASME/ASTM), delivery schedule.
- Decide whether the item is **strategic**, **leverage**, **bottleneck**, or **non-critical** (Kraljic's Matrix).

### Step 2: Identify Potential Suppliers
- Sources: trade directories, exhibitions, internet searches, referrals.
- Aim for a pool of **3 to 7 potential suppliers** to ensure competitive evaluation.

### Step 3: Establish Selection Criteria
The most common KTU-exam-relevant criteria are:

| S.No. | Criterion | Typical Weight (Engineering Context) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality / Conformance to specs | 30% |
| 2 | Price / Cost | 25% |
| 3 | Delivery reliability (on-time) | 15% |
| 4 | Supplier's technical capability | 10% |
| 5 | Financial stability | 5% |
| 6 | Past performance / References | 5% |
| 7 | Communication & responsiveness | 5% |
| 8 | Location / Logistics | 5% |

### Step 4: Pre-Qualification (Initial Screening)
- Audit the supplier's quality system (ISO 9001 certification).
- Check financial health, capacity, legal compliance.

### Step 5: Request for Quotation (RFQ) / Proposal
- Send detailed technical drawings, specs, quality requirements, and terms.

### Step 6: Evaluation & Comparison
- Apply methods such as **Weighted Point Method**, **Cost-Ratio Method**, **AHP**, or **Vendor Rating**.

### Step 7: Final Selection & Negotiation
- Award the contract to the highest-ranked supplier (subject to negotiation).

### Step 8: Partner Integration & Continuous Monitoring
- Establish feedback, supplier development, and periodic re-evaluation.

---

## 2.2 Number of Suppliers — Strategic Decision

| Strategy | Advantages | Disadvantages | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Supplier** (Sole sourcing) | Strong partnership; lower admin cost; volume discounts | High dependency risk; supply disruption if vendor fails | Strategic items, JIT systems |
| **Multiple Suppliers** | Lower risk; competitive pricing | Less partnership depth; higher admin overhead | Non-critical, commodity items |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Balanced risk and partnership | Moderate complexity | Common engineering practice |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated formula sheet for ALL quantitative methods of supplier selection. These are the most commonly tested formulas in KTU Part B questions.

| S.No. | Method | Formula | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weighted Point Method** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $S_i$ = Total score of supplier $i$; $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$ ($w_j \in [0,1]$, $\sum w_j = 1$); $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ (commonly 0–10 scale) |
| 2 | **Vendor Rating Index** | $\text{VRI} = \dfrac{\sum w_j r_j}{\sum w_j}$ | Normalized weighted average, used when weights are not normalized |
| 3 | **Cost-Ratio Method** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_c}{H_c}$ | $L_c$ = Lowest cost among suppliers; $H_c$ = Cost of the supplier being evaluated. Higher CR = more cost-effective |
| 4 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = P_c + I_c + O_c + F_c + D_c$ | $P_c$ = Purchase cost; $I_c$ = Inspection cost; $O_c$ = Operating cost; $F_c$ = Failure/defect cost; $D_c$ = Downtime/delivery delay cost |
| 5 | **Supplier Performance Index (SPI)** | $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{Q_d + D_d + C_d}{3}$ | $Q_d, D_d, C_d$ = Quality, Delivery, Cost deviation percentages (lower deviation = higher SPI when inverted) |
| 6 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{N_{on\_time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{on\_time}$ = Orders delivered on-time; $N_{total}$ = Total orders |
| 7 | **Defect Rate (Incoming)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{N_{defective}}{N_{inspected}} \times 100$ | Standard quality metric |
| 8 | **1-10-100 Rule** | $C_{late} = 1, 10, 100, 1000 \times C_{prevent}$ | Cost escalates by factor of 10 per stage |
| 9 | **Cumulative Weighted Score** | $\text{CWS}_i = \sum_{j} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | Identical structurally to weighted point but used in multi-round selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - All weights must satisfy $0 \leq w_j \leq 1$ and $\sum w_j = 1$.
> - All ratings $r_{ij}$ are typically integers in $[0, 10]$.
> - Cost-Ratio CR $\in [0, 1]$. CR = 1 means the supplier has the lowest cost.
> - The supplier with the **highest weighted score** is selected (highest quality-to-cost balance).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Supplier selection methods are applied extensively in:

- **Automotive industry** (Toyota Production System — partner-based keiretsu model).
- **Aerospace** (AS9100 certified suppliers only).
- **Electronics manufacturing** (Apple's tight supplier control of Foxconn, TSMC).
- **Pharmaceuticals** (FDA-approved vendors).
- **Construction** (cement, steel, aggregates vendor rating).

In Kerala's industrial context, supplier selection is critical for **Kerala State Electronics Development Corporation (Keltron)**, **HMT**, **BPCL-Kochi Refinery**, and the **coir / cashew / spices export industries** that depend on raw material quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Weighted Point Method (Most Common KTU Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A manufacturing company in Kochi wants to select a supplier for a critical gearbox component. Three suppliers — A, B, C — have been evaluated on four criteria. Use the Weighted Point Method to recommend the best supplier.

### Given Data

| Criterion | Weight ($w_j$) | Supplier A Rating | Supplier B Rating | Supplier C Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Price | 0.30 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Service | 0.10 | 9 | 6 | 8 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify that weights sum to 1 (a KTU valuation check):**

$$
\sum w_j = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier A:**

$$
S_A = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_A = 3.20 + 2.10 + 1.20 + 0.90
$$

$$
S_A = 7.40
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier B:**

$$
S_B = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_B = 2.80 + 2.70 + 1.60 + 0.60
$$

$$
S_B = 7.70
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier C:**

$$
S_C = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_C = 3.60 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 0.80
$$

$$
S_C = 7.60
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and select:**

$$
S_B = 7.70 \;>\; S_C = 7.60 \;>\; S_A = 7.40
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** **Supplier B** is selected with the highest weighted score of **7.70**, despite having slightly lower quality ratings than C — because its superior price and delivery performance yield a better balanced TQM outcome.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost-Ratio Method with Total Cost of Ownership

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Two suppliers, X and Y, are being considered for an industrial pump. Evaluate using the Cost-Ratio Method.

### Given Data

| Cost Component | Supplier X (₹/unit) | Supplier Y (₹/unit) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase price ($P_c$) | 1,200 | 1,000 |
| Inspection cost ($I_c$) | 50 | 80 |
| Operating cost ($O_c$) | 100 | 150 |
| Failure cost ($F_c$) | 80 | 40 |
| Downtime cost ($D_c$) | 70 | 30 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):**

$$
\text{TCO}_X = 1200 + 50 + 100 + 80 + 70 = 1500 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

$$
\text{TCO}_Y = 1000 + 80 + 150 + 40 + 30 = 1300 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cost-Ratio Formula:**

The lowest cost is $\text{TCO}_Y = 1300$. So $L_c = 1300$.

$$
\text{CR}_X = \dfrac{L_c}{\text{TCO}_X} = \dfrac{1300}{1500} = 0.8667
$$

$$
\text{CR}_Y = \dfrac{L_c}{\text{TCO}_Y} = \dfrac{1300}{1300} = 1.0000
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
\text{CR}_Y = 1.00 \;>\; \text{CR}_X = 0.8667
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** **Supplier Y** is selected (CR = 1.00). Even though Supplier X had a higher purchase price, its *total ownership cost* is lower than X. The Cost-Ratio Method correctly captures hidden costs of poor quality.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strengths & Limitations of Each Method

| Method | Strengths | Limitations | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Point** | Simple, transparent, multi-criteria | Subjective weights; linear assumption | Standard procurement with 3–7 suppliers |
| **Cost-Ratio** | Reveals hidden TCO | Requires accurate cost data | High-value strategic items |
| **AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process)** | Handles complex trade-offs, consistency check | Mathematically complex, pairwise comparisons | Multi-decision-maker environments |
| **Vendor Rating Index** | Quantitative, repeatable | Requires historical data | Established long-term suppliers |
| **Categorical Method** | Very fast | Lacks precision | Low-value, low-risk items |

---

## 3.4 KTU Common Pitfalls in Numerical Problems

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 numerical mistakes students make in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** so they sum to 1.00.
> 2. **Confusing TCO with purchase price** — TCO includes inspection, failure, and downtime.
> 3. **Inverting the Cost-Ratio** — always divide lowest cost by the supplier's cost, never the reverse.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define supplier selection. List any four criteria used for evaluating suppliers.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Supplier selection is the process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing external vendors who can provide materials, components, or services of the required quality, at acceptable cost, and on time, in accordance with ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4.

**Four Criteria:**
1. **Quality** — Conformance to specifications and standards.
2. **Price / Cost** — Total cost of ownership, not just purchase price.
3. **Delivery Performance** — On-time delivery reliability.
4. **Technical Capability** — Manufacturing capacity, R&D, and process capability.

*(Any four relevant criteria accepted — also valid: financial stability, past performance, communication, location, ISO certification.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the 1-10-100 Rule in supplier quality management.**

**Model Answer:**

The **1-10-100 Rule** states that the cost of fixing a defect increases by a factor of **10** as it moves further downstream in the supply chain:

| Stage | Cost Multiplier |
|---|---|
| Prevention at supplier | **1 ×** |
| Detection at incoming inspection | **10 ×** |
| Detection during production | **100 ×** |
| Detection after customer delivery | **1,000 ×** |

> **Significance:** This rule justifies the TQM emphasis on prevention, supplier partnership, and zero-defect sourcing rather than downstream inspection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

### Question A
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Weighted Point Method** of supplier selection, with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the strategic considerations in choosing **single vs multiple suppliers**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution (a) — Weighted Point Method

**Definition:** The Weighted Point Method is a quantitative multi-criteria decision technique where each supplier is scored on several criteria, each criterion is assigned a relative weight reflecting its importance, and a weighted total is computed. The supplier with the **highest total weighted score** is selected.

**Formula:**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

Where $S_i$ = score of supplier $i$, $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$, with the constraint $\sum w_j = 1$.

**Procedure (8 Steps):**

1. Identify the criteria for evaluation (e.g., quality, cost, delivery, service).
2. Assign weights $w_j$ to each criterion such that $\sum w_j = 1$.
3. Develop a rating scale (commonly 0–10).
4. Rate each supplier on each criterion.
5. Multiply each rating by its weight to get weighted scores.
6. Sum the weighted scores for each supplier.
7. Rank suppliers by total score.
8. Select the supplier with the highest score.

**Worked Example:**

A company evaluates three suppliers on Quality (weight 0.5), Cost (weight 0.3), and Delivery (weight 0.2). Ratings (out of 10):

| Supplier | Quality | Cost | Delivery |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| B | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| C | 9 | 6 | 7 |

$$
S_A = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 6) = 4.0 + 2.1 + 1.2 = 7.3
$$

$$
S_B = (0.5 \times 7) + (0.3 \times 9) + (0.2 \times 8) = 3.5 + 2.7 + 1.6 = 7.8
$$

$$
S_C = (0.5 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 6) + (0.2 \times 7) = 4.5 + 1.8 + 1.4 = 7.7
$$

> **Supplier B is selected** with the highest score of 7.8.

---

### Model Solution (b) — Single vs Multiple Suppliers

**Single Supplier (Sole Sourcing):**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Strong, long-term partnership | High dependency risk |
| Volume discounts possible | Loss of bargaining power |
| Lower administrative overhead | Supply disruption if vendor fails |
| Easier communication & quality alignment | Limited innovation exposure |

**Multiple Suppliers:**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Reduced supply risk | Higher administrative cost |
| Competitive pricing | Weaker partnerships |
| Continuous supply even if one fails | Quality variability |
| Access to diverse expertise | Loss of volume benefits |

**Strategic Recommendations:**

- **Single supplier** is preferred for **strategic, high-value, JIT-driven** items where deep partnership is essential (e.g., Toyota's keiretsu).
- **Multiple suppliers** are preferred for **commodity, non-critical** items where continuity is vital.
- A **hybrid approach (dual sourcing)** is commonly used in engineering industries to balance the trade-offs.

> **TQM Implication:** Deming emphasized *single, loyal suppliers* for quality, while modern supply chain theory favors *resilience through multiple suppliers* — a balance is the practical answer.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024]`

### Question B
**(a)** Explain the **concept of Vendor Rating** and describe any **five parameters** used for vendor rating. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Cost-Ratio Method**? Compute the Cost-Ratio for the following two suppliers and recommend the better one. **(7 Marks)**

| Cost Item | Supplier P (₹) | Supplier Q (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase cost | 500 | 480 |
| Inspection cost | 30 | 50 |
| Operating cost | 80 | 100 |
| Failure cost | 60 | 20 |
| Downtime cost | 30 | 50 |

---

### Model Solution (a) — Vendor Rating

**Definition:** Vendor Rating is a systematic, quantitative evaluation of a supplier's overall performance based on multiple parameters over a defined period. It is used to decide **order placement, allocation of purchase volume, and re-qualification** of the supplier.

**Five Parameters:**

1. **Quality Rating** — Percentage of lots accepted without rejection.
2. **Delivery Rating** — Percentage of orders delivered on or before the due date.
3. **Price Rating** — Comparative price competitiveness vs market.
4. **Service Rating** — Responsiveness to complaints, technical support, communication.
5. **Financial Stability Rating** — Ability to sustain operations during economic downturns.

**Vendor Rating Index Formula:**

$$
\text{VRI} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_j}{\sum w_j}
$$

Where $w_j$ is the weight and $r_j$ is the rating on parameter $j$. The supplier with the highest VRI is rated the best.

---

### Model Solution (b) — Cost-Ratio Method Computation

**Step 1 — Compute Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):**

$$
\text{TCO}_P = 500 + 30 + 80 + 60 + 30 = 700 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{TCO}_Q = 480 + 50 + 100 + 20 + 50 = 700 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interesting case — both have equal TCO! So we proceed with the Cost-Ratio using TCO.

**Step 2 — Identify Lowest Cost:**

$$
L_c = \min(700, 700) = 700
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cost-Ratio Formula:**

$$
\text{CR}_P = \dfrac{L_c}{\text{TCO}_P} = \dfrac{700}{700} = 1.000
$$

$$
\text{CR}_Q = \dfrac{L_c}{\text{TCO}_Q} = \dfrac{700}{700} = 1.000
$$

**Step 4 — Tie-Breaker Using Individual Quality Components:**

> Since both suppliers have equal TCO, examine the **quality dimension** of TCO:

- Supplier P: Inspection (₹30) + Failure (₹60) = **₹90** in quality-related costs.
- Supplier Q: Inspection (₹50) + Failure (₹20) = **₹70** in quality-related costs.

> **Recommendation:** **Supplier Q** is preferred because it incurs **lower quality-related costs (₹70 vs ₹90)** within an identical TCO. This reflects superior supplier quality capability.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Supplier Selection questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Not showing the weight normalization check** — always display $\sum w_j = 1$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing weighted average with weighted sum** — show formula substitution, not just final number.
> 3. **Forgetting to list both advantages AND disadvantages** in single vs multiple supplier questions (valuation key mandates both columns).
> 4. **Skipping the recommendation sentence** — even in numerical problems, end with a clear *"Supplier X is recommended because..."* sentence.
> 5. **Ignoring ISO 9001 reference** — examiners award bonus marks for connecting the answer to Clause 8.4.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Supplier Selection (Module 2)**

- **Supplier Selection** = identifying, evaluating, choosing external vendors meeting quality, cost, delivery, and service criteria.
- **Strategic Decision** under TQM, governed by **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4**.
- **Eight-step process**: Requirement → Criteria → Identification → Pre-qualification → RFQ → Evaluation → Selection → Monitoring.
- **Number of suppliers**: Single (partnership, JIT) vs Multiple (risk diversification) vs Dual (balanced).
- **Weighted Point Method** formula: $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$; weights must sum to **1.00**.
- **Cost-Ratio Method** formula: $\text{CR} = L_c / H_c$; highest CR selected.
- **TCO** = Purchase + Inspection + Operating + Failure + Downtime costs.
- **Vendor Rating Index** = $\sum w_j r_j / \sum w_j$.
- **1-10-100 Rule** cost multipliers: **1 → 10 → 100 → 1000** as defects move downstream.
- **Criteria hierarchy** in order of typical importance: Quality > Cost > Delivery > Service > Other.
- **Continuous monitoring** through PDCA cycle — supplier evaluation is **not a one-time activity**.
- **Deming's view**: prefer long-term, single, loyal supplier relationships for quality.
- **Kraljic Matrix** is often used to classify items as Strategic / Leverage / Bottleneck / Non-critical.
- **Key valuation tricks**: always normalize weights, show formula substitution, end with a recommendation sentence.

> **End of Notes — Supplier Selection | PEEIT754 Total Quality Management | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplier Rating (Vendor Rating)** is a **systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation methodology** used by purchasing organizations to assess, classify, and rank the performance of their suppliers/vendors against a predefined set of performance criteria, with the ultimate objective of ensuring incoming material quality, minimizing supply chain risk, and supporting strategic sourcing decisions.

In the KTU 2024 TQM framework, supplier rating is positioned as a **critical pillar of Incoming Quality Control (IQC)** and is directly linked to the philosophy of **Partnering with Suppliers** (one of the pillars of TQM as per the KTU syllabus). It transforms subjective supplier perception into **objective, data-driven decision support**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hiring a cook for a restaurant:
- You don't just taste one dish — you evaluate **taste, hygiene, punctuality, cost, and reliability** over time.
- You assign **different importance** to each factor (taste might matter more than presentation).
- Finally, you **score** each cook numerically and pick the best.

**Supplier Rating works exactly the same way!**
- The "cook" is the **supplier/vendor**.
- The "evaluation criteria" are **Quality, Price, Delivery, Service**.
- The "scoring system" is the **Weighted Point Method**.
- The "hiring decision" is the **Make-or-Buy / Source Selection Decision**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Supplier Rating falls under the broader topic of *Supplier Management* and is a pre-requisite concept for advanced topics like *Supplier Partnership, JIT Supply, and Supply Chain Quality Management*.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following key metrics are universally applied in supplier rating (all must be in **bold** as per protocol):

- **Quality (Q)** — Measured as **% Defect Rate** or **Acceptance Rate** of incoming lots.
- **Delivery (D)** — Measured as **% On-Time Delivery (OTD)**.
- **Price (P)** — Measured as **Price Competitiveness Index** or **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**.
- **Service (S)** — Measured through **Responsiveness Score** and **Warranty Support Rating**.
- **Capacity (C)** — Measured as **% Capacity Utilization** of the supplier.

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Standard:** Most KTU-evaluated frameworks use **5 to 7 criteria**, with **Quality + Delivery** carrying the highest combined weight (typically **60–70%** of the total).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories / Types of Supplier Rating

Supplier Rating is broadly classified into **three hierarchical levels** in the KTU TQM syllabus:

### 2.1.1 Vendor Rating (VR) — The Basic Level
A **simple, single-criterion or limited-criterion** evaluation, primarily focused on **Quality and Delivery performance** of past supplies. It is the *first line* of supplier evaluation.

### 2.1.2 Vendor Analysis (VA) — The Advanced Level
A **comprehensive, multi-criteria evaluation** that incorporates **cost analysis, financial health, technical capability, and strategic fit**. It is used for **make-or-buy decisions** and long-term supplier partnerships.

### 2.1.3 Vendor Rating vs. Vendor Analysis (Comparison)

| Parameter | Vendor Rating | Vendor Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single/dual criteria | Multi-criteria (5+ parameters) |
| **Purpose** | Performance monitoring | Strategic sourcing & partnership |
| **Frequency** | Continuous / periodic | Annual or quarterly |
| **Output** | Numerical score/rank | Comprehensive supplier profile |
| **Decision Support** | Operational | Strategic (make-or-buy) |

## 2.2 Methods of Vendor Rating

### Method 1: Categorical (Ranking) Method
Suppliers are classified into broad categories based on subjective judgment:
- **Excellent** (Score 9–10)
- **Good** (Score 7–8)
- **Average** (Score 5–6)
- **Poor** (Score 3–4)
- **Unacceptable** (Score 1–2)

> *Limitation:* Highly subjective; not preferred for KTU numerical problems.

### Method 2: Weighted Point Method (Most KTU-Relevant)
Each criterion is assigned a **weight (wᵢ)** based on its strategic importance. The supplier is rated on a **scale (e.g., 1–10)** for each criterion. The **Weighted Score** is calculated as:

### Method 3: Cost-Based Rating
Focuses purely on **price analysis** — comparing quoted prices against market benchmarks, historical prices, or standard cost estimates.

### Method 4: Matrix Method
A **Quality vs. Price 2×2 matrix** (or similar) is plotted to visually classify suppliers into quadrants:
- **Star Supplier:** High Quality + Low Price
- **Strategic Partner:** High Quality + High Price
- **Marginal:** Low Quality + Low Price
- **Reject:** Low Quality + High Price

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | **Weighted Score** of supplier $i$ | Where $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$ | **Weight Normalization** (or 100%) | Sum of all criterion weights must equal 1 (or 100%) |
| 3 | $\text{Quality Index} = \dfrac{\text{Accepted Units}}{\text{Inspected Units}} \times 100$ | **Acceptance Rate %** | Incoming Quality performance |
| 4 | $\text{Defect Rate \%} = \dfrac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | **Defect Rate** | Inverse of Quality Index |
| 5 | $\text{OTD \%} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | **On-Time Delivery %** | Delivery reliability |
| 6 | $\text{Normalized Rating} = \dfrac{r_{ij}}{\max(r_{ij})}$ | **Normalization (0 to 1)** | Standardizing ratings across criteria |
| 7 | $\text{Category Rank} = \text{Rank}(S_i)$ | **Supplier Ranking** | Sorting suppliers by weighted score |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Weights $w_j$ are non-negative and must sum to 1. Ratings $r_{ij}$ are typically bounded in the interval $[0, 10]$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive Industry:** ISO/TS 16949 (now IATF 16949) mandates systematic supplier rating for all Tier-1 suppliers.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100 requires supplier scorecards combining **Quality, Delivery, and Cost**.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Apple's supplier rating system famously scores components on **PPM (Parts Per Million) defects** and **on-time shipment rates**.
- **Healthcare / Pharma:** GMP regulations require **approved vendor lists** maintained through formal rating systems.
- **Defense Engineering:** DRDO and BEL use formal vendor rating for procurement under **Make in India** initiatives.

## 2.5 The Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why assign weights?** Different criteria have different strategic importance. A **Quality-critical** industry (e.g., pharma) gives higher weight to Quality than Delivery.
- **Why use a scale (1–10)?** Standardization enables **mathematical aggregation** and **comparability** across suppliers and time periods.
- **Why sum weights to 1?** Ensures the final score is **dimensionless and bounded** for easy interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation: Weighted Point Score

We define the **Weighted Score (Sᵢ)** of supplier *i* as the scalar projection of the supplier's rating vector onto the criterion weight vector.

Let:
- $n$ = total number of evaluation criteria
- $w_j$ = weight assigned to criterion $j$ (such that $w_j \ge 0$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$)
- $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ (typically $r_{ij} \in [0, 10]$)

Then the **Weighted Score** is derived as:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Step 1 — Establish the rating matrix** of dimension $m \times n$ for $m$ suppliers and $n$ criteria:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1n} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{m1} & r_{m2} & \cdots & r_{mn} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the weight vector** of dimension $n \times 1$:

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the score vector** $S$ of dimension $m \times 1$ via matrix multiplication:

$$
S = R \cdot W = \begin{bmatrix} \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{1j} w_j \\ \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{2j} w_j \\ \vdots \\ \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{mj} w_j \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_1 \\ S_2 \\ \vdots \\ S_m \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Rank the suppliers** in descending order of $S_i$. The supplier with the **highest weighted score** is the preferred vendor.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing company wants to rate 3 suppliers (A, B, C) on **4 criteria** with the following weights and ratings (on a scale of 1–10):

| Criterion | Weight (wⱼ) | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Price | 0.20 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Service | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Validate the weight constraint:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

The sum equals 1, satisfying the normalization condition.

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier A:**

$$
S_A = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_A = 3.60 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 0.90
$$

$$
S_A = 7.85
$$

This represents the contribution of Quality (3.60), Delivery (1.75), Price (1.60), and Service (0.90) to the final aggregated score.

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier B:**

$$
S_B = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_B = 3.20 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20
$$

$$
S_B = 8.05
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier C:**

$$
S_C = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_C = 2.80 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.05
$$

$$
S_C = 7.65
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the suppliers in descending order of $S_i$:**

$$
S_B (8.05) > S_A (7.85) > S_C (7.65)
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** **Supplier B** is selected as the preferred vendor.

## 3.3 Normalized Weighted Score Derivation (Alternative Method)

When criteria are measured on **different scales** (e.g., Quality in %, Delivery in days), normalization is required before weighting.

Define the **normalized rating** as:

$$
\hat{r}_{ij} = \frac{r_{ij} - r_{j}^{\min}}{r_{j}^{\max} - r_{j}^{\min}}
$$

where $r_{j}^{\min}$ and $r_{j}^{\max}$ are the minimum and maximum ratings across all suppliers for criterion $j$. The **normalized score** becomes:

$$
\hat{S}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \hat{r}_{ij}, \quad \hat{S}_i \in [0, 1]
$$

This **min-max normalization** ensures all criteria contribute on a **uniform 0-to-1 scale**, eliminating scale bias.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Supplier Rating Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Sourcing Need] --> B[Identify Evaluation Criteria]
    B --> C[Assign Weights to Criteria]
    C --> D{Weights Sum = 1?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Collect Supplier Performance Data]
    E --> F[Rate Each Supplier on Scale 1-10]
    F --> G[Construct Rating Matrix R]
    G --> H[Compute Weighted Score Si]
    H --> I[Rank Suppliers]
    I --> J{Best Supplier Identified?}
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Generate Supplier Scorecard]
    K --> L[Decision: Approve / Reject / Develop]
    L --> M[Monitor and Review Periodically]
    M --> E
```

## 4.2 Vendor Rating vs. Vendor Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supplier Evaluation Triggered] --> B{Strategic or Operational Decision?}
    B -- Operational --> C[Use Vendor Rating]
    B -- Strategic --> D[Use Vendor Analysis]
    C --> C1[Criteria: Quality + Delivery]
    C1 --> C2[Output: Simple Score and Rank]
    D --> D1[Criteria: Quality, Cost, Delivery, Service, Finance, Tech]
    D1 --> D2[Output: Comprehensive Supplier Profile]
    D2 --> D3{Supports Make or Buy Decision?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Proceed to Make or Buy Analysis]
    D3 -- No --> D5[Periodic Strategic Review]
```

## 4.3 Supplier Selection Matrix (Quality vs. Price Quadrant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1 [High Quality, Low Price]
        Q1N[STAR SUPPLIER: Preferred Partner]
    end
    subgraph Q2 [High Quality, High Price]
        Q2N[PREMIUM SUPPLIER: Negotiate Volume Discounts]
    end
    subgraph Q3 [Low Quality, Low Price]
        Q3N[MARGINAL SUPPLIER: Develop or Drop]
    end
    subgraph Q4 [Low Quality, High Price]
        Q4N[REJECT: Disqualify Immediately]
    end
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Supplier Rating System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer [INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Quality Inspection Data]
        I2[Delivery Logs]
        I3[Purchase Price Records]
        I4[Service Feedback]
    end
    subgraph ProcessLayer [PROCESSING LAYER]
        P1[Data Validation and Normalization]
        P2[Weight Assignment Engine]
```
