```mermaid
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer [OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[Supplier Scorecard]
        O2[Approved Vendor List]
        O3[Supplier Development Plan]
        O4[Strategic Sourcing Report]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Vendor Rating. List any four criteria used for vendor rating.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Vendor rating is a **systematic evaluation technique** used to assess and classify suppliers based on their performance against predefined criteria. It enables organizations to make **objective, data-driven sourcing decisions** and maintain a reliable supply base.

**Four Criteria for Vendor Rating:**
1. **Quality** — Conformance of supplied materials to specifications (measured via % acceptance or PPM defects).
2. **Delivery** — Ability to deliver on the promised date (measured via On-Time Delivery %).
3. **Price** — Competitiveness of quoted price relative to market benchmarks.
4. **Service** — Post-sale support, warranty handling, and responsiveness to complaints.

> Additional criteria may include financial stability, technical capability, and capacity.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Vendor Rating and Vendor Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Vendor Rating | Vendor Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Limited criteria (1–2 parameters) | Comprehensive multi-criteria evaluation |
| 2 | Used for routine performance monitoring | Used for strategic decisions (make-or-buy) |
| 3 | Simple numerical score | Detailed supplier profile and report |
| 4 | Short-term, transaction-level focus | Long-term, relationship-level focus |
| 5 | Less data-intensive | Requires financial, technical, and market data |

> Vendor Rating is a **subset** of Vendor Analysis in terms of scope and depth.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Weighted Point Method** of vendor rating in detail. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company wants to rate **4 suppliers (P, Q, R, S)** based on **3 criteria** as given below. Compute the weighted score and rank the suppliers. **(7 Marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | P | Q | R | S |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.50 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Delivery | 0.30 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 8 |
| Price | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 9 |

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Weighted Point Method (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Weighted Point Method is a **multi-criteria decision-making technique** where each evaluation criterion is assigned a relative weight based on its strategic importance, and each supplier is rated on a numerical scale (typically 1–10) for every criterion. The final score is the **weighted sum** of all ratings.

**Steps of the Method (3 Marks):**
1. **Identify criteria** relevant to the purchasing decision (e.g., Quality, Delivery, Price, Service).
2. **Assign weights** ($w_j$) to each criterion such that $\sum w_j = 1$.
3. **Rate each supplier** ($r_{ij}$) on a uniform scale (1–10).
4. **Compute weighted score:** $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$.
5. **Rank suppliers** in descending order of $S_i$.

**Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Objective, quantitative, and free from personal bias.
- Allows multiple criteria to be considered simultaneously.
- Easy to implement and audit.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Subjectivity in weight assignment.
- Assumes linear trade-offs between criteria.
- May not capture qualitative factors like trust and culture.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Validate weights:** $0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \checkmark$

**Step 2 — Score for Supplier P:**

$$
S_P = (0.50 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_P = 4.00 + 2.10 + 1.80 = 7.90
$$

**Step 3 — Score for Supplier Q:**

$$
S_Q = (0.50 \times 7) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_Q = 3.50 + 2.70 + 1.60 = 7.80
$$

**Step 4 — Score for Supplier R:**

$$
S_R = (0.50 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_R = 4.50 + 2.40 + 1.40 = 8.30
$$

**Step 5 — Score for Supplier S:**

$$
S_S = (0.50 \times 6) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_S = 3.00 + 2.40 + 1.80 = 7.20
$$

**Step 6 — Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Supplier | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **R** | **8.30** |
| 2 | P | 7.90 |
| 3 | Q | 7.80 |
| 4 | S | 7.20 |

**Decision:** **Supplier R** is selected as the preferred vendor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **Categorical Method** and **Matrix Method** of vendor rating. Compare them with the Weighted Point Method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two suppliers, **X and Y**, have supplied 1000 and 800 components respectively in a quarter. Out of these, X had 40 defects and Y had 20 defects. Both delivered on time for all orders. Using a **Weighted Point Method** with weights **Quality = 0.6, Delivery = 0.3, Price = 0.1**, and a uniform rating scale of **(Acceptance Rate %, OTD %, Price Score 1–10)**, recommend the preferred supplier. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Categorical and Matrix Methods (7 Marks)

**Categorical Method (2 Marks):**
Suppliers are classified into broad qualitative bands such as **Excellent, Good, Average, Poor, Unacceptable** based on overall performance. It is **simple, fast, and low-cost**, but highly **subjective** and unsuitable for complex multi-criteria decisions.

**Matrix Method (2 Marks):**
A 2D or 3D matrix plots suppliers on key dimensions (e.g., Quality vs. Price). Suppliers falling in the **"High Quality, Low Price"** quadrant are preferred. It provides a **visual decision aid** but is limited to 2–3 criteria.

**Comparison with Weighted Point Method (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Categorical | Matrix | Weighted Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| Criteria count | All-in-one | 2–3 | Unlimited |
| Objectivity | Low | Medium | High |
| Numerical output | No | No | Yes |
| Scalability | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU Exam relevance | Low | Medium | **Very High** |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Quality (Acceptance Rate %):**

For Supplier X:

$$
\text{Quality}_X = \frac{1000 - 40}{1000} \times 100 = 96.0\%
$$

For Supplier Y:

$$
\text{Quality}_Y = \frac{800 - 20}{800} \times 100 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 2 — Delivery (OTD %):** Both = **100%**

**Step 3 — Convert Quality % to a 1–10 scale** (linear: 100% → 10):

$$
r_{Q,X} = \frac{96.0}{10} = 9.6
$$

$$
r_{Q,Y} = \frac{97.5}{10} = 9.75
$$

**Step 4 — Assume Price Score** (assume both quoted same price → rating 8 for both):
$r_{P,X} = r_{P,Y} = 8$

**Step 5 — Compute Weighted Score for X:**

$$
S_X = (0.6 \times 9.6) + (0.3 \times 10) + (0.1 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_X = 5.76 + 3.00 + 0.80 = 9.56
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Weighted Score for Y:**

$$
S_Y = (0.6 \times 9.75) + (0.3 \times 10) + (0.1 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_Y = 5.85 + 3.00 + 0.80 = 9.65
$$

**Step 7 — Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Supplier | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Y** | **9.65** |
| 2 | X | 9.56 |

**Decision:** **Supplier Y** is recommended due to higher acceptance rate (97.5% vs. 96.0%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to validate that weights sum to 1. (Penalty: **0.5 to 1 mark**)
> - **Always** show the substitution step in the formula before the final answer. Writing only the final score invites **partial marking**.
> - **Units matter:** If Quality is given in %, convert it to the rating scale **before** multiplying by weight.
> - **Do not** confuse Vendor Rating (simple) with Vendor Analysis (comprehensive) in 2-mark definition questions.
> - For the Matrix Method, **memorize the 4 quadrants** — examiners frequently ask "which quadrant is the preferred supplier?"

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Supplier Rating** is the quantitative evaluation of suppliers against predefined criteria.
- The **Weighted Point Method** is the most exam-relevant technique in KTU 2024.
- **Core formula:** $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$
- **Weight constraint:** $\sum w_j = 1$ (must always be verified).
- **Common criteria:** Quality, Delivery, Price, Service (memorize the order of importance).
- **Vendor Rating** = limited criteria, operational; **Vendor Analysis** = comprehensive, strategic.
- **Categorical Method** = subjective bands; **Matrix Method** = visual 2D plot; **Weighted Point** = numerical aggregation.
- **Quality Index** = (Accepted / Inspected) × 100; **OTD %** = (On-time Orders / Total Orders) × 100.
- **Normalization** may be required when criteria are on different scales.
- **Matrix Quadrants:** High Quality + Low Price = STAR supplier (preferred).
- **Decision Rule:** Supplier with the **highest weighted score** is selected.
- Always **rank suppliers** in descending order in the final table for full marks.
- Supplier rating is a **continuous process** — it must be **monitored and reviewed periodically**, not done once.
- **Key real-world link:** IATF 16949 (Automotive), AS9100 (Aerospace), and ISO 9001 mandate supplier rating systems.
- **KTU focus areas:** Definition (2 marks), Comparison table (3 marks), Numerical Weighted Point Method (7 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3 — Core "Concepts" in Total Quality Management (TQM)

Module 3 of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEEIT754 — Total Quality Management** focuses on advanced, structured, and mathematically rigorous *quality concepts* that organizations deploy after they have adopted the basic TQM philosophy. These concepts move TQM from being a "philosophy of doing things right" to a **measurable, data-driven engineering discipline**.

The four pillars (concepts) of Module 3 are:

1. **Benchmarking**
2. **Six Sigma (6σ)**
3. **Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality**
4. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Benchmarking — process, types; Six Sigma concepts, methodology; Quality Function Deployment; FMEA — concept, process, RPN."*
> These four topics are *guaranteed* to appear in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) every academic cycle, either as Part A (3-mark) definitions or as Part B (14-mark) analytical questions.

---

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

### A. Benchmarking
**Benchmarking** is the *continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class organizations, internal units, or industry leaders*, with the explicit goal of identifying performance gaps and importing superior practices to close them.

- First popularized by **Rank Xerox (Xerox Corporation)** in 1979, when it benchmarked against Canon and Fuji to recover from competitive collapse.
- Codified by **Robert C. Camp** in his book *"Benchmarking: The Search for Industry Best Practices That Lead to Superior Performance"* (1989).

### B. Six Sigma (6σ)
**Six Sigma** is a *disciplined, data-driven, statistical methodology* that seeks to eliminate defects in any process by identifying and removing the root causes of variation, targeting a process performance level of no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

- Pioneered at **Motorola** in **1986** by engineer **Bill Smith**, championed by CEO **Bob Galvin**.
- Popularized globally by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)**, where it reportedly saved over **\$12 billion** in five years.
- Statistical foundation: process must achieve **±6σ** spread between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.

### C. Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a *structured planning and product development framework* that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable technical and engineering requirements throughout the design and production stages.

- Invented in Japan by **Yoji Akao** in **1966**; first major deployment by **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** at the Kobe shipyard in **1972**.
- The central artefact is the **House of Quality (HoQ)** — a matrix that links *"Whats"* (customer demands) with *"Hows"* (engineering characteristics).

### D. Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
**FMEA** is a *step-by-step, proactive analytical technique* used to identify potential failure modes in a product, process, or design, evaluate their severity, occurrence, and detectability, and prioritize corrective actions through a numerical metric called the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

- Developed by the **U.S. Military** as **MIL-STD-1629** in **1949**.
- Now standardized globally as **IEC 60812** and adopted by **AIAG / VDA** in the automotive industry.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Real-World Analogy: Planning the Perfect Birthday Party**

Imagine you are throwing a birthday party. The four TQM concepts act like different planning tools:

| Concept | Birthday Analogy | What It Really Does |
|---|---|---|
| **Benchmarking** | You visit three friends' parties, take notes on the best games, food, and music, and copy/improve those ideas for your own party. | Learn from the *best-in-class* to raise your standard. |
| **Six Sigma** | You decide the cake should be perfect **3.4 times per million slices** — meaning almost no guest ever gets a bad slice. | Drive variation toward zero, target near-perfect quality. |
| **QFD** | You write a list of what guests *want* (chocolate, music, games) and convert it into a *shopping list* (2 kg cocoa, 1 speaker, 5 game props). | Translate customer "Whats" into technical "Hows." |
| **FMEA** | Before the party, you list everything that could go wrong (rain → no outdoor games; power cut → no music) and rank them by danger to fix the worst first. | Predict failures, prevent them before they occur. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Constants & Universal Metrics (Bold)

- **Six Sigma standard DPMO target = 3.4 defects per million opportunities.**
- **FMEA Severity, Occurrence, Detectability scale = 1 to 10** (1 = best, 10 = worst).
- **RPN = S × O × D**, maximum possible value = **10 × 10 × 10 = 1000**.
- **Six Sigma process spread = 12σ** total (from −6σ to +6σ), with specification limits set at **±6σ** from the mean.
- **QFD Matrix (House of Quality)** has approximately **6 standard rooms/blocks**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Benchmarking: Theoretical Framework

Benchmarking is a **4-phase closed-loop process** (often called the *Camp's 10-step Benchmarking Model* simplified for KTU answers).

### The 4-Step Logic
1. **Plan:** Identify what to benchmark (a function, product, or process) and choose the benchmarking partner (best-in-class organization).
2. **Collect:** Gather performance data using site visits, surveys, public databases, reverse engineering, or third-party reports.
3. **Analyze:** Compute the **performance gap** between your organization and the benchmark; perform *root-cause analysis* of the gap.
4. **Adapt & Improve:** Implement changes, monitor, and *re-benchmark* periodically — benchmarking is a **continuous cycle**, not a one-time event.

### Types of Benchmarking (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "List the types of benchmarking" is a recurring **3-mark Part A question**. Memorize the *four types* below.

| # | Type | Benchmark Against | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Internal** | Other units/departments within the *same* company | Easy data access, low cost |
| 2 | **Competitive** | *Direct rivals* in the same industry | Highly relevant; legally sensitive |
| 3 | **Functional / Generic** | Best-in-class in the *same function* (but different industry) | Wide idea pool; e.g., hospitals benchmarking hotels for admission |
| 4 | **Strategic** | Industry *leaders* on long-term strategies | Future-oriented |

### Pitfalls of Benchmarking (Often asked as Part B sub-question)
- **Pitfall 1 — Copying without understanding:** Xerox's lesson: copy principles, not just results.
- **Pitfall 2 — Tunnel vision:** Focusing only on metrics, ignoring culture.
- **Pitfall 3 — Static benchmarking:** Benchmarking once and stopping. Must be **continuous**.
- **Pitfall 4 — Comparing apples to oranges.**

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Six Sigma (6σ): Statistical Theory

### 2.2.1 The Bell Curve Logic

In any process, outputs naturally vary. In a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution**, the mean (μ) is at the center, and standard deviation (σ) measures spread.

- **±1σ** = ~68.27% of data inside
- **±2σ** = ~95.45% of data inside
- **±3σ** = ~99.73% of data inside
- **±6σ** = 99.9999998% of data inside (Six Sigma quality)

### 2.2.2 The DMAIC Methodology

Six Sigma projects follow the **DMAIC** roadmap (pronounced *duh-MAY-ick*):

| Phase | Name | Purpose | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, customer, project scope | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| **M** | **Measure** | Quantify the current process capability | Control charts, MSA, DPMO |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Find the *root cause* of defects | Pareto, Fishbone (Ishikawa), 5-Why, Regression |
| **I** | **Improve** | Implement solutions to fix root causes | DOE, Pilot runs, Poka-Yoke |
| **C** | **Control** | Sustain the improvements | Control plans, SOPs, SPC |

### 2.2.3 Critical Formula — Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Number of Units Produced} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

### 2.2.4 Critical Formula — Process Capability (Cp and Cpk)

The **process capability index** measures how well a process fits within specification limits.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$

For **Six Sigma performance**, **Cpk ≥ 2.0** (industry rule) and ideally **Cpk = 2.0** with a **1.5σ shift** built into the calculation.

### 2.2.5 The 1.5σ Shift — Why 3.4 DPMO?

Motorola discovered that real processes *drift* by an average of **1.5σ** over time due to wear, calibration, human factors, etc. So the *actual* defect rate is computed assuming the mean shifts by 1.5σ toward the nearest spec limit. The result is **3.4 DPMO** instead of the theoretical 0.002 DPMO.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

### 2.3.1 The House of Quality (HoQ) — Six Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Students must draw the **House of Quality diagram** and explain each of the six rooms. This appears every cycle.

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — left wall (Voice of Customer, prioritized).
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — ceiling (engineering parameters).
3. **Relationship Matrix (Roof interior)** — center (strong / medium / weak correlations between Whats and Hows, marked as ●, ◐, ○).
4. **Technical Correlations (Roof triangle)** — top (synergies/conflicts among Hows, e.g., ◊ strong positive, ▲ strong negative).
5. **Competitive Assessment** — right side and bottom (how competitors perform on each What and How).
6. **Technical Importance & Targets** — outputs (ranked engineering priorities, target values).

### 2.3.2 Step Logic

1. Gather **VOC** (Voice of Customer) → convert to *Whats*.
2. Prioritize Whats using a **1–5 scale**.
3. List *Hows* (technical descriptors) on the ceiling.
4. Fill the **inter-relationship matrix** (●◐○).
5. Compute **technical importance rating** for each How:
   $$\text{Tech Importance}_j = \sum_i (\text{Customer Importance}_i \times \text{Relationship}_{ij})$$
6. Evaluate competitors on each What and How.
7. Select targets for each How.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

### 2.4.1 The FMEA Logic

FMEA is performed in **7 steps**:

1. Assemble a cross-functional team.
2. Identify the *scope* (system / sub-system / component).
3. List all **failure modes** for each component.
4. List the **effects** (consequences) of each failure.
5. List the **causes** of each failure.
6. Assign **S, O, D scores (1–10 each)**.
7. Compute **RPN = S × O × D** and prioritize corrective actions.

> [!NOTE]
> **RPN Thresholds (Industry Standard):**
> - RPN > 100 → Mandatory corrective action
> - S ≥ 9 → High severity, must address even if RPN is low
> - S × O > 30 → Investigate irrespective of D

### 2.4.2 FMEA Scales (Standard)

| Score | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detectability (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | Very unlikely (≈1 in 1,000,000) | Almost certain to detect |
| 5 | Moderate | Occasional (≈1 in 100) | Moderate detection |
| 8 | High, loss of function | Frequent (≈1 in 50) | Low detection |
| 10 | Catastrophic, safety hazard | Very high (≈1 in 2) | Impossible / no control |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Memorize this table — at least one formula will appear in every KTU ESE paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million |
| 2 | Yield (Rolled Throughput Yield) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | % (0–100%) |
| 3 | First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | % |
| 4 | Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Unitless |
| 5 | Process Capability (Shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Unitless |
| 6 | Sigma Level | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{10^6} \rightarrow \text{via yield tables}$ | σ units |
| 7 | Technical Importance (QFD) | $\text{TI}_j = \sum_i (\text{CI}_i \times R_{ij})$ | Weighted score |
| 8 | RPN (FMEA) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | 1 – 1000 |
| 9 | Severity Score | $S \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Occurrence Score | $O \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Detectability Score | $D \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Performance Gap (Benchmarking) | $\text{Gap} = \text{Our Performance} - \text{Best-in-Class}$ | Same metric unit |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Six Sigma** is used by **Texas Instruments, Amazon, Honeywell, Boeing, Ford** to reduce manufacturing defects. **Amazon's "Working Backwards" Six Sigma** reduced order errors by 26%.
- **Benchmarking** powers India's **AS9100** aerospace certification by comparing against global primes (Boeing, Airbus).
- **QFD** was used by **Toyota** to engineer the Lexus LS400 from scratch in 1989.
- **FMEA** is mandatory in **ISO 9001:2015**, **IATF 16949** (automotive), and **AS9100D** (aerospace) certified organizations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the *exhaustive mathematical workings* for the four core Module 3 concepts. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivations: Six Sigma Quantitative Framework

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Relationship Between Process Standard Deviation and Tolerance

Consider a process with:
- Mean = **μ**
- Standard deviation = **σ**
- Upper Specification Limit = **USL**
- Lower Specification Limit = **LSL**

For the process to produce **zero defects**, every output must lie within [LSL, USL]. The width of the specification window is:

$$\text{Tolerance Width} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

For a normally distributed process, **99.9999998%** of outputs lie within **±6σ** of the mean, i.e., a total spread of **12σ**.

To fit this natural process spread within the specification window:

$$12\sigma \le \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

Dividing both sides by 12:

$$\sigma \le \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{12}$$

This is the **maximum allowable standard deviation** for a Six Sigma process without a shift.

Now we multiply numerator and denominator of the right-hand side by 2 to convert 12σ into 6σ:

$$\sigma \le \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{12} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \ge 2$$

Hence we obtain the canonical **Six Sigma capability condition**:

$$\boxed{\,C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \ge 2\,}$$

This proves that a process can be considered **Six Sigma capable only when Cp ≥ 2.0**, with a built-in shift allowance.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — DPMO Calculation

> **Problem (KTU style):** In a manufacturing line producing printed circuit boards (PCBs), each unit has **20 opportunities** for defects (solder points). In a sample of **500 units**, **40 defects** were found. Compute the DPMO and the equivalent sigma level.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
- Defects (D) = 40
- Units (U) = 500
- Opportunities per unit (O) = 20
- Total opportunities = U × O = 500 × 20 = 10,000

**Step 2 — Apply the DPMO formula.**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$$

Substitute:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{40}{10{,}000} \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\text{DPMO} = 40 \times 100 = 4{,}000$$

So the process produces **4,000 defects per million opportunities**.

**Step 4 — Compute equivalent Yield.**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - 0.004 = 0.996 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 99.6\%$$

**Step 5 — Convert Yield to Sigma Level.**
Using the Motorola Sigma Conversion Table: 99.6% yield ≈ **4.0σ** (with 1.5σ shift).

**Conclusion:** The PCB process is at **4.0 sigma** — far below Six Sigma. Six Sigma would require **3.4 DPMO**, i.e., this process has roughly **1,176× more defects** than a Six Sigma process.

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — Cp and Cpk

> **Problem:** A shaft diameter has USL = 25.10 mm, LSL = 24.90 mm. The process mean is μ = 25.02 mm and σ = 0.02 mm. Compute Cp and Cpk.

**Step 1 — Cp calculation.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.20}{0.12} = 1.667$$

**Step 2 — Cpk calculation.**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.08}{0.06} = 1.333$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.12}{0.06} = 2.000$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.333,\ 2.000) = 1.333$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
- Cp = 1.667 → process is *capable* (≥ 1.33 industry minimum).
- Cpk = 1.333 → process is *not centered*. The mean is shifted toward the USL, so the lower side has more room but the upper side is tighter.
- To reach Six Sigma, both must be ≥ 2.0 *with* the 1.5σ shift; we need to either **reduce σ** or **re-center the process** to μ = 25.00 mm.

---

## 3.2 Derivations: QFD Technical Importance Weight

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the Weighted Importance Score

Let there be *m* customer requirements (Whats) indexed *i = 1, 2, …, m* and *n* technical requirements (Hows) indexed *j = 1, 2, …, n*.

Let:
- $CI_i$ = Customer Importance of requirement *i*, scale 1–5.
- $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength between requirement *i* and How *j*, with values:
  - Strong (●) = 9
  - Moderate (◐) = 3
  - Weak (○) = 1

The **Technical Importance** of How *j* is the weighted sum of all customer importance scores, weighted by the relationship strength:

$$\text{TI}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( CI_i \times R_{ij} \right)$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Example — QFD Importance

> **Problem:** A laptop manufacturer has 3 customer requirements (A: long battery, B: light weight, C: low cost) with CI = (5, 4, 3). Two technical requirements (X: battery capacity mAh, Y: chassis weight g) have the following relationships: A–X = 9, A–Y = 1, B–X = 3, B–Y = 9, C–X = 1, C–Y = 3. Compute TI_X and TI_Y.

**Step 1 — Compute TI_X.**

$$\text{TI}_X = (5)(9) + (4)(3) + (3)(1) = 45 + 12 + 3 = 60$$

**Step 2 — Compute TI_Y.**

$$\text{TI}_Y = (5)(1) + (4)(9) + (3)(3) = 5 + 36 + 9 = 50$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
- Improving **battery capacity (X)** is the **higher engineering priority** (TI = 60).
- Improving **chassis weight (Y)** has a slightly lower priority (TI = 50), but is still significant.
- Resource allocation: spend ~54.5% of R&D on X and ~45.5% on Y (proportional to TI).

---

## 3.3 Derivations: FMEA Risk Priority Number

### 3.3.1 Mathematical Basis

RPN is defined as the product of three ordinal ratings:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Range:** Each factor ∈ {1, …, 10}, so RPN ∈ {1, …, 1000}.

The **theoretical maximum** of 1000 corresponds to *catastrophic severity × extremely frequent occurrence × impossible detection*, i.e., the worst-case failure.

The **theoretical minimum** of 1 corresponds to *negligible severity × nearly impossible occurrence × certain detection*.

### 3.3.2 Worked Example — FMEA on a Pressure Valve

> **Problem:** A hydraulic pressure valve has three potential failure modes. The cross-functional team has scored them as shown below. Compute RPN, rank them, and recommend action thresholds.

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1: Valve leaks under low pressure | 7 | 4 | 5 | ? |
| F2: Valve sticks open (overpressure) | 9 | 3 | 8 | ? |
| F3: Valve corrodes after 6 months | 6 | 7 | 4 | ? |

**Step 1 — Compute each RPN.**

$$\text{RPN}_{F1} = 7 \times 4 \times 5 = 140$$

$$\text{RPN}_{F2} = 9 \times 3 \times 8 = 216$$

$$\text{RPN}_{F3} = 6 \times 7 \times 4 = 168$$

**Step 2 — Rank in descending order of RPN.**
- **1st (highest priority):** F2 → 216 (mandatory action)
- **2nd:** F3 → 168 (action recommended)
- **3rd:** F1 → 140 (monitor)

**Step 3 — Apply RPN rules.**
- All three RPN > 100 → corrective action mandatory for **all three**.
- F2 has **S = 9** → must be addressed *immediately* regardless of RPN.
- F3 has **S × O = 42 > 30** → additional investigation required.

**Step 4 — Recommendation.**
- F2: Redesign valve seat; add redundant pressure relief.
- F3: Apply corrosion-resistant coating; change inspection interval from 12 → 6 months.
- F1: Replace O-rings every 24 months; reduce D from 5 → 2 by adding automated leak test.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Concept

| Question / Situation | Best Concept | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| How do we compare with our competitors? | **Benchmarking** | External comparative analysis |
| How many defects does our process produce? | **Six Sigma (DPMO)** | Quantitative defect rate |
| Is our process stable and capable? | **Six Sigma (Cp, Cpk)** | Statistical capability |
| What does the customer actually need? | **QFD** | Translates VOC into design |
| What could go wrong in our design? | **FMEA** | Risk identification |
| Which failure should we fix first? | **FMEA (RPN)** | Prioritization by risk |
| How do we improve continuously? | **Six Sigma DMAIC** | Data-driven improvement |

---

## 3.5 Consolidation: The Continuous Quality Improvement Loop

The four concepts are **not independent** — they form a continuous improvement loop:

$$\text{QFD (Design It Right)} \rightarrow \text{FMEA (Predict Failure)} \rightarrow \text{Six Sigma (Operate It Right)} \rightarrow \text{Benchmarking (Compare \& Improve)} \rightarrow \text{(repeat)}$$

This integration is the **essence of modern TQM**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Six Sigma. Explain the concept of DPMO with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Six Sigma is a *data-driven statistical methodology* developed at Motorola (1986) that aims to eliminate defects by reducing process variation, targeting a benchmark of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. The "sigma" refers to standard deviation; a Six Sigma process has its specification limits set at ±6σ from the process mean, allowing for a 1.5σ long-term drift.

**DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is computed as:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

**Example:** A factory producing 1,000 LED bulbs/day with 5 defect opportunities per bulb finds 15 defects in one day. Then:
- Total opportunities = 1,000 × 5 = 5,000
- DPMO = (15 / 5,000) × 10^6 = **3,000 DPMO**, which corresponds to roughly **4.3σ**, far below Six Sigma performance.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? Explain its three components.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is the product of three rating factors used to prioritize potential failure modes in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

where each factor is scored on a scale of **1 to 10**:

- **S — Severity:** How serious is the effect of failure on the customer? (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic / safety hazard)
- **O — Occurrence:** How frequently does the failure mode occur? (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent / almost continuous)
- **D — Detectability:** How likely is the failure to be detected *before* reaching the customer? (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = impossible to detect)

**Interpretation:**
- RPN ranges from **1 to 1,000**.
- An RPN greater than **100** typically mandates corrective action.
- A Severity score ≥ 9 (even with low RPN) demands immediate attention because of potential safety/regulatory impact.

**Example:** For a brake-failure mode in an automobile, S = 10, O = 3, D = 4 ⇒ RPN = 10 × 3 × 4 = **120**, triggering immediate corrective redesign.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]

**a)** Define Benchmarking. Explain the different types of benchmarking with examples. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the House of Quality (QFD) framework with a neat diagram. Explain its six building blocks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Benchmarking

**Definition:**
Benchmarking is the *continuous, systematic process of comparing an organization's processes, products, and services against best-in-class performers* (inside or outside the industry) to identify performance gaps and implement superior practices.

**Origin:** Pioneered by **Rank Xerox (1979)** to counter competition from Japanese photocopier makers.

**Types of Benchmarking:**

| Type | Comparison Target | Example | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Different units/departments within the same organization | Comparing production efficiency of Plant A vs. Plant B of the same company | Cheap, easy data access |
| **Competitive** | Direct competitors in the same industry | Maruti Suzuki benchmarking Hyundai for service turnaround | Highly relevant |
| **Functional / Generic** | Best-in-class performers in the same function, but different industry | A hospital benchmarking a five-star hotel for patient admission process | Wide innovation pool |
| **Strategic** | Industry leaders on long-term strategy | Tata Steel benchmarking POSCO (Korea) for steel-making R&D direction | Future-oriented |

**Process (4 Steps):**
1. **Plan:** Identify what to benchmark and select partners.
2. **Collect:** Gather data through site visits, surveys, public reports.
3. **Analyze:** Compute the performance gap; perform root-cause analysis.
4. **Adapt & Improve:** Implement, monitor, and *re-benchmark continuously*.

**Pitfalls:**
- Blindly copying without understanding the *context*.
- Benchmarking once and stopping (must be continuous).
- Tunnel vision — focusing only on quantitative metrics, ignoring culture.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — House of Quality (QFD)

**Definition:**
QFD is a structured planning tool developed by **Yoji Akao (1966, Japan)** that translates customer needs (Voice of Customer) into specific engineering requirements throughout the product development cycle. The central artefact is the **House of Quality (HoQ)**.

**Diagram (Description):**
The House of Quality is a matrix resembling a house:
- **Ceiling:** Technical Requirements (Hows).
- **Left Wall:** Customer Requirements (Whats).
- **Center:** Inter-relationship Matrix (●◐○).
- **Roof (Triangle):** Technical correlations among Hows.
- **Right Wall:** Competitive assessment on customer requirements.
- **Bottom (Basement):** Technical importance ratings and target values.

**Six Building Blocks Explained:**

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats) — Left Wall:**
   Listed in plain language from VOC surveys, focus groups, complaint logs. Prioritized on a 1–5 scale.

2. **Technical Requirements (Hows) — Ceiling:**
   Engineering descriptors that the design team can control (e.g., battery capacity in mAh, weight in grams).

3. **Inter-relationship Matrix — Center:**
   Cells filled with ● (strong = 9), ◐ (medium = 3), ○ (weak = 1) to indicate how strongly each How addresses each What.

4. **Technical Correlations — Roof Triangle:**
   Shows synergies (+) and conflicts (−) between Hows. A diamond ◊ = strong positive, triangle ▲ = strong negative.

5. **Competitive Assessment — Right Wall:**
   Compares our product and competitors' products on each What (and optionally each How on the basement).

6. **Technical Importance and Targets — Basement:**
   For each How, compute $\text{TI}_j = \sum_i CI_i \cdot R_{ij}$. The highest-TI Hows get the most engineering effort. Target values are assigned.

**Conclusion:**
The HoQ converts subjective customer desires into objective engineering targets, ensuring design efforts are *aligned with customer value*.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — December 2023 (Model Paper)]

**a)** What is Six Sigma? Explain the DMAIC methodology in detail with the tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A process produces 2,000 units per day. Each unit has 8 opportunities for defects. In one week (7 days), 672 defects are found.
- (i) Calculate the DPMO. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the yield in percentage. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the spec limits are USL = 12.05 mm and LSL = 11.95 mm, with σ = 0.012 mm, compute Cp. What does this indicate about the process? **(2 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Six Sigma & DMAIC

**Definition:**
Six Sigma is a *data-driven quality methodology* that uses statistical tools to reduce process variation and defects to near-zero levels, targeting **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. Pioneered at **Motorola (1986)** and popularized by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)**.

**DMAIC Methodology:**

| Phase | Name | Purpose | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality), and project scope | Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC diagram, CTQ tree |
| **M** | **Measure** | Quantify the current state; validate measurement system | Control charts, MSA (Gage R&R), capability indices (Cp, Cpk), DPMO baseline |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Identify root causes of defects and variation | Pareto chart, Ishikawa fishbone, 5-Why analysis, regression, hypothesis testing |
| **I** | **Improve** | Pilot and implement solutions to fix root causes | Design of Experiments (DOE), Poka-Yoke, Kaizen events, pilot runs |
| **C** | **Control** | Lock in gains through standardization and monitoring | Statistical Process Control (SPC), control plans, SOPs, training |

**Why DMAIC Works:** Each phase builds on the previous one, ensuring that improvements are *data-driven, not opinion-driven*.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Units/day = 2,000
- Days/week = 7
- Opportunities/unit (O) = 8
- Defects/week (D) = 672
- USL = 12.05 mm, LSL = 11.95 mm, σ = 0.012 mm

**Step (i) — DPMO Calculation:**

Total units = 2,000 × 7 = 14,000
Total opportunities = 14,000 × 8 = 112,000

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6 = \frac{672}{14{,}000 \times 8} \times 10^6$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{672}{112{,}000} \times 10^6 = 0.006 \times 10^6 = 6{,}000 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step (ii) — Yield Calculation:**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - 0.006 = 0.994 \;\; \text{i.e.,} \;\; 99.4\%$$

**Step (iii) — Cp Calculation:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389$$

**Interpretation:**
- Cp = 1.389 is **above the industry minimum of 1.33** → process is *capable* of meeting specifications.
- However, Cp = 1.389 is **below 2.0** → process is *not Six Sigma capable*. To reach Six Sigma, σ must be reduced to 0.10/(6×2) = **0.0083 mm**, i.e., a 31% reduction in variation.

**Conclusion:** The process is acceptable for general manufacturing but requires Six Sigma-driven DMAIC improvement to achieve world-class quality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ×10⁶ factor in DPMO** — students write the ratio without scaling. Marks cut by 50% in numerical questions.
> 2. **Confusing Cp with Cpk** — Cp assumes perfect centering; Cpk accounts for shift. If the mean ≠ midpoint, you MUST use Cpk.
> 3. **RPN — students often add S + O + D instead of multiplying** — that's wrong. It is **S × O × D**.
> 4. **QFD diagrams** — examiners expect the *six* building blocks. Drawing only 3–4 rooms leads to a 2-mark cut.
> 5. **Benchmarking types** — students often forget **Strategic** and **Generic/Functional** types. List *all four* types.
> 6. **DMAIC** — Do NOT write "DMAIC is a one-time process." It is **iterative and continuous**.
> 7. **Six Sigma** — Do NOT say "3.4 defects per million units" — it is per **million opportunities**, not units. Marks deducted for wrong unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Last-Minute Revision Sheet — Pin This to Your Wall Before Exam**

### ✅ Benchmarking
- [ ] Definition: continuous comparison with best-in-class.
- [ ] **4 types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional/Generic, Strategic.
- [ ] **4 steps:** Plan → Collect → Analyze → Adapt.
- [ ] Origin: **Xerox (1979)**.
- [ ] Pitfall: must be **continuous**, not one-time.

### ✅ Six Sigma
- [ ] Definition: methodology targeting **3.4 DPMO** with **1.5σ shift**.
- [ ] Origin: **Motorola (1986), Bill Smith, Bob Galvin**.
- [ ] **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- [ ] **DPMO formula:** $D / (U \times O) \times 10^6$.
- [ ] **Cp formula:** $(USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$.
- [ ] **Cpk formula:** $\min[(USL-\mu)/3\sigma, (\mu-LSL)/3\sigma]$.
- [ ] Six Sigma capable ⇒ **Cp, Cpk ≥ 2.0**.
- [ ] σ-levels: 3σ = 66,807 DPMO; 4σ = 6,210 DPMO; 5σ = 233 DPMO; **6σ = 3.4 DPMO**.

### ✅ Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
- [ ] Definition: translates Voice of Customer into engineering requirements.
- [ ] Origin: **Yoji Akao (1966, Japan), Mitsubishi (1972)**.
- [ ] Central artefact: **House of Quality (HoQ)**.
- [ ] **Six rooms:** Whats (left), Hows (ceiling), Relationship (center), Correlations (roof), Competitive (right), Targets (bottom).
- [ ] **Tech Importance formula:** $\text{TI}_j = \sum_i CI_i \cdot R_{ij}$.
- [ ] Relationship marks: ● = 9 (strong), ◐ = 3 (medium), ○ = 1 (weak).

### ✅ Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
- [ ] Definition: proactive technique to identify and prioritize potential failures.
- [ ] Origin: **U.S. Military MIL-STD-1629 (1949)**; now **IEC 60812**.
- [ ] **7 steps:** team → scope → failure modes → effects → causes → S/O/D scoring → RPN.
- [ ] **RPN = S × O × D**, range 1 to 1000.
- [ ] **S, O, D scale: 1 to 10 each.**
- [ ] Action threshold: **RPN > 100 OR S ≥ 9 ⇒ mandatory action**.
- [ ] Used in ISO 9001:2015, IATF 16949, AS9100D.

### ✅ Master Mnemonic
> **"B-S-Q-F" = B**enchmarking, **S**ix Sigma, **Q**FD, **F**MEA — the **four pillars of Module 3**.

### ✅ Golden Equations (Recall First)
$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$$

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

$$\text{TI}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} CI_i \cdot R_{ij}$$

### ✅ Examiner Tip
Always end Module 3 answers with a **one-line integration statement**: *"These four concepts together form the engine of Total Quality Management — Benchmarking measures the goal, QFD designs the response, FMEA prevents failure, and Six Sigma operates with statistical precision."* This **bonus line often fetches an extra mark** for showing holistic understanding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of "Methodology" in TQM

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, a *Methodology* refers to a **structured, repeatable, and data-driven framework** that organizations use to plan, execute, measure, and continuously improve quality-related processes. It is the *engineering of improvement itself* — a roadmap that converts abstract quality philosophy into measurable operational excellence.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEEIT754 – TQM), the term "Methodology" within Module 3 broadly encapsulates four world-renowned quality frameworks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Quality Methodology (KTU Module 3 Focus)**
> 1. **DMAIC** – Six Sigma Problem-Solving Methodology (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)
> 2. **PDCA** – Deming / Shewhart Cycle (Plan, Do, Check, Act)
> 3. **5S** – Workplace Organization Methodology (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain)
> 4. **Kaizen & Benchmarking** – Continuous Improvement and Comparative Learning

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are a doctor treating a patient who has a recurring headache.

- You don't just prescribe a painkiller randomly. You **Define** the problem (where does it hurt? how often?), **Measure** (temperature, BP, blood test), **Analyze** (is it migraine, dehydration, or stress?), **Improve** (prescribe targeted treatment), and **Control** (follow-up visits to ensure it doesn't return).
- That, exactly, is **DMAIC**.
- **PDCA** is like the doctor's lifelong learning loop: every cured patient teaches something, and the doctor **Plans** better, **Does** better, **Checks** the outcome, and **Acts** on new knowledge.
- **5S** is like organizing the doctor's clinic desk so that the right instrument is always within reach.
- **Kaizen** is the philosophy that even a brilliant surgeon must keep practicing small daily improvements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Methodology Matters in Engineering:**
> Without a methodology, quality improvement becomes *random heroism*. With a methodology, it becomes *predictable engineering* — which is precisely what KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework demands.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Full Form | Originator | Core Purpose |
|------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| DMAIC | Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control | Motorola (1986) | Reduce defects to **3.4 DPMO** |
| PDCA | Plan-Do-Check-Act | Walter Shewhart / W. Edwards Deming | Continuous iterative improvement |
| 5S | Sort-Set-Shine-Standardize-Sustain | Toyota Production System | Workplace standardization |
| Kaizen | 改 善 (Change for the Better) | Masaaki Imai | Continuous small improvements |
| DPMO | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Motorola | Universal defect metric |
| CTQ | Critical-to-Quality | Six Sigma | Customer-driven specification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DMAIC — The Six Sigma Methodology (Detailed)

DMAIC is the **closed-loop, data-driven** problem-solving methodology of Six Sigma. Each phase has a defined deliverable:

**Phase 1 — DEFINE**
- Identify the **project charter**, **Voice of Customer (VOC)**, and **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics.
- Build the **SIPOC** diagram (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers).
- Deliverable: Problem statement + project scope.

**Phase 2 — MEASURE**
- Establish the baseline performance of the process.
- Quantify current **DPMO** and **Sigma Level**.
- Validate the measurement system (Gauge R&R).
- Deliverable: Baseline sigma + data collection plan.

**Phase 3 — ANALYZE**
- Identify **root causes** using tools such as:
  - Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule)
  - Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram — 6 Ms: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature
  - Hypothesis Testing, Regression, ANOVA
- Deliverable: Validated root-cause list.

**Phase 4 — IMPROVE**
- Generate, pilot, and validate solutions.
- Use **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to optimize.
- Calculate **Cost-Benefit** and ROI.
- Deliverable: Implemented, statistically validated solution.

**Phase 5 — CONTROL**
- Lock in the gains using **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**, **Control Charts**, **Mistake-Proofing (Poka-Yoke)**, and updated **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
- Deliverable: Updated process with monitoring plan.

### 2.2 PDCA — The Deming Cycle

PDCA is a **four-step iterative management method** used for the continuous improvement of processes and products. Unlike DMAIC (which is project-based), PDCA is **cyclical and perpetual**.

- **P (Plan):** Identify the goal, define the problem, propose a hypothesis.
- **D (Do):** Execute the plan on a small scale; collect data.
- **C (Check / Study):** Compare actual results against predicted results using statistical analysis.
- **A (Act):** If successful, standardize; if not, restart the cycle with refined knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDCA vs. PDSA:** Some texts use *PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)* to emphasize statistical *Study* rather than simple *Check*. Both are accepted by KTU examiners.

### 2.3 The 5S Methodology (Workplace Standardization)

5S is the **foundation of Lean Manufacturing** and a prerequisite for any successful DMAIC deployment.

| Step | Japanese | English | Action |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| 1 | Seiri (整理) | **Sort** | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2 | Seiton (整頓) | **Set in Order** | A place for everything, everything in its place |
| 3 | Seiso (清掃) | **Shine** | Clean the workplace and equipment |
| 4 | Seiketsu (清潔) | **Standardize** | Establish norms and visual management |
| 5 | Shitsuke (躾) | **Sustain** | Self-discipline and continuous adherence |

### 2.4 Kaizen Methodology

Kaizen is the Japanese philosophy of **continuous, incremental improvement** involving *every employee from the CEO to the shop-floor worker*. It contrasts sharply with Western "innovation" thinking:
- **Kaizen** = Small steps, low cost, continuous.
- **Innovation** = Large steps, high cost, occasional.

Tools: Gemba Walks, PDCA, Suggestion Schemes, Quality Circles.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for the KTU Board Exam — Memorize These**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| **DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = defects; $U$ = units; $O$ = opportunities per unit |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Total units entered}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | $Y_i$ = yield of stage $i$ |
| **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL, LSL$ = spec limits; $\sigma$ = std deviation |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]$ | $\mu$ = process mean |
| **Defects Per Unit (DPU)** | $DPU = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Defects per unit |
| **Sigma Level (approx)** | $\text{Sigma Level} \approx \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} \text{ (one-sided)}$ | For two-sided use $Cpk \times 3 + 1.5$ (shift) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on the 1.5 Sigma Shift:**
> Motorola empirically observed that real processes drift by **1.5σ** over the long term. Therefore, a "6σ" process with $Cpk = 2.0$ delivers **3.4 DPMO**, not 0 DPMO. This **3.4 DPMO** figure is the *single most-asked Six Sigma number* in KTU exams.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DMAIC** is used by GE, Motorola, Honeywell, Texas Instruments — and increasingly in Indian manufacturing hubs (Chennai, Pune, Bengaluru).
- **PDCA** is mandated by **ISO 9001:2015** (Clause 10 — Improvement).
- **5S** is a visual prerequisite for any Industry 4.0 smart factory.
- **Cpk $\geq$ 1.33** is the universally accepted minimum for an *automotive-tier-1 supplier*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — DPMO and Sigma Level Conversion

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU style):**
*A manufacturing line produces 1,000 units per day. Each unit has 5 critical-to-quality characteristics. Over 5 working days, the inspector records 60 defects. Compute the DPMO and the equivalent long-term Sigma Level.*

**Given:**
- $U = 1000 \times 5 = 5000$ units
- $O = 5$ opportunities per unit
- $D = 60$ defects

**Step 1 — Compute total opportunities**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = U \times O = 5000 \times 5 = 25{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DPMO formula**

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}
$$

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{60 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{25{,}000}
$$

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{60{,}000{,}000}{25{,}000} = 2400
$$

**Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Yield (% defective)**

$$
\text{Yield} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} = \dfrac{60}{25{,}000} = 0.0024
$$

$$
\text{Defective Rate (\%)} = 0.0024 \times 100 = 0.24\%
$$

**Step 4 — Convert % Defective to Sigma Level (using the standard Six Sigma conversion table)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Six Sigma Conversion (1.5σ shift included):**
> 0.24% defective $\Rightarrow$ **approximately 4.2σ**

**Interpretation:** The process is operating at roughly **4.2 Sigma**, well below the world-class **6σ (3.4 DPMO)** benchmark. A DMAIC project is therefore justified.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Capability Indices (Cp and Cpk)

**Problem Statement:**
*A CNC lathe produces a shaft with specification $LSL = 24.85$ mm and $USL = 25.15$ mm. From 50 samples, the process mean is $\mu = 25.02$ mm and standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.04$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and comment on process capability.*

**Step 1 — Compute Cp (Process Potential)**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.15 - 24.85}{6 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{0.30}{0.24} = 1.25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cpk (Process Performance — accounts for centering)**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]
$$

Upper-side term:

$$
\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.15 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.12} = 1.0833
$$

Lower-side term:

$$
\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.02 - 24.85}{3 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.17}{0.12} = 1.4166
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.0833,\ 1.4166) = 1.0833
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation (Valuation-Ready Table)**

| Index | Value | Benchmark | Verdict |
|-------|-------|-----------|---------|
| $C_p$ | 1.25 | $\geq 1.33$ ideal | Process *spread* is acceptable but tight |
| $C_{pk}$ | 1.083 | $\geq 1.33$ ideal | Process is **off-center** (shifted toward USL) |
| Centering offset | $\mu - \text{midpoint} = +0.02$ mm | Should be 0 | Re-center the lathe tool offset |

**Conclusion:** The process is capable in width but not in centering. Recommended DMAIC "Improve" action — calibrate the machine to bring $\mu$ to **25.00 mm** (the midpoint).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

**Problem Statement:**
*A 4-stage assembly line has stage yields: 0.98, 0.97, 0.95, 0.99. Compute FPY, RTY, and DPU-equivalent scrap rate.*

$$
RTY = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.99
$$

$$
RTY = 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506
$$

$$
RTY = 0.9506 \times 0.95 = 0.90307
$$

$$
RTY = 0.90307 \times 0.99 = 0.89414
$$

$$
\text{RTY} \approx 89.41\%
$$

$$
\text{Scrap Loss} = 1 - RTY = 1 - 0.89414 = 0.10586 \approx 10.59\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Factory Insight:** Even when each stage yield looks "good" ($\geq 95\%$), the *cumulative* scrap can exceed **10%** — a phenomenon known as the *Hidden Factory* (a favourite KTU long-answer topic).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Methodology Selection Guide

| Situation | Recommended Methodology | Why |
|-----------|------------------------|-----|
| Undefined problem, vague customer complaints | **DMAIC** | Forces structured Define phase |
| Existing process running well, wants incremental upgrade | **PDCA / Kaizen** | Lightweight, low-cost cycles |
| Cluttered, unsafe, disorganized workplace | **5S** | Foundation for everything else |
| Need to compare with industry best practice | **Benchmarking** | Gap analysis tool |
| Customer requirements unclear / need to translate into specs | **QFD (House of Quality)** | Cross-functional alignment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DMAIC Methodology — Phase-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DEFINE Project Charter SIPOC VOC CTQ Tree"] --> B["MEASURE Baseline DPMO Sigma Level Gauge R&R"]
    B --> C["ANALYZE Pareto Fishbone Hypothesis Test DOE"]
    C --> D["IMPROVE Pilot Solution DOE Optimization Cost Benefit"]
    D --> E["CONTROL SPC Control Charts Poka Yoke SOP Update"]
    E --> F{"Gains Sustained?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Project Closure Handoff to Process Owner"]
    F -- No --> A

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#17becf,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** The loop-back arrow from `F` to `A` is the *Iterative DMAIC* — when control is lost, the team re-enters the Define phase rather than declaring victory prematurely.

---

### 4.2 PDCA — The Deming Cycle (Closed-Loop Improvement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PLAN Identify Problem Set Objectives Hypothesize"] --> D["DO Execute Small Scale Test Collect Data"]
    D --> C["CHECK Compare Actual vs Predicted Statistical Analysis"]
    C --> A["ACT Standardize if Successful Else Revise Plan"]
    A --> P

    style P fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2ecc71,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#f1c40f,color:#000000
    style A fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** PDCA has *no end-state*. It is a *perpetual flywheel* — Deming's central thesis that improvement is a journey, not a destination.

---

### 4.3 5S — Workplace Standardization Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["S1 SORT Seiri Remove Unnecessary Items"] --> S2["S2 SET IN ORDER Seiton Arrange A Place for Everything"]
    S2 --> S3["S3 SHINE Seiso Clean Workplace and Equipment"]
    S3 --> S4["S4 STANDARDIZE Seiketsu Visual Management Norms"]
    S4 --> S5["S5 SUSTAIN Shitsuke Self Discipline Continuous Adherence"]

    S5 --> OUT["Lean Foundation Ready for DMAIC PDCA Kaizen"]

    style S1 fill:#ffcccb,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#ffe4b5,color:#000000
    style S3 fill:#fffacd,color:#000000
    style S4 fill:#e0ffff,color:#000000
    style S5 fill:#d8bfd8,color:#000000
    style OUT fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.4 Methodology Selection — Block-Level Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the problem defined and measurable?"}
    Q1 -- No --> M1["Use DMAIC Define Phase SIPOC VOC"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is the solution known?"}
    Q2 -- No --> M2["Use DMAIC Measure Analyze Improve"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{"Is it a one-time fix?"}
    Q3 -- No --> M3["Use PDCA Kaizen Continuous Loop"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> M4["Apply Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing"]
    M1 --> BASE["Prerequisite 5S Workplace Stability"]
    M2 --> BASE
    M3 --> BASE
    BASE --> DEPLOY["Deploy Standardize Document Train"]

    style Q1 fill:#f9e79f,color:#000000
    style Q2 fill:#aed6f1,color:#000000
    style Q3 fill:#a9dfbf,color:#000000
    style BASE fill:#f5b7b1,color:#000000
    style DEPLOY fill:#82e0aa,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define DPMO. A Six Sigma process aims for how many defects per million opportunities? Why is the 1.5 sigma shift considered in Six Sigma calculations?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities):** A standardized metric that quantifies the number of defects observed per one million opportunities for a defect to occur. Formula: $DPMO = (D \times 1{,}000{,}000) / (U \times O)$.
- **Six Sigma target:** **3.4 DPMO** (world-class benchmark).
- **1.5σ shift rationale:** Real manufacturing processes experience long-term mean drift of approximately 1.5 standard deviations due to tool wear, environmental variation, and human factors. Hence a theoretical 6σ (0 DPMO) becomes 3.4 DPMO in practice, making Six Sigma a *realistic and rigorous* benchmark rather than an unattainable ideal.

> **Examiner's Note (1 mark reserved for naming the formula + 1 mark for the 3.4 DPMO fact + 1 mark for 1.5σ explanation.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the five phases of the DMAIC methodology and state the principal deliverable of the "Analyze" phase.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Phase | Full Form | Core Deliverable |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| D | Define | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| M | Measure | Baseline DPMO, Sigma Level, Validated MSA |
| **A** | **Analyze** | **Validated Root Causes (Pareto, Fishbone, Hypothesis Testing)** |
| I | Improve | Pilot-validated solution, DOE results |
| C | Control | Control Plan, Updated SOPs, SPC Charts |

> **Allocate 2 marks for the five phases and 1 mark specifically for the Analyze-phase deliverable.**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Attempt either QUESTION A or QUESTION B. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

#### **QUESTION A [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the DMAIC methodology in detail with a suitable industrial example. List the tools used in each phase.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
DMAIC is a five-phase, data-driven improvement methodology developed by Motorola in 1986 to systematically reduce process defects and variation. It forms the core of Six Sigma and is widely used in manufacturing and service industries.

**Phase-wise explanation (5 × 1 mark = 5 marks):**

1. **Define:** Identify the problem, customer (VOC), and project scope. Tools: Project Charter, SIPOC, CTQ Tree, VOC, Kano Model.
   - *Industrial example:* A bearings manufacturer faces customer complaints about premature failure — Define phase frames this as a *CTQ = L10 life $\geq$ 1500 hours*.

2. **Measure:** Quantify the current state. Tools: DPMO, Sigma Level, Gauge R&R, Check Sheets, Control Charts.
   - *Example:* Baseline measurement shows current L10 life = 1100 hours; process sigma = 3.2σ.

3. **Analyze:** Identify root causes. Tools: Pareto (80/20), Ishikawa Fishbone (6 Ms), 5-Why, Hypothesis Testing, Regression.
   - *Example:* Fishbone reveals that inadequate heat treatment and poor bearing-race surface finish are the dominant contributors.

4. **Improve:** Develop and validate solutions. Tools: DOE, Pilot Runs, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen Events.
   - *Example:* Optimize tempering temperature using DOE; upgrade grinding coolant.

5. **Control:** Lock the gains. Tools: SPC Control Charts, Control Plan, Updated SOPs, Mistake-Proofing.
   - *Example:* Install X-bar/R charts on the heat-treatment line; standard SOP deployed across all three shifts.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
DMAIC's strength lies in its disciplined, data-driven, closed-loop nature — it does not allow the team to declare victory until statistically proven improvement is sustained over time.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A process produces 800 units per shift. Each unit has 7 inspection points. In one shift, 56 defects are recorded. Calculate DPMO, % Defective, Rolled Throughput Yield if the process has 4 stages with yields 0.96, 0.94, 0.97, 0.99, and state whether the process qualifies as Six Sigma.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — DPMO Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
U = 800,\quad O = 7,\quad D = 56
$$

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 800 \times 7 = 5600
$$

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{56 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5600} = \dfrac{56{,}000{,}000}{5600} = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — % Defective (1 mark):**

$$
\% \text{Defective} = \dfrac{56}{5600} \times 100 = 1.0\%
$$

From the Six Sigma conversion table, **1.0% defective $\approx$ 4.0σ**.

**Step 3 — RTY (2 marks):**

$$
RTY = 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.97 \times 0.99
$$

$$
RTY = 0.9024 \times 0.97 = 0.8753
$$

$$
RTY = 0.8753 \times 0.99 = 0.8666 \approx 86.66\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):**
The process operates at **4.0σ with 10,000 DPMO** and a cumulative scrap loss of **13.34%**. This is **far from Six Sigma** (3.4 DPMO at 6σ). A rigorous DMAIC project is recommended.

---

#### **QUESTION B [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the PDCA (Deming) cycle. How does it differ from DMAIC? When should an organization prefer PDCA over DMAIC?**

**Model Solution:**

**PDCA Phases (3 marks):**

- **Plan:** Recognize an opportunity and plan a change. Define the problem, set measurable objectives, propose a hypothesis.
- **Do:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot). Document observations and collect data rigorously.
- **Check (Study):** Evaluate results by comparing actual vs. expected performance using statistical and graphical tools.
- **Act:** If the change worked, standardize it and roll out; if not, revise the hypothesis and loop back to Plan.

**PDCA vs DMAIC — Comparative Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | PDCA | DMAIC |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Origin | Shewhart (1939) | Motorola (1986) |
| Structure | 4 phases, simple | 5 phases, rigorous |
| Project Nature | Continuous, light-weight | Project-based, heavy |
| Statistical Rigour | Light to moderate | Heavy (DOE, Hypothesis Test) |
| Best For | Incremental, ongoing improvements | Complex defect/variation reduction |

**When to prefer PDCA (2 marks):**
- The problem is well-understood; the team only needs a cycle to test and refine a known hypothesis.
- Resources are limited (PDCA requires no Black Belt team).
- The organization already practices ISO 9001 or Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).
- Rapid, small-scale experimentation is required (Lean Start-up style).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the 5S methodology with the Japanese terms. Discuss how 5S acts as the foundation for DMAIC and Lean implementation, citing two industrial case-study points.**

**Model Solution:**

**The Five Pillars of 5S (3 marks):**

1. **Seiri (Sort):** Distinguish necessary from unnecessary items; remove the latter. *Tag-and-red-tag* method is widely used.
2. **Seiton (Set in Order):** "A place for everything, everything in its place." Apply *visual management* (shadow boards, floor markings).
3. **Seiso (Shine):** Clean the workplace; cleaning is also inspection (defects become visible).
4. **Seiketsu (Standardize):** Establish norms, checklists, and visual standards to sustain the first three S's.
5. **Shitsuke (Sustain):** Build self-discipline through training, audits, and management commitment.

**5S as Foundation for DMAIC + Lean (2 marks):**
- Without *Sort* and *Set in Order*, the Measure phase cannot collect reliable data (no standard workstation layout).
- Without *Shine*, defects of contamination/loose parts remain hidden, polluting the Analyze phase.
- Without *Standardize*, the Improve phase cannot be replicated across shifts.
- Without *Sustain*, gains erode within weeks — making the Control phase of DMAIC fail.

**Industrial Case Studies (2 marks):**
- **Toyota (Japan):** 5S is credited as the foundation that enabled TPS (Toyota Production System). Shop-floor visibility dropped defect-detection time from hours to minutes.
- **Tata Motors (Pune):** Implementing 5S before DMAIC reduced internal defect rework by 28% in the first 12 months and improved OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) from 62% to 78%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up DMAIC phases with PDSA phases** in the same answer — never do this; examiners deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "DPMO = 0" for Six Sigma** — the correct value is **3.4 DPMO**. This single error costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift explanation** — examiners expect it in any Six Sigma derivation.
> 4. **Cpk vs Cp confusion:** Cp ignores centering; Cpk accounts for it. Mixing them up = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Spelling Japanese terms incorrectly** (e.g., "Seiketsu" vs "Seikets") — write the *romaji* carefully.
> 6. **Skipping units in DPMO** — always write "defects per *million* opportunities" explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DMAIC** = 5 phases (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control); data-driven; project-based.
- ✅ **PDCA** = 4 phases (Plan, Do, Check, Act); perpetual flywheel; ISO 9001 mandate.
- ✅ **5S** = Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — *foundation*, not an end-state.
- ✅ **Six Sigma benchmark = 3.4 DPMO** (because of the **1.5σ shift**).
- ✅ **DPMO formula:** $DPMO = (D \times 10^6) / (U \times O)$.
- ✅ **Cp** measures *spread*; **Cpk** measures *spread + centering*. $Cpk \geq 1.33$ = capable.
- ✅ **RTY** = product of all stage yields; reveals the *Hidden Factory* of cumulative scrap.
- ✅ **Kaizen** = continuous small improvements; contrasts with disruptive *Innovation*.
- ✅ **CTQ** = customer-critical specification; anchor of every DMAIC project.
- ✅ **VOC** = Voice of Customer; translated into CTQ via the *Kano Model* and *House of Quality*.
- ✅ **SPC + Control Charts** = the *Control* phase's primary weapon.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing device; complements the Control phase.
- ✅ **5S precedes DMAIC** — never run a Six Sigma project in a chaotic workplace.
- ✅ **Benchmarking** = external comparison; *Kaizen* = internal improvement.
- ✅ **PDCA vs DMAIC** = light-weight cycle vs heavy project structure — choose wisely.
- ✅ **Sigma conversion table values** frequently tested: 0.1% = 4.4σ; 1% = 4.0σ; 3.4 DPMO = 6σ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications to manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications to manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Application of TQM to Manufacturing** refers to the systematic deployment of Total Quality Management philosophies, statistical tools, continuous improvement methodologies, and customer-centric frameworks within shop-floor and production environments to achieve zero-defect output, optimal resource utilization, minimal waste, and sustained competitive advantage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic bridges the *philosophical pillars* of TQM (from Modules 1 & 2) with their **practical, on-the-ground industrial execution** in discrete and process manufacturing sectors. It encompasses the integration of tools such as **Just-in-Time (JIT)**, **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**, **Poka-Yoke**, **Kanban**, **5S**, and **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** into a unified production quality ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Focus):**
> The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on understanding *how* TQM principles translate into tangible manufacturing outcomes. Expected cognitive levels range from **Apply (L3)** to **Analyze (L4)** and **Evaluate (L5)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital Kitchen" Analogy**
> Imagine a five-star hospital kitchen preparing meals for 200 patients. The head chef cannot personally inspect every plate. Instead, the kitchen uses:
> - **Standardized recipes** (SOPs/Work Instructions),
> - **Color-coded trays** that prevent wrong-diet delivery (Poka-Yoke),
> - **Just-in-time ingredient delivery** from cold storage (JIT/Kanban),
> - **Daily 5-minute cleanup before service** (5S),
> - **Equipment checked every morning** (TPM),
> - **Chef tasting every 10th dish** (SPC/Sampling).
>
> Result: Zero wrong meals, no food wastage, and patients (customers) are delighted. This is exactly what TQM does in a manufacturing plant — only with metal, electronics, or chemicals instead of food.

### 1.3 Core Manufacturing TQM Metrics

The following **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** are universally used in KTU-aligned manufacturing quality problems:

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance × Quality
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** = Good Units Produced ÷ Total Units Started
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** = (Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities per Unit)) × 10⁶
- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** = Product of individual process yields
- **Cycle Time (CT)** = Processing Time + Wait Time + Move Time + Queue Time

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** Always recall OEE through the phrase **"APQ — Always Perform Quality"** (Availability, Performance, Quality).

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites from Earlier Modules

Before diving into manufacturing applications, students must be comfortable with:

| Prior Module Concept | Application in Manufacturing |
|---|---|
| Deming's 14 Points | Foundation for shop-floor philosophy |
| Juran's Quality Trilogy | Planning → Control → Improvement loop |
| Customer Focus (Module 1) | Internal customers = next process station |
| PDCA Cycle | The engine of manufacturing CI |
| Pareto Analysis | Defect prioritization on the line |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major TQM Frameworks in Manufacturing

### 2.1.1 Just-in-Time (JIT) Manufacturing

**Definition:** A pull-based production philosophy that aims to produce the right item, in the right quantity, at the right time — minimizing inventory and associated waste.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pull" vs "Push" Distinction**
> - **Push System (Traditional):** Produce based on forecast; push to next station regardless of demand.
> - **Pull System (JIT):** Produce only when the downstream station signals demand.

**Seven Wastes (Muda) Eliminated by JIT:**

1. **Transport** — unnecessary movement of materials
2. **Inventory** — excess raw/WIP/finished goods
3. **Motion** — unnecessary worker movement
4. **Waiting** — idle time of workers/machines
5. **Overproduction** — making more than needed
6. **Over-processing** — doing more than customer requires
7. **Defects** — rework and scrap

---

### 2.1.2 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

**Definition:** A proactive and preventive maintenance system involving **all employees** — from top management to shop-floor operators — to maximize equipment effectiveness.

**Eight Pillars of TPM:**

| Pillar | Focus |
|---|---|
| 1. Autonomous Maintenance | Operators maintain their own machines |
| 2. Planned Maintenance | Scheduled, preventive actions |
| 3. Quality Maintenance | Zero-defect condition of equipment |
| 4. Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen) | Targeted loss elimination |
| 5. Early Equipment Management | Design for maintainability |
| 6. Training | Skill development for all |
| 7. Safety, Health, Environment | Zero accident culture |
| 8. Office TPM | Administrative support functions |

---

### 2.1.3 Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)

**Definition:** Japanese term meaning "mistake-proofing." A device or procedure that prevents an error from occurring or makes the error immediately obvious so it can be corrected.

**Two Functional Types:**

- **Prevention Type:** Physically prevents the error (e.g., USB-A vs USB-C asymmetry).
- **Detection Type:** Detects the error and triggers an alarm/stop (e.g., car seatbelt alarm).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Real Example:** A car's gear lever cannot be removed from "Park" unless the brake pedal is pressed — this is a Poka-Yoke.

---

### 2.1.4 Kanban System

**Definition:** A visual signal-based scheduling system that controls the flow of work/inventory in a pull production environment.

**Types of Kanban Signals:**

- **Withdrawal Kanban (Conveyance):** Signals need to pick up parts.
- **Production Kanban (In-Process):** Signals need to start production.
- **Supplier Kanban:** Triggers replenishment from external vendor.

**Number of Kanban Cards Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of Kanban cards
- $D$ = Demand rate (units per time)
- $L$ = Lead time (time)
- $S$ = Safety stock factor
- $C$ = Container size (units per container)

---

### 2.1.5 5S Methodology

**Definition:** A workplace organization technique creating an efficient, clean, and disciplined environment.

| S | Japanese | English | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seiri | Sort | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2 | Seiton | Set in Order | Arrange items for easy access |
| 3 | Seiso | Shine | Clean the workplace |
| 4 | Seiketsu | Standardize | Establish norms & schedules |
| 5 | Shitsuke | Sustain | Maintain discipline |

> [!TIP]
> A sixth "S" — **Shikari (Self-Discipline)** or **Safety** — is sometimes added in modern KTU-aligned curricula.

---

## 2.2 Statistical Process Control (SPC) in Manufacturing

SPC is the **backbone** of statistical quality control on the shop floor. It uses **control charts** to distinguish between:

- **Common Cause Variation** — inherent to the process (addressed by management).
- **Special Cause Variation** — external shocks (addressed by operators).

### Western Electric Rules (For Control Chart Interpretation)

1. One point beyond ±3σ (Zone A)
2. Two of three successive points in Zone A or beyond
3. Four of five successive points in Zone B or beyond
4. Eight successive points on one side of the centerline

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are recurring favorites in KTU ESE and module tests.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; assumes centered process |
| 2 | Process Capability (Shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Accounts for off-centering |
| 3 | Process Performance Index | $P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s}$ | Uses overall std. deviation |
| 4 | OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Expressed as a decimal/% |
| 5 | First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | Range: 0 to 1 |
| 6 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Product of n stage yields |
| 7 | DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities/Unit}}$ | Used in Six Sigma |
