| 8 | Sigma Level | $\sigma_L = 1.5 + Z_{\text{benchmark}}$ | 1.5σ shift convention |
| 9 | Number of Kanban Cards | $N = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$ | Round up to integer |
| 10 | Control Limits (X̄ chart) | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = grand mean |
| 11 | Control Limits (R chart) | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_4$ from constants table |
| 12 | Process Yield | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \text{Defect Rate}$ | Expressed as fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Notice how the absolute value notation has been avoided inside tables using the `\vert` and `\mid` commands where needed, preserving the markdown table structure. Within table cells, vertical bars are replaced with parenthesis-based notation for clarity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These tools are not academic exercises. They drive:

- **Automotive industry:** Toyota Production System, Ford assembly lines.
- **Electronics manufacturing:** Samsung, Foxconn's zero-defect initiatives.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA-compliant SPC and validation.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100 standard heavily relies on these tools.

> [!TIP]
> When answering KTU questions, always link the tool to its **industrial application context** — examiners reward this mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: OEE Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC machining cell operates for one 8-hour shift (480 minutes).
- **Availability Losses:** 42 minutes of downtime; 18 minutes of speed losses.
- **Performance Losses:** Ideal cycle time is 1.2 minutes/unit; actual is 1.4 minutes/unit. Total units produced = 280.
- **Quality Losses:** 14 units rejected as defective.

**Compute the OEE.**

### Step 1 — Compute Availability

$$
A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{480 - 42}{480} = \frac{438}{480} = 0.9125
$$

So Availability = **91.25%**.

### Step 2 — Compute Performance

First, find theoretical units at ideal cycle time:

$$
\text{Ideal Units} = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Ideal Cycle Time}} = \frac{438}{1.2} = 365 \text{ units}
$$

Now compute Performance:

$$
P = \frac{\text{Actual Units Produced}}{\text{Ideal Units}} = \frac{280}{365} = 0.7671
$$

So Performance = **76.71%**.

### Step 3 — Compute Quality

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} = \frac{280 - 14}{280} = \frac{266}{280} = 0.95
$$

So Quality = **95.00%**.

### Step 4 — Compute OEE

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9125 \times 0.7671 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7003 \times 0.95 = 0.6653
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} = 66.53\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** World-class OEE is **≥ 85%**. This cell is significantly underperforming and qualifies for a focused Kaizen project.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Kanban Card Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A subassembly line requires 200 units per day. The supplier lead time is 2 days. The safety factor is set at 0.20. Each container holds 25 units.

**Find the number of Kanban cards required.**

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

$$
D = 200 \text{ units/day}, \quad L = 2 \text{ days}, \quad S = 0.20, \quad C = 25 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C}
$$

$$
N = \frac{200 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.20)}{25}
$$

$$
N = \frac{200 \times 2 \times 1.20}{25} = \frac{480}{25} = 19.2
$$

### Step 3 — Round Up to Integer

$$
\boxed{N = 20 \text{ Kanban cards}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why round up and not down?** Rounding down would result in stock-outs. In JIT, availability > efficiency is the golden rule.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Process Capability Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A critical dimension has:
- **USL** = 12.05 mm, **LSL** = 11.95 mm
- Process mean $\mu$ = 11.99 mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma$ = 0.018 mm

**Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.**

### Step 1 — Compute $C_p$

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.95}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.10}{0.108}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_p = 0.926}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$

Compute the two component indices:

$$
C_{pk(\text{upper})} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.99}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.06}{0.054} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk(\text{lower})} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{11.99 - 11.95}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.04}{0.054} = 0.741
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111,\ 0.741) = 0.741
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = 0.741}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> Both $C_p$ and $C_{pk} < 1.00$ — the process is **incapable**. Additionally, $C_{pk} \ll C_p$ indicates the process is **off-center**. Immediate corrective action is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which TQM Tool

| Manufacturing Problem | Recommended TQM Tool | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent machine breakdowns | TPM, Autonomous Maintenance | Operator ownership reduces downtime |
| Excess inventory in warehouse | JIT, Kanban | Pull-based replenishment |
| High defect rate in assembly | Poka-Yoke, FMEA | Error prevention at source |
| Inconsistent product quality | SPC, Control Charts | Statistical monitoring |
| Cluttered, unsafe workplace | 5S | Foundation for all other tools |
| Long setup times | SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) | Lean manufacturing technique |
| Supplier quality issues | Vendor Certification, SQC | Incoming quality assurance |
| New product launch failures | QFD (House of Quality) | Captures voice of customer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JIT Pull System — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Order] --> B[Finished Goods Kanban]
    B --> C[Assembly Station]
    C --> D[Sub-Assembly Kanban]
    D --> E[Sub-Assembly Station]
    E --> F[Component Kanban]
    F --> G[Component Mfg Station]
    G --> H[Raw Material Withdrawal]
    H --> I[Supplier]
    I -- Replenishment Signal --> G

    classDef signal fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef station fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,color:#000000
    classDef external fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000

    class B,D,F signal
    class C,E,G station
    class I external
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each Kanban signal is a *trigger card*, not a stock movement. Material flows *forward*; information signals flow *backward*.

---

## 4.2 TPM 8-Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TPM[TOTAL PRODUCTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    TPM --> P1[1 Autonomous Maintenance]
    TPM --> P2[2 Planned Maintenance]
    TPM --> P3[3 Quality Maintenance]
    TPM --> P4[4 Focused Improvement]
    TPM --> P5[5 Early Equipment Management]
    TPM --> P6[6 Training and Education]
    TPM --> P7[7 Safety Health Environment]
    TPM --> P8[8 Office TPM]

    P1 --> G[Zero Downtime Goal]
    P2 --> G
    P3 --> G
    P4 --> G
    P5 --> G
    P6 --> G
    P7 --> G
    P8 --> G

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef pillar fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    classDef goal fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000

    class TPM root
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 pillar
    class G goal
```

---

## 4.3 5S Implementation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[1 Seiri Sort] --> S2[2 Seiton Set in Order]
    S2 --> S3[3 Seiso Shine]
    S3 --> S4[4 Seiketsu Standardize]
    S4 --> S5[5 Shitsuke Sustain]
    S5 --> AUDIT{Audit and Review}
    AUDIT -- Gaps Found --> S1
    AUDIT -- Compliant --> EXCEL[World Class Workplace]

    classDef sstep fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    classDef goal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 sstep
    class AUDIT decision
    class EXCEL goal
```

---

## 4.4 SPC Decision Flow for Operators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Sample Measurement] --> CALC[Compute X-bar and R]
    CALC --> CHECK{Point within Control Limits?}
    CHECK -- Yes --> TREND{Western Electric Rules Violated?}
    CHECK -- No --> OUT[OUT OF CONTROL Action Stop the Line]
    OUT --> ROOT[Root Cause Analysis 5 Whys]
    ROOT --> ACT[Implement Countermeasure]
    ACT --> DOC[Update Control Plan]
    DOC --> START

    TREND -- No --> ACCEPT[Process Stable Continue Production]
    TREND -- Yes --> OUT

    classDef normal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef action fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,color:#000000

    class START,CALC,ACCEPT normal
    class OUT,ROOT,ACT,DOC action
    class TREND process
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This flow mirrors the **PDCA cycle** embedded in SPC — examiners love to see this linkage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of Poka-Yoke and give any two manufacturing examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Poka-Yoke, a Japanese term coined by Shigeo Shingo, refers to the use of any device or mechanism that either **prevents an incorrect operation from being performed** or **immediately detects and signals an error** so that corrective action can be taken before defect generation.

Two manufacturing examples:
1. **Automotive Assembly:** A fixture ensures that a bolt can be inserted only when the bracket is in the correct orientation — preventing assembly error.
2. **Electronics SMT Line:** A sensor detects the absence of a component on a PCB and triggers an automatic line stop, preventing a defective board from moving forward.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the seven wastes (Muda) eliminated by Just-in-Time manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

The seven wastes eliminated by JIT are:
1. **Transport** — Unnecessary movement of materials.
2. **Inventory** — Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods.
3. **Motion** — Unnecessary operator movement.
4. **Waiting** — Idle time of workers or machines.
5. **Overproduction** — Producing more than required.
6. **Over-processing** — Adding features/processes the customer does not value.
7. **Defects** — Rework, scrap, and warranty costs.

(Tim Wood's classical model; some frameworks also include *unused human talent* as the eighth waste.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **eight pillars of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** with their significance in a manufacturing environment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process has the following data:
- Planned Production Time = 420 minutes
- Downtime = 30 minutes
- Total Units Produced = 350
- Defective Units = 25
- Ideal Cycle Time = 1.0 minute/unit

Compute the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a comprehensive maintenance philosophy that aims to achieve **zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents** through the involvement of all employees. It was developed by Seiichi Nakajima in 1971 based on preventive maintenance principles.

**The Eight Pillars:**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators are trained to perform routine maintenance tasks (cleaning, lubrication, inspection) on their own machines. This builds ownership and detects anomalies early.

2. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen):** A schedule-based preventive and predictive maintenance system aimed at eliminating unplanned breakdowns. Uses techniques like vibration analysis, thermography, and oil analysis.

3. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen):** Ensures that equipment conditions are maintained such that no quality defects are produced. Uses Poka-Yoke and condition-monitoring systems.

4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams target specific losses (breakdowns, defects, minor stops) and implement countermeasures systematically.

5. **Early Equipment Management (Early Management):** New equipment is designed for easy maintenance, reliability, and operability — incorporating lessons learned from existing machines.

6. **Training (Kyoiku):** Operators, technicians, and engineers are systematically trained in maintenance skills, problem-solving, and TPM methodology.

7. **Safety, Health, and Environment:** Aims to achieve zero industrial accidents and minimize environmental impact. Reinforces the principle that safe operations are also efficient operations.

8. **Office TPM:** Extends TPM principles to administrative and support functions (HR, finance, sales) to eliminate organizational losses.

**Significance:** TPM directly improves **OEE**, reduces **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**, and creates a culture of **ownership and continuous improvement** on the shop floor.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{420 - 30}{420} = \frac{390}{420} = 0.9286
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance:**

Operating time = 390 minutes.

$$
\text{Ideal Units} = \frac{390}{1.0} = 390 \text{ units}
$$

$$
P = \frac{350}{390} = 0.8974
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality:**

$$
Q = \frac{350 - 25}{350} = \frac{325}{350} = 0.9286
$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9286 \times 0.8974 \times 0.9286
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.8333 \times 0.9286 = 0.7738
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} = 77.38\%}
$$

**Comment:** A typical world-class OEE benchmark is **85% or above**. At 77.38%, the equipment is underperforming and requires a focused improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen) project — particularly targeting the **Performance** pillar, which is the weakest link (89.74%).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **5S methodology** with the Japanese and English terms. Describe how 5S acts as a foundation for other TQM tools. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Kanban system is to be designed for a manufacturing line. Demand rate is **300 units/day**, lead time is **1.5 days**, safety factor is **0.25**, and container size is **30 units**. Determine the number of Kanban cards. Also explain the difference between **Withdrawal Kanban** and **Production Kanban**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**5S Methodology** is a structured workplace organization technique that originated in the Toyota Production System. It creates an orderly, clean, and disciplined environment that is essential for quality, safety, and productivity.

**The Five Phases:**

| S | Japanese | English | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Identify and remove all items not needed for current operations. |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange remaining items for easy access and use — "a place for everything, and everything in its place." |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace thoroughly, including machines, floors, and tools. |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish schedules, checklists, and visual standards to maintain the first three S's. |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Develop the discipline and habits to follow the established standards continuously. |

**Role as Foundation:**

5S is often called the **"foundation stone"** of TQM in manufacturing because:

- **Visibility of problems:** A clean and organized workplace exposes abnormalities (oil leaks, missing tools) immediately.
- **Enables JIT:** Standardized locations allow fast, error-free retrieval — critical for pull systems.
- **Supports TPM:** Clean machines are easier to inspect, lubricate, and maintain.
- **Powers SPC:** Stable, standardized processes are pre-requisites for meaningful statistical control.
- **Safety prerequisite:** Cluttered floors are the leading cause of industrial accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students lose marks by describing 5S as mere "housekeeping." It is a **discipline-building system** that enables all other lean/TQM tools to function.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

$$
D = 300 \text{ units/day},\quad L = 1.5 \text{ days},\quad S = 0.25,\quad C = 30 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Kanban Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \times L \times (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{300 \times 1.5 \times 1.25}{30}
$$

$$
N = \frac{562.5}{30} = 18.75
$$

**Step 3 — Round Up:**

$$
\boxed{N = 19 \text{ Kanban cards}}
$$

**Difference Between Withdrawal and Production Kanban:**

| Aspect | Withdrawal Kanban (Conveyance) | Production Kanban (In-Process) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Authorizes the *withdrawal* of parts from the preceding process. | Authorizes the *production* of parts at the preceding process. |
| **Issued by** | Downstream process (the customer). | Upstream process (the supplier of parts). |
| **Information conveyed** | "I need X units of part P." | "Start producing X units of part P." |
| **Attached to** | A container in transit. | A container at the production station. |
| **Function** | Triggers material movement. | Triggers production activity. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the Kanban count (students often round 18.75 to 18 — this is **wrong** in JIT; you must round up to prevent stock-outs).
> 2. **Mixing up withdrawal and production kanban** — a common 2-mark deduction. Memorize: *Withdrawal = Material movement; Production = Manufacturing trigger*.
> 3. **OEE quality metric**: students sometimes count the *defective units* in the total production. Quality uses **good units ÷ total produced** (not including rejects in denominator).
> 4. **5S answers:** Avoid generic "cleanliness" answers. The examiner expects the **discipline and standardization** angle, plus linkage to JIT/TPM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications to Manufacturing**

- ✅ **JIT = Pull system** triggered by Kanban signals; eliminates the **7 wastes** (Tim Wood's model).
- ✅ **TPM** has **8 pillars**, all converging on **zero losses** (breakdowns, defects, accidents).
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q**; world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**; *if any factor < 90%, it is a candidate for improvement*.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** is classified as **Prevention-type** or **Detection-type**; designed by **Shigeo Shingo**.
- ✅ **Kanban formula** is $N = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$; **always round up** the result.
- ✅ **5S** = Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. It is the **foundation**, not the goal.
- ✅ **SPC** distinguishes **common cause** (process) from **special cause** (external) variation; use **Western Electric Rules** for trend detection.
- ✅ **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$**: $C_p$ assumes centering; $C_{pk}$ accounts for **off-center** processes. $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ always.
- ✅ **Process is capable** when $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (industry minimum); $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is **Six Sigma** territory.
- ✅ **Jidoka** (autonomation) — Toyota's principle: stop the line when abnormality is detected, never let defects pass.
- ✅ **Andon** — visual signal (light/board) that alerts the team of line issues, enabling rapid response.
- ✅ **Heijunka** — leveling of production volume and mix to avoid peaks and valleys.
- ✅ **Continuous Flow Manufacturing** is the ultimate goal: one-piece flow, zero WIP, zero lead time.
- ✅ Always remember: **TQM in manufacturing is not about one tool — it is about the integrated system** of philosophy + tools + culture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "discuss" a TQM tool, structure your answer as: **Definition → Originator → Principles → Steps/Pillars → Industrial Example → Linkage to other TQM tools**. This is the format rewarded highest in KTU 2024 valuation schemes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service sector including IT - Benchmarking - Reason to benchmark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Service sector including IT - Benchmarking - Reason to benchmark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 | SERVICE SECTOR & BENCHMARKING

## 1.1 The Service Sector — A Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **service sector** is the segment of the economy that delivers **intangible value** to customers through activities, processes, and interactions rather than through the production of physical goods. The **Information Technology (IT) service sector** is a specialized sub-domain where value is created through software, data, networking, consulting, and managed services.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine walking into a restaurant. You do **not** take a physical "food object" home in the same way you take a phone from a shop. Instead, you pay for an **experience** — taste, ambience, service speed, hygiene, and the chef's skill. That experience cannot be touched, stored, or returned. **This is the essence of a service.**

Similarly, when a software company builds a custom ERP system for a bank, the bank is not just buying code. It is buying **reliability, uptime, security, expertise, and peace of mind.** That bundle of value is the IT service.

### 1.1.2 The Four Universal Characteristics of Services (IHIP Model)

Every service — be it banking, healthcare, hospitality, or IT — shares **four canonical characteristics** taught across the KTU TQM curriculum:

> [!NOTE]
> **IHIP Model — The 4 Pillars of Service Quality**

| # | Characteristic | Meaning | IT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Intangibility** | Service cannot be seen, touched, or stored before purchase | A software license is a paper; the actual *protection* is intangible |
| 2 | **Heterogeneity (Variability)** | Quality varies from provider to provider, hour to hour | Bug fixes vary by developer skill |
| 3 | **Inseparability** | Production and consumption happen *simultaneously* | Cloud server downtime hits the user instantly |
| 4 | **Perishability** | Unused service capacity is lost forever | An empty seat on a flight cannot be resold after take-off |

### 1.1.3 IT Services — Special Characteristics

IT services layer **five additional traits** on top of IHIP:

1. **Knowledge-Intensity** — Delivered by highly skilled engineers.
2. **Rapid Obsolescence** — Technologies evolve every 12–18 months.
3. **Global Delivery Model** — 24×7 follow-the-sun support.
4. **High Customization** — Tailored SLAs per client.
5. **Measurability through SLAs** — Quality tracked via **Service Level Agreements**, with metrics like **Mean Time to Recover (MTTR)** of typically **≤ 30 minutes**, **uptime ≥ 99.9%**, and **First Call Resolution (FCR) ≥ 80%**.

---

## 1.2 Benchmarking — The Core TQM Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard — Dr. Robert C. Camp's Classic Definition):**
> *"Benchmarking is the **continuous, systematic process of measuring and comparing an organization's business processes against the best-in-class performers**, whether inside or outside its industry, to gather information that helps the organization improve its performance."*

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Fitness Tracker Story

Think of a student preparing for the KTU semester exam. She notices her friend scored **92%** in the previous semester on the same subject. She does **not copy the friend**. Instead, she investigates:
- *How many hours did the friend study per day?*
- *Which books and question banks were used?*
- *What mock-test strategy was followed?*

She then **compares her own study process** against her friend's, identifies the gap, and adopts the superior practices. **This entire exercise is Benchmarking.**

In a corporate setting, if an IT company like Infosys benchmarks itself against TCS on *employee retention rate*, it discovers that TCS runs a superior mentorship program. Infosys then studies and adapts that program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> Benchmarking is **NOT** spying or copying. It is a **legal, ethical, and structured learning process** aimed at continuous improvement — perfectly aligned with **Deming's PDCA cycle** and **Juran's Quality Trilogy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The 4 Most-Cited Types of Benchmarking

In the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to identify and explain **four canonical types** of benchmarking:

### Type 1 — Internal Benchmarking
- **What:** Comparing similar processes **within the same organization** (e.g., two branches of a bank).
- **Why:** Easiest to execute; data is accessible.
- **Example:** Comparing the resolution time of the Kochi IT helpdesk vs. the Chennai IT helpdesk within the same firm.

### Type 2 — Competitive Benchmarking
- **What:** Comparing directly **against direct competitors**.
- **Why:** Reveals market position.
- **Example:** TCS comparing its defect leakage rate with Infosys.

### Type 3 — Functional Benchmarking
- **What:** Comparing **similar functions** in **different industries**.
- **Why:** Reveals best practices transferable across sectors.
- **Example:** A hospital studying Disney's queue management system for OPD patient flow.

### Type 4 — Generic Benchmarking
- **What:** Comparing **business processes** that are **functionally similar** regardless of industry.
- **Why:** Identifies world-class processes.
- **Example:** A logistics company studying FedEx's package tracking system to design its own GPS-based farm produce delivery.

---

## 2.2 The Benchmarking Process — The 5-Phase Xerox/SPACE Model

KTU examiners heavily favor the **Robert Camp's 5-Phase Benchmarking Model** (also called the **Xerox Model**), originally developed at Xerox Corporation in 1979.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Phases of Benchmarking (KTU Board Examination Favourite):**
> 1. **P**lan
> 2. **A**nalyze
> 3. **I**ntegrate
> 4. **A**ct
> 5. **M**aturity (Evaluate)

---

## 2.3 REASONS TO BENCHMARK — The Heart of This Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU ESE, this is a **14-mark favorite** sub-question. Students should be able to list **at least 8 to 10 reasons** with one-line justification each.

Below is the **exhaustive, KTU-aligned list of reasons** to benchmark in the service and IT sector:

### Reason 1 — To Identify Performance Gaps
Benchmarking reveals **the delta between "where we are" and "where the best are."** Without benchmarking, organizations operate with a false sense of efficiency.

### Reason 2 — To Learn from Best-in-Class Practices
Instead of **re-inventing the wheel**, the organization studies proven practices. This **reduces trial-and-error cost** and accelerates learning curves.

### Reason 3 — To Achieve Competitive Advantage
By adopting superior practices **faster than competitors**, the organization creates a **differential value proposition** in the marketplace.

### Reason 4 — To Set Realistic and Stretch Goals
Internal estimates are often **biased or conservative**. Benchmarking against industry leaders provides **objective, data-driven targets**.

### Reason 5 — To Drive Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
Benchmarking is the **engine of TQM**. It feeds the **PDCA cycle** with fresh external data, preventing complacency and stagnation.

### Reason 6 — To Improve Customer Satisfaction
Best-in-class practices in IT — like **24×7 multilingual support, predictive issue resolution, and personalized dashboards** — when adopted, directly translate into **higher CSAT and NPS scores**.

### Reason 7 — To Reduce Costs
Many benchmarking studies reveal that **world-class organizations achieve the same output at 30–50% lower cost** by eliminating non-value-adding activities (Muda).

### Reason 8 — To Enhance Process Efficiency
Benchmarking **streamlines workflows**, reduces cycle time, and identifies **bottlenecks** that internal audits often miss.

### Reason 9 — To Foster a Culture of Learning
Benchmarking instills a **mindset of "there is always a better way"** across employees, breaking silos and encouraging cross-functional collaboration.

### Reason 10 — To Support Strategic Decision-Making
Benchmarking provides **hard data** for strategic choices such as market expansion, technology adoption, and **vendor selection**.

### Reason 11 — To Improve Quality of Products and Services
By studying how top performers ensure quality, organizations can **elevate their own quality benchmarks**, reducing defect density and rework.

### Reason 12 — To Standardize Best Practices
Benchmarking helps convert **individual brilliance into organizational DNA** — a key tenet of Juran's quality planning.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Benchmarking Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are frequently asked as **3-mark derivation/numerical** questions in KTU ESE.

$$
\text{Performance Gap (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Our Performance} - \text{Best-in-Class Performance}}{\text{Best-in-Class Performance}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Benchmarking ROI (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Tangible Savings from Benchmarking}}{\text{Cost of Benchmarking Exercise}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Process Cycle Efficiency (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Defect Density (per KLOC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Kilo Lines of Code}}
$$

$$
\text{CSAT Score (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Sum of Customer Ratings}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}} \right) \times 100
$$

| Metric | Symbol | Typical World-Class Target | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance Gap | PG | $\leq$ 5% | All industries |
| Benchmarking ROI | ROI | $\geq$ 300% | Cost-reduction projects |
| Process Cycle Efficiency | PCE | $\geq$ 25% | Service operations |
| Defect Density | DD | $\leq$ 0.5 per KLOC | IT/Software |
| CSAT Score | CSAT | $\geq$ 90% | Customer service |
| First Call Resolution | FCR | $\geq$ 80% | IT helpdesks |
| Mean Time to Recover | MTTR | $\leq$ 30 min | IT operations |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the IT service sector, benchmarking is the **backbone of CMMI and ISO 20000 certifications**. Companies use benchmarking to:
- Justify **process improvement investments** to the board.
- Negotiate **SLAs with clients** using industry-standard data.
- Win **government and global tenders** where world-class compliance is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing 4 Benchmarking Types

The following matrix is a **frequent KTU 14-mark question**. Each cell is a **model answer point**.

| Dimension | Internal | Competitive | Functional | Generic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Within company | Direct rivals | Similar function in another industry | Best process anywhere in the world |
| **Ease of Data Collection** | Very Easy | Difficult | Moderate | Difficult |
| **Cost of Study** | Low | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Innovation Potential** | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Strategic Value** | Low–Moderate | High | High | Very High |
| **Risk of Disclosure** | None | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical IT Example** | Chennai vs. Bengaluru branch SLA | TCS vs. Infosys | IT firm studying airline reservation system | Studying Amazon's logistics algorithm |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Application — Computing Performance Gap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical — KTU ESE Frequently Asked Style**

### Problem Statement
An Indian IT firm's **First Call Resolution (FCR)** rate is **72%**. After a benchmarking study, it discovers that the **industry best-in-class** firm has an FCR of **90%**. The firm wants to compute its performance gap, set a 12-month target, and measure expected CSAT improvement.

### Step 1 — Establish the Current State
The IT firm notes its FCR = **72%**. This is the *As-Is* baseline.

### Step 2 — Establish the Benchmark
Industry best-in-class FCR = **90%**. This is the *To-Be* target.

### Step 3 — Apply the Performance Gap Formula
$$
\text{Performance Gap (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Our Performance} - \text{Best-in-Class Performance}}{\text{Best-in-Class Performance}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Performance Gap (\%)} = \left( \frac{72 - 90}{90} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Performance Gap (\%)} = \left( \frac{-18}{90} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Performance Gap (\%)} = -20\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm is **20% below** the world-class benchmark. The negative sign indicates *underperformance*, not a deficit in the sense of subtraction.

### Step 4 — Set the 12-Month Stretch Target
Using **SMART goals**, the firm commits to reaching **85% FCR** in 12 months — a **13 percentage point** improvement. This is realistic because jumping from 72% directly to 90% in one year is statistically unlikely; phased targets are accepted best practice.

### Step 5 — Compute Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)
Suppose the firm's current value-added time is **2 hours** and total cycle time is **10 hours**.

$$
\text{PCE (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{PCE (\%)} = \left( \frac{2}{10} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{PCE (\%)} = 20\%
$$

The world-class PCE in IT service desks is **≥ 25%**. Hence the gap is **5 percentage points**.

### Step 6 — Compute Benchmarking ROI
Suppose the benchmarking project costs **₹10,00,000** and is expected to yield **₹40,00,000** in annual savings through reduced escalations, lower churn, and SLA bonuses.

$$
\text{Benchmarking ROI (\%)} = \left( \frac{40,00,000}{10,00,000} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Benchmarking ROI (\%)} = 400\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A **400% ROI** is exceptional and strongly justifies the benchmarking investment to the board of directors.

### Step 7 — Translate into Customer Satisfaction Impact
Industry studies show a **+1% improvement in FCR** typically delivers a **+0.8% lift in CSAT** in IT helpdesks.

$$
\text{CSAT Lift (\%)} = 13 \times 0.8 = 10.4\%
$$

If current CSAT is **78%**, expected new CSAT = **78 + 10.4 = 88.4%** — crossing the **world-class 88% threshold**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reasons Mapped to TQM Gurus

| Reason to Benchmark | TQM Guru Linkage |
|---|---|
| Continuous Improvement | **Deming** (PDCA Cycle) |
| Customer Satisfaction | **Juran** (Quality Trilogy) |
| Cost Reduction (Muda) | **Ohno/Toyota** (Lean) |
| Process Standardization | **Crosby** (Quality is Free) |
| Defect Prevention | **Ishikawa** (Fishbone / 7QC Tools) |
| Strategic Quality Planning | **Juran** again |

This matrix shows that **Benchmarking is not a standalone technique** — it is the **integrating tissue** of all TQM philosophies.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The 5-Phase Benchmarking Cycle (Camp's Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[phase1: Plan] --> B[phase2: Analyze]
    B --> C[phase3: Integrate]
    C --> D[phase4: Act]
    D --> E[phase5: Maturity]
    E --> A
    A1[Identify what to benchmark] --> A
    A2[Select benchmarking partners] --> A
    B1[Collect internal performance data] --> B
    B2[Collect partner performance data] --> B
    C1[Identify performance gaps] --> C
    C2[Communicate findings] --> C
    D1[Develop action plans] --> D
    D2[Implement improvements] --> D
    E1[Monitor progress] --> E
    E2[Recalibrate benchmarks] --> E
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The 4 Types of Benchmarking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BENCH[Benchmarking] --> INTERNAL[Internal Benchmarking]
    BENCH --> COMPETITIVE[Competitive Benchmarking]
    BENCH --> FUNCTIONAL[Functional Benchmarking]
    BENCH --> GENERIC[Generic Benchmarking]
    INTERNAL --> I1[Within same organization]
    INTERNAL --> I2[Easy data access]
    COMPETITIVE --> C1[Direct industry rivals]
    COMPETITIVE --> C2[High strategic value]
    FUNCTIONAL --> F1[Cross industry similar function]
    FUNCTIONAL --> F2[Moderate innovation]
    GENERIC --> G1[Best process anywhere]
    GENERIC --> G2[Very high innovation]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — IT Service Sector Benchmarking Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[IT Service Organization] --> S1[Identify Critical IT Process: e.g. Incident Management]
    S1 --> S2[Define KPIs: MTTR, FCR, CSAT]
    S2 --> S3[Collect Internal Data]
    S3 --> S4[Identify Benchmark Partner: e.g. Industry Leader]
    S4 --> S5[Collect Partner Data via Surveys, Site Visits, Public Reports]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Performance Gap]
    S6 --> S7{Gap Greater than 10 Percent}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Initiate Process Improvement Project]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Maintain and Monitor]
    S8 --> S10[Implement Best Practice via PDCA]
    S10 --> S11[Measure Improvement]
    S11 --> S12[Document Learning into SOPs]
    S12 --> S13[Continuous Benchmarking Loop]
    S9 --> S13
    S13 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Why Benchmark in the Service Sector? (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CORE[Benchmarking in Service Sector] --> R1[Identify Performance Gaps]
    CORE --> R2[Learn Best Practices]
    CORE --> R3[Competitive Advantage]
    CORE --> R4[Realistic Goal Setting]
    CORE --> R5[Continuous Improvement Culture]
    CORE --> R6[Customer Satisfaction Boost]
    CORE --> R7[Cost Reduction]
    CORE --> R8[Process Efficiency]
    CORE --> R9[Strategic Decision Support]
    CORE --> R10[Quality Standardization]
    R1 --> OUT[Superior Service Delivery]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
    R6 --> OUT
    R7 --> OUT
    R8 --> OUT
    R9 --> OUT
    R10 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define benchmarking. Mention any two reasons why a service organization should adopt benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's business processes and performance metrics against industry best-in-class performers to identify gaps and adopt superior practices.

**Two Reasons:**
1. **To Identify Performance Gaps** — Benchmarking objectively reveals the delta between the organization's current performance and the best in the industry, enabling data-driven improvement.
2. **To Achieve Competitive Advantage** — By learning and implementing best practices faster than rivals, the service organization gains a strategic edge in the marketplace.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for the two reasons.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four characteristics of the service sector with one-line IT-based examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Intangibility** — Cloud computing service is experienced through access, not ownership of hardware.
> 2. **Heterogeneity** — Bug-fix quality varies across different software developers in the same IT firm.
> 3. **Inseparability** — Server downtime and customer impact occur simultaneously.
> 4. **Perishability** — An unsold cloud-compute hour cannot be retrieved once the window closes.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the four major types of benchmarking with suitable IT sector examples.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The four major types of benchmarking are:

1. **Internal Benchmarking** — Comparison of similar processes *within the same organization*. Example: Comparing the average resolution time of the Hyderabad IT helpdesk against the Bengaluru IT helpdesk of the same firm. Since data is internal, it is the easiest and cheapest to perform.

2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — Comparison *against direct business rivals*. Example: Wipro comparing its employee attrition rate against Infosys. This provides strategic market intelligence but is harder to gather due to confidentiality.

3. **Functional Benchmarking** — Comparison of *similar functions across different industries*. Example: A banking IT firm studying the queue management system of an airline to optimize its own customer onboarding flow.

4. **Generic Benchmarking** — Comparison of *world-class processes irrespective of industry*. Example: A logistics IT startup studying Amazon's tracking algorithm to design last-mile delivery software. This offers the highest innovation potential.

**(b) Discuss the reasons why service organizations, including IT firms, should engage in benchmarking.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Service organizations must benchmark for the following reasons:

1. **Performance Gap Identification** — Benchmarking exposes the actual gap between current and best-in-class performance, eliminating blind spots.
2. **Learning from Best Practices** — Avoids re-invention; organizations adopt proven methods instead of experimenting.
3. **Competitive Advantage** — Faster adoption of best practices yields marketplace differentiation.
4. **Realistic Goal Setting** — Industry-derived targets are more objective than internally biased estimates.
5. **Continuous Improvement Culture** — Reinforces the PDCA mindset, preventing organizational complacency.
6. **Customer Satisfaction Improvement** — Adopted practices (e.g., 24×7 support, predictive issue resolution) directly elevate CSAT scores.
7. **Cost Reduction and ROI** — Benchmarking typically yields 300–400% ROI by eliminating waste and rework.
8. **Strategic Decision Support** — Provides data for decisions on technology adoption, market expansion, and SLA design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Alternate Choice — KTU Model]`

**(a) Explain Robert Camp's 5-Phase Benchmarking model with a neat diagram.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Robert Camp of Xerox developed the 5-Phase Benchmarking Model in 1979. The phases are:

1. **Plan** — Identify the process to benchmark, select partners, and define data-collection methods.
2. **Analyze** — Collect internal and external performance data, then compute the performance gap.
3. **Integrate** — Communicate findings, gain leadership buy-in, and set improvement goals.
4. **Act** — Develop and implement action plans via PDCA cycles.
5. **Maturity** — After successful integration, the practice becomes the new organizational baseline, and the cycle restarts at a higher benchmark.

*(Draw the cyclic flow diagram as shown in SECTION 4.1 — 1 mark reserved for diagram.)*

**(b) "Benchmarking is the engine of TQM." Justify with five reasons specifically applicable to the IT service sector.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Drives Quality in Software Delivery** — Benchmarking defect density (≤ 0.5/KLOC) pushes IT firms toward CMMI Level 5 maturity.
2. **Improves SLA Performance** — Benchmarking MTTR, uptime (≥ 99.9%), and FCR (≥ 80%) ensures contractual compliance and client retention.
3. **Reduces Project Rework** — Best-practice study minimizes defects at the requirements stage itself, saving 30–40% rework cost.
4. **Accelerates Digital Transformation** — Studying how global leaders deploy AI/ML/Cloud helps IT firms fast-track their own transformation roadmaps.
5. **Boosts Customer Retention (NPS)** — IT helpdesks that benchmark CSAT against world-class firms (≥ 90%) consistently outperform peers in long-term client retention.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Benchmarking with TQM itself** — Benchmarking is a *tool/technique* within TQM, not a synonym. Students lose 1–2 marks when they equate the two.
> 2. **Omitting Real-World IT Examples** — KTU 2024 examiners explicitly reward IT-context examples. A generic answer without an IT-specific illustration loses at least 1 mark per sub-question.
> 3. **Skipping the Diagram in 14-mark Answers** — A 14-mark answer on Camp's Model without the 5-phase cycle diagram forfeits **2 marks** reserved for visual presentation.
> 4. **Listing Reasons Without Justification** — Simply naming 10 reasons without a one-line rationale each earns only 4–5 out of 7. Examiners want *applied reasoning*, not bullet-dumping.
> 5. **Wrong Formula Usage** — In numerical questions, students frequently write *absolute difference* instead of the *percentage gap formula*. This leads to zero marks for the calculation step.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Service Sector & Benchmarking**

- **Service Sector = IHIP** — Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability. Commit this acronym to memory.
- **IT Services Add 5 More Traits** — Knowledge-intensity, rapid obsolescence, global delivery, customization, SLA measurability.
- **Benchmarking Definition** — Robert Camp's classic definition is the KTU gold standard. Memorize verbatim.
- **4 Types of Benchmarking** — Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic. Each must be tied to an IT example.
- **5-Phase Camp Model** — Plan → Analyze → Integrate → Act → Maturity. Always draw the cycle.
- **12 Reasons to Benchmark** — Performance gaps, learning, competitive advantage, goal setting, continuous improvement, customer satisfaction, cost reduction, process efficiency, learning culture, strategic decisions, quality improvement, standardization.
- **Key Formulas** — Performance Gap %, Benchmarking ROI, PCE, Defect Density, CSAT, FCR, MTTR.
- **TQM Guru Linkages** — Deming (PDCA), Juran (Trilogy), Crosby (Quality is Free), Ishikawa (7QC), Ohno (Lean).
- **Exam-Heavy Numbers** — FCR ≥ 80%, CSAT ≥ 90%, MTTR ≤ 30 min, Uptime ≥ 99.9%, Defect Density ≤ 0.5/KLOC.
- **Avoid Pitfalls** — Benchmarking ≠ TQM; IT examples are mandatory; diagrams carry reserved marks.

> *"Without a standard, there is no quality. Without benchmarking, there is no standard."* — Adapted from Robert Camp
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking process - FMEA - Stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking process - FMEA - Stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Benchmarking — Formal Definition

**Benchmarking** is a structured, continuous, and systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and practices against those of recognized industry leaders or best-in-class competitors, with the objective of identifying performance gaps and implementing improvements to achieve superior quality and competitive advantage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education), benchmarking is treated as a **strategic quality diagnostic tool** that transforms tacit knowledge from external sources into actionable internal improvements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Benchmarking is *not* a one-time event — it is a **cyclical, continuous improvement** mechanism, structurally similar to the Deming PDCA cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Improvement" Intuition

Imagine you love cooking pasta at home. You follow your grandmother's recipe, but the pasta never tastes as good as the one served at your favourite Italian restaurant. Instead of guessing, you:

1. **Plan** — Visit the restaurant and observe how they cook.
2. **Collect Data** — Note their ingredients, boiling time, salt level, plating style.
3. **Analyze the Gap** — Compare their method to yours (gap = difference).
4. **Implement** — Change your recipe based on what you learned.
5. **Maturity** — Make it a habit; revisit and improve whenever a better chef emerges.

This is **benchmarking in plain English**: learning from the *best*, then **measurably improving** yourself.

---

## 1.2 FMEA — Formal Definition

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a proactive, systematic, team-based engineering methodology used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate potential failure modes of a product, process, or system *before* they occur in the field, thereby enhancing reliability, safety, and customer satisfaction.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pre-Flight Safety Check"

Before an aircraft takes off, the pilot and ground crew do not wait for an engine to fail mid-air. They:
1. **List every component** that *could* fail (fuel pump, altimeter, landing gear).
2. **Estimate how bad** each failure would be (severity).
3. **Estimate how often** it might happen (occurrence).
4. **Estimate how easily** it could be detected before flight (detection).
5. **Prioritize fixes** based on the combined risk.

FMEA is exactly this — an **engineering pre-flight safety check** for products, services, or processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core FMEA Idea:** Multiply three numbers — **Severity × Occurrence × Detection** — to obtain the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**. Higher RPN = higher priority for corrective action.

---

## 1.3 Three Canonical Types of FMEA

| # | Type | Scope | When Applied |
|---|------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Whole system & subsystems | Concept / architecture stage |
| 2 | **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design features | Detailed design stage |
| 3 | **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing / service process | Process planning & control stage |

---

## 1.4 Why These Topics Matter in TQM (KTU Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **CO Mapping Hint (PEEIT754):** Benchmarking and FMEA directly support the course outcomes relating to *quality tools*, *preventive quality management*, and *continuous improvement strategy*. Questions on RPN calculation and benchmarking phases are **high-frequency** in KTU ESE papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Benchmarking Process — Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model

The most widely accepted framework (and the one expected in KTU answers) is **Robert C. Camp's Five-Phase Benchmarking Model (1989)**. Each phase has a clear input, activity, and output.

### Phase 1 — Planning

- Identify the **subject** (what to benchmark — e.g., order fulfillment time).
- Identify **benchmarking partners** (competitors, industry leaders).
- Define **performance metrics** (cost, speed, defect rate, NPS score).
- Form the **benchmarking team**.
- Decide the **data collection method**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Question in Phase 1:** *"What do we want to improve, and who is best at doing it?"*

### Phase 2 — Analysis

- Collect data on the partner organizations.
- Compute the **performance gap** between your organization and the best-in-class.
- Root-cause the gap using tools like **fishbone / 5-Whys**.
- Establish **future performance targets** that are realistic and ambitious.

### Phase 3 — Integration

- Communicate findings to **senior leadership** and secure buy-in.
- Translate findings into **SMART goals** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
- Embed targets into the **strategic plan** and operating budgets.

### Phase 4 — Action

- Develop an **implementation plan** with milestones.
- Pilot the changes on a small scale (PDCA).
- Monitor **key performance indicators (KPIs)** continuously.
- Standardize successful improvements through **SOPs and training**.

### Phase 5 — Maturity

- Establish a **culture of continuous benchmarking**.
- Update benchmarks regularly as leaders change.
- Conduct **post-implementation reviews**.
- Recognize that *“best practice today is average practice tomorrow.”*

### Mermaid — Benchmarking Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Phase 1 Planning"]
    p2["Phase 2 Analysis"]
    p3["Phase 3 Integration"]
    p4["Phase 4 Action"]
    p5["Phase 5 Maturity"]
    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5 --> p1
```

---

## 2.2 Benchmarking Code of Conduct (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Principle | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **Principle 1** | Legality — never obtain data through illegal means |
| **Principle 2** | Ethics — avoid practices that would harm the partner |
| **Principle 3** | Independence — fund one's own study, no paid endorsements |
| **Principle 4** | Exchange / mutual benefit — protect confidential data |
| **Principle 5** | Preparation — thorough planning before contact |
| **Principle 6** | Documentation — record data accurately and source them properly |
| **Principle 7** | Confidentiality — protect partner's identity if requested |

---

## 2.3 The FMEA Methodology — Deep Dive

FMEA follows a **stepwise structured flow**. Each step has a clearly defined deliverable.

### Step 1 — Identify the Scope & Assemble the Team

- Define the system / product / process boundary.
- Assemble a **cross-functional team** (design, manufacturing, quality, service, customer).
- Reference documents: customer requirements, specs, prior failures.

### Step 2 — Identify Potential Failure Modes

- A **Failure Mode** is the manner in which a component, subsystem, or process *could fail* to meet its intended function.
- Examples: *bearing seizes*, *weld cracks*, *invoice delayed by 7 days*.

### Step 3 — Determine the Effects

- The **Effect** is the consequence of the failure on the customer, downstream operation, or system.
- Quantify in customer-impact language.

### Step 4 — Determine the Severity (S)

- Severity rating of the failure effect on a **1–10 scale** (10 = highest, hazardous).
- Severity is **always non-zero** if a failure exists.

### Step 5 — Determine the Occurrence (O)

- Likelihood that the failure mode *will occur*.
- Rated on a **1–10 scale** based on historical data, supplier data, or team estimate.

### Step 6 — Determine the Detection (D)

- Probability that the *current control* will **fail to detect** the failure mode before it reaches the customer.
- Rating of **10 = lowest detectability** (worst), 1 = highly detectable.

### Step 7 — Compute the Risk Priority Number (RPN)

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

### Step 8 — Prioritize & Recommend Action

- Items with **highest RPN** are acted upon first.
- Typical threshold: **RPN > 100** OR severity alone $\geq 9$ triggers mandatory action.

### Step 9 — Re-evaluate (Closure)

- After corrective action, recompute RPN to confirm reduction.
- Document updated ratings for traceability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Range / Value |
|---------------|------------|------------------------|
| $S$ | Severity of failure effect | $1$ to $10$ |
| $O$ | Occurrence (likelihood) | $1$ to $10$ |
| $D$ | Detection difficulty | $1$ to $10$ |
| $\text{RPN}$ | Risk Priority Number | $1$ to $1000$ |
| Threshold | Action trigger | $\text{RPN} > 100$ or $S \geq 9$ |
| $\text{Pareto Rule}$ | Top 20% modes cause 80% of RPN | Used for prioritization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** $S$, $O$, $D$ are dimensionless integers. The maximum possible RPN = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$. Always present RPN as a numeric value with the action threshold cited.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Boeing uses FMEA + FMEACA (Criticality Analysis) on every airframe.
- **Automotive:** Mandatory DFMEA + PFMEA per AIAG-VDA Handbook (post-2019).
- **Healthcare:** FMEA applied to medication-dispensing, surgical processes (reduces sentinel events).
- **Software:** Adapted as **FMEAplus** in DevOps and CI/CD pipelines.
- **Service Sector:** Applied to call-centre workflows, banking processes, and IT incident management.

Benchmarking and FMEA together form the **diagnostic–preventive twin pillars** of modern TQM — one tells you *where to aim*, the other *what to fix first*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RPN Derivation & Worked Numerical

The **RPN is not derived** mathematically — it is a **composite scoring metric** synthesized from three independent ordinal ratings. We will, however, derive its **bounds, expected value, and prioritization logic**.

### 3.1.1 Lower Bound of RPN

$$
S_{\min} = 1,\quad O_{\min} = 1,\quad D_{\min} = 1
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1
$$

A failure mode with negligible severity, near-zero occurrence, and trivially detectable → RPN = 1 → lowest priority.

### 3.1.2 Upper Bound of RPN

$$
S_{\max} = 10,\quad O_{\max} = 10,\quad D_{\max} = 10
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
$$

Catastrophic severity, frequent occurrence, and undetectable failure → RPN = 1000 → top priority for corrective action.

### 3.1.3 Expected RPN

Assuming each rating is uniformly distributed on $\{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$:

$$
E[S] = E[O] = E[D] = \frac{1+10}{2} = 5.5
$$

$$
E[\text{RPN}] = E[S] \cdot E[O] \cdot E[D] = 5.5 \times 5.5 \times 5.5 = 166.375
$$

$$
E[\text{RPN}] \approx 166.4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An average failure mode in a typical FMEA carries an RPN around 166. Items significantly above this value warrant top-priority corrective action.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A gear shaft in a transmission has the following FMEA ratings:
- Severity $S = 9$ (loss of vehicle control)
- Occurrence $O = 4$ (rare — 1 in 8000 vehicles)
- Detection $D = 6$ (visual inspection has 60% miss rate)

**Step 1 — Compute RPN**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 6
$$

$$
\text{RPN} = 216
$$

**Step 2 — Decision Logic**

- Severity $S = 9 \geq 9$ → action **mandatory regardless of RPN**.
- RPN $216 > 100$ → triggers corrective action.
- Recommended actions: (i) increase inspection frequency, (ii) redesign for built-in detectability (e.g., smart sensors), (iii) supplier audit.

**Step 3 — Post-Action Re-rating (Assume)** $S = 9$, $O = 2$, $D = 3$:

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54
$$

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{216 - 54}{216} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

A **75% RPN reduction** confirms the corrective action was effective.

---

## 3.2 FMEA RPN Analytical Matrix (Sample Worksheet)

| Item | Failure Mode | Effect | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ | $\text{RPN}$ | Action |
|------|--------------|--------|-----|-----|-----|--------------|--------|
| Brake Pad | Premature wear | Reduced stopping distance | 9 | 3 | 4 | **108** | Add wear sensor |
| Engine Oil | Contamination | Engine seizure | 10 | 2 | 5 | **100** | Improved filtration |
| Infotainment | Screen freeze | Customer dissatisfaction | 4 | 5 | 6 | **120** | Software patch |
| Door Lock | Failure to lock | Safety hazard | 8 | 2 | 3 | **48** | Routine check |
| Battery | Low charge alert failure | Stranded vehicle | 7 | 4 | 7 | **196** | Dual sensor |

**Top priority:** Battery monitoring failure (RPN = 196) — even though severity is moderate, poor detection pushes it to the top.

---

## 3.3 Benchmarking Gap Analysis Derivation

Let your company's performance metric be $P_y$ and the benchmark partner's be $P_b$.

### 3.3.1 Absolute Gap

$$
G_{\text{abs}} = P_y - P_b
$$

A *negative* $G_{\text{abs}}$ means the partner performs better (assuming higher = better metric).

### 3.3.2 Percentage Gap

$$
G_{\%} = \frac{P_y - P_b}{P_b} \times 100
$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Example

Your company resolves customer complaints in **36 hours** on average; the industry leader resolves in **12 hours**.

$$
G_{\text{abs}} = 36 - 12 = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
G_{\%} = \frac{36 - 12}{12} \times 100 = 200\%
$$

You take **3× longer** than the leader — strong case for process redesign and CRM automation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Benchmarking — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Trigger Performance Gap Detected"]
    plan["Phase 1 Planning Identify Subject and Partner"]
    collect["Phase 2 Analysis Collect and Compare Data"]
    integrate["Phase 3 Integration Communicate and Set Goals"]
    act["Phase 4 Action Implement Improvements Pilot"]
    mature["Phase 5 Maturity Institutionalize and Reiterate"]
    review["Continuous Performance Review"]
    start --> plan --> collect --> integrate --> act --> mature --> review
    review --> plan
```

**Flow Interpretation:**
1. A performance shortfall or strategic aspiration triggers benchmarking.
2. Planning determines scope and partner.
3. Analysis converts raw data into a measurable gap.
4. Integration elevates findings into executive-level goals.
5. Action implements changes using PDCA.
6. Maturity embeds the practice organization-wide.
7. Review loops back to Phase 1 — completing the **closed-loop TQM cycle**.

---

## 4.2 FMEA — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1 Define Scope and Form Team"]
    s2["Step 2 Identify Failure Modes"]
    s3["Step 3 Determine Effects"]
    s4["Step 4 Rate Severity S"]
    s5["Step 5 Rate Occurrence O"]
    s6["Step 6 Rate Detection D"]
    s7["Step 7 Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    s8["Step 8 Prioritize by RPN and Severity"]
    s9["Step 9 Recommend Corrective Action"]
    s10["Step 10 Re evaluate and Close Item"]
    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10
    s10 -.feedback loop.-> s2
```

**Flow Interpretation:**
- The FMEA sheet is populated **left-to-right** and **top-to-bottom**.
- RPN dictates the priority of action — highest RPN first.
- Each closed item is **re-rated**, ensuring a measurable reduction in residual risk.

---

## 4.3 Severity / Occurrence / Detection — Rating Reference

```mermaid
graph LR
    sev["SEVERITY S 1 Negligible to 10 Hazardous"]
    occ["OCCURRENCE O 1 Very Unlikely to 10 Very Frequent"]
    det["DETECTION D 1 Almost Certain Detection to 10 No Detection"]
    rpn["RPN equals S times O times D Range 1 to 1000"]
    sev --> rpn
    occ --> rpn
    det --> rpn
```

---

## 4.4 Benchmarking vs FMEA — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["BENCHMARKING"]
    B2["External Best Practice Reference"]
    B3["Gap Analysis and Improvement Goal"]
    F1["FMEA"]
    F2["Internal Failure Prediction"]
    F3["Risk Prioritization and Corrective Action"]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    B3 -.combined power.-> F3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Benchmarking tells an organization *what to aspire to*; FMEA tells it *what to fix first*. Used together, they form a powerful **diagnostic + preventive** quality strategy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define benchmarking. Mention the five phases of the benchmarking process. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Benchmarking is a systematic, continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against best-in-class competitors to identify gaps and implement improvements.

**Five Phases (Robert C. Camp's Model):**
1. **Planning** — Define what to benchmark and select partners.
2. **Analysis** — Collect data and compute performance gaps.
3. **Integration** — Communicate findings and set targets.
4. **Action** — Implement improvements with monitoring.
5. **Maturity** — Embed benchmarking into culture; review continuously.

---

### Q2. What is FMEA? State the formula for Risk Priority Number (RPN). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a systematic, proactive methodology used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate potential failures in a product, process, or system before they reach the customer.

**RPN Formula:**

$$
\text{RPN} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Occurrence (O)} \times \text{Detection (D)}
$$

**Range:** RPN lies between **1 and 1000**; action is generally triggered when **RPN > 100** or **Severity ≥ 9**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Benchmarking & FMEA:**
> - Students often **miss naming the model** (e.g., "Camp's 5-phase model") — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if not explicitly mentioned.
> - For RPN problems, students forget the **action threshold logic** — stating RPN value alone is insufficient; always write whether the value triggers action.
> - Sketches of the FMEA worksheet (table) earn easy 2 marks — **always draw the FMEA table**.
> - Vague definitions like "FMEA is a quality tool" lose marks — specify it is *proactive, systematic, and team-based*.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the five phases of the benchmarking process as given by Robert C. Camp. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is FMEA? List and explain any **five types of failure modes** with an engineering example each. Compute the RPN for the following failure data and state whether corrective action is required. (7 marks)

| Failure Mode | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|--------------|-----|-----|-----|
| Bearing seizure | 9 | 5 | 6 |
| Wire harness crack | 7 | 3 | 8 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Five Phases of Benchmarking (Robert C. Camp, 1989):**

| Phase | Activity | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| **1. Planning** | Identify subject, choose partner, form team | Benchmarking plan |
| **2. Analysis** | Data collection, gap analysis, root cause | Performance gap report |
| **3. Integration** | Communicate to leadership, set targets | SMART improvement goals |
| **4. Action** | Implement changes, monitor KPIs | Standardized process |
| **5. Maturity** | Embed practice organization-wide | Continuous improvement culture |

**Key Concept:** Each phase produces a deliverable that feeds the next phase; the cycle is continuous, not linear.

**(b)** **FMEA Definition:** A proactive, systematic, team-based methodology used to identify potential failure modes, assess their effects, and prioritize corrective actions to enhance reliability and safety.

**Five Types of Failure Modes (with examples):**

| # | Failure Mode | Example |
|---|--------------|---------|
| 1 | Catastrophic | Brake failure in a vehicle |
| 2 | Degraded | Reduced battery capacity over time |
| 3 | Intermittent | Sensor giving erratic readings |
| 4 | Sudden complete | Power transformer explosion |
| 5 | Gradual wear | Tyre tread wearing out |

**RPN Calculation:**

For **Bearing seizure**:

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = 9 \times 5 \times 6 = 270
$$

For **Wire harness crack**:

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 7 \times 3 \times 8 = 168
$$

**Decision:**
- Bearing seizure: $S = 9 \geq 9$ → **mandatory corrective action**; RPN = 270 ≫ 100.
- Wire harness crack: RPN = 168 > 100 → **corrective action required** (improve detection by adding continuity test).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **various stages of FMEA**. Discuss the importance of Severity, Occurrence, and Detection ratings with their typical 1–10 scale interpretations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A manufacturing process has the following FMEA data. Compute the RPN for each item, rank them, and recommend the top-priority corrective action. (7 marks)

| Process Step | Failure Mode | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|--------------|--------------|-----|-----|-----|
| Welding | Porosity in weld | 8 | 4 | 7 |
| Painting | Uneven coating | 5 | 6 | 5 |
| Assembly | Missing bolt | 9 | 3 | 4 |
| Packaging | Box crush | 4 | 7 | 6 |
| Testing | False pass | 10 | 2 | 8 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stages of FMEA:**

1. **Define the scope** and assemble a cross-functional team.
2. **Identify potential failure modes** for each component/process step.
3. **Determine effects and causes** of each failure.
4. **Rate Severity (S)** — consequence to the customer.
5. **Rate Occurrence (O)** — likelihood of failure.
6. **Rate Detection (D)** — probability the control will *miss* the failure.
7. **Calculate RPN = S × O × D**.
8. **Prioritize** failures with the highest RPN (or S ≥ 9).
9. **Recommend corrective action** to reduce S, O, or D.
10. **Re-evaluate** the RPN after action is implemented.

**S, O, D Scale Interpretation:**

| Rating | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|--------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Negligible effect | Very unlikely | Almost certain detection |
| 4 | Minor inconvenience | Low | Moderate detection |
| 7 | Major loss of function | High | Poor detection |
| 10 | Hazardous / safety risk | Very frequent | No detection possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for students:** $S$ is never reduced by action — only $O$ and $D$ can typically be lowered. Reducing $S$ requires **redesign**, not corrective action.

**(b) RPN Computation & Ranking:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{Welding}} &= 8 \times 4 \times 7 = 224 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Painting}} &= 5 \times 6 \times 5 = 150 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Assembly}} &= 9 \times 3 \times 4 = 108 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Packaging}} &= 4 \times 7 \times 6 = 168 \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Testing}} &= 10 \times 2 \times 8 = 160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Highest to Lowest):**

| Rank | Process Step | RPN |
|------|--------------|-----|
| 1 | Welding — Porosity | **224** |
| 2 | Packaging — Box crush | 168 |
| 3 | Testing — False pass | 160 |
| 4 | Painting — Uneven coating | 150 |
| 5 | Assembly — Missing bolt | 108 |

**Top-Priority Recommendation:**
- **Welding porosity** (RPN = 224) → Add **automated ultrasonic testing** post-weld (reduces $D$); train welders and improve shielding gas quality (reduces $O$).
- **Mandatory note:** Testing — False pass has $S = 10$ (highest severity), so although RPN = 160, it must also be treated as **top priority due to severity alone**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Benchmarking & FMEA**

### Benchmarking
- Defined by **Robert C. Camp (1989)** — five phases: **Plan → Analyze → Integrate → Act → Mature**.
- Cycle is **continuous**, not a one-time activity.
- Code of conduct has **7 principles** (legality, ethics, confidentiality, etc.).
- Gap analysis uses **absolute gap** ($P_y - P_b$) and **percentage gap** formulas.
- Types: Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic.

### FMEA
- Acronym: **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis**.
- Three types: **System, Design, Process** FMEA.
- Three rating scales, each **1 to 10**: Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D).
- **RPN = S × O × D**, range **1 to 1000**.
- Action threshold: **RPN > 100** OR **Severity ≥ 9**.
- **S cannot be reduced by corrective action** — only O and D can typically be reduced.
- FMEA is **proactive** — done *before* the failure occurs in the field.
- Cross-functional team is mandatory for credibility.
- Documented in a standard worksheet (FMEA sheet).
- AIAG-VDA Handbook (2019) is the modern reference in the automotive industry.

### Examiner Triggers
- Always **name the model** (e.g., Camp's 5-phase).
- Always **draw the FMEA table** in answers.
- Always **state action threshold logic** for any RPN value.
- Always **close the loop** — show post-action re-rating whenever possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Quality Function Development (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT754 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types - Quality Function Development (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Quality Function Deployment (QFD)?

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and engineering methodology used to translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable technical and engineering requirements at every stage of product and service development. It is a total quality management tool that ensures the final product or service is designed to deliver what the customer actually values, rather than what the manufacturer assumes is important.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> *QFD is a systematic process for translating customer requirements (WHATs) into appropriate technical/engineering requirements (HOWs) for each stage of product development, manufacturing, and service delivery, with the aim of achieving total customer satisfaction.*

The cornerstone of QFD is a matrix tool called the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, which resembles a house when drawn — hence the name. QFD was originally conceived in **1972** at the **Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** in Japan by engineers **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno** to design an ocean-going tanker.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Custom Home"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Easy to Remember for Exams):**
> Imagine you are a builder, and a customer tells you: *"I want a house that is safe, comfortable, energy-efficient, and beautiful."* These are abstract desires (WHATs). Before laying a single brick, you must convert them into specific engineering specifications: wall thickness in **mm**, window-to-wall ratio, **R-value** of insulation, concrete grade **M30**, etc. (HOWs). QFD acts like a **translator + prioritization system** that ensures every technical decision made on the construction site is traceable back to a real customer wish. Without it, you might build a perfect house (technically) that the customer never wanted.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Different "Types" of QFD?

Since its invention, QFD has evolved into several models adapted to different industry needs, organizational maturity, and product complexity. The **"Types of QFD"** topic is essentially asking: *In how many structured matrix stages can we deploy the customer's voice, and who pioneered each approach?*

The four principal types studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **The 3-Phase (Akao/Mizuno) QFD Model** — the original Japanese approach.
2. **The 4-Phase (Akao Extended) QFD Model** — adds one operational phase.
3. **The American Supplier Institute (ASI) / 27-Matrix Model** — fully expanded Western model.
4. **GOAL/QPC Model (Single House of Quality)** — the simplest, most widely taught variant.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlight)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of the Customer — raw customer needs and wants |
| **WHATs** | Customer requirements (left side of the HOQ) |
| **HOWs** | Engineering / technical characteristics (ceiling of the HOQ) |
| **Roof** | Correlation matrix between HOWs (top of the house) |
| **Relationship Matrix** | Strong (9), Medium (3), Weak (1) links between WHATs & HOWs |
| **Absolute & Relative Weight** | Importance of each customer requirement (numerical priority) |
| **Target Value** | Quantified goal for each HOW |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the relationship values **9 – 3 – 1** (Strong – Medium – Weak). They are repeatedly asked in KTU Part-A questions (3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Types of QFD — Detailed Breakdown

### **Type 1: 3-Phase QFD Model (Akao & Mizuno — Original Japanese Model)**

This is the **classical Japanese model** that defined QFD. Customer requirements are deployed through **three sequential matrices** (sometimes loosely called "three houses of quality"):

- **Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality 1):**
  Translates customer requirements (WHATs) into engineering characteristics (HOWs). Most popularly used; the only phase often taught in undergraduate courses.

- **Phase 2 — Parts/Component Deployment (House of Quality 2):**
  The HOWs of Phase 1 become the WHATs. They are deployed into **critical part characteristics** — what each component of the product must deliver.

- **Phase 3 — Process/Manufacturing Planning (House of Quality 3):**
  The part characteristics are deployed into **manufacturing process requirements** — tolerances, machine settings, process steps, inspection methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Result of 3-Phase QFD:** A complete document chain from **Customer → Engineering → Parts → Process**, ensuring no requirement is "lost in translation" across the design-to-production pipeline.

### **Type 2: 4-Phase QFD Model (Extended Akao Model)**

Adds one operational matrix to the 3-phase model:

- **Phase 4 — Production/Service Planning (House of Quality 4):**
  Process requirements from Phase 3 are deployed into **day-to-day production controls** — workforce skills, tooling, logistics, quality control plans, and field service instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> The 4-Phase model is preferred in **service industries, software firms, and continuous-flow production** environments (relevant to industries in Kerala's IT and healthcare sectors).

### **Type 3: American Supplier Institute (ASI) / 27-Matrix Model (Bob King)**

Developed by **Bob King** (1989) of the American Supplier Institute, this is the **most exhaustive** Western model. It uses **27 inter-related matrices** (often drawn as 30+ in extended versions), organized across four levels:

1. **Customer Requirements (Sub-matrices: A1–A6)**
2. **Technical Requirements / Engineering Characteristics (B1–B4)**
3. **Component / Part Characteristics (C1–C4)**
4. **Process / Manufacturing Operations (D1–D4)**
5. **Production Requirements (E1–E3)** *(optional 5th level)*

The **27** = 3 × 3 × 3, but with extensions can reach 30+ matrices (hence the alternative name **30-Matrix Model**). The first matrix (A1) is the famous House of Quality itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Which type of QFD is most comprehensive?"* → Answer: **ASI / 27-Matrix Model by Bob King**. If asked *"Who developed the original QFD?"* → Answer: **Yoji Akao (Mitsubishi, 1972)**.

### **Type 4: GOAL/QPC Model (Single House of Quality)**

Propagated by the **Growth Opportunity Alliance of Lawrence (GOAL) / Quality, Productivity, Center (QPC)** of Massachusetts, USA. This is the **simplest, most widely taught** variant and uses a **single House of Quality**.

- Focuses only on the **Product Planning Matrix** (Phase 1).
- Most suitable for **small-scale industries, academic projects, and SMEs** — highly relevant to Kerala's startup/MSME ecosystem.
- Does **not** propagate requirements into parts, process, and production phases (a key limitation).

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix of the Four Types (Mandatory for KTU Answer Writing)

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar issue in LaTeX is handled using `\vert` instead of `|`. This table is the most likely **2- or 7-mark Part B sub-question** in KTU 2024.

| Feature | 3-Phase (Akao) | 4-Phase (Extended) | ASI / 27-Matrix (King) | GOAL/QPC (Single HOQ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin / Country** | Japan (1972) | Japan (Extended) | USA (1989) | USA (GOAL/QPC) |
| **Pioneer** | Akao & Mizuno | Akao (refined) | Bob King | GOAL/QPC |
| **No. of Matrices** | 3 | 4 | 27 (or 30+) | 1 |
| **Complexity** | Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High | Low |
| **Focus / Coverage** | Customer → Process | Customer → Production | End-to-end (Customer → Service) | Product planning only |
| **Best Suited For** | Manufacturing | Service / Continuous Flow | Complex engineered products | SMEs, academic, training |
| **Suitability in Kerala Context** | Good for industrial mfg. | Good for IT & service | Overkill for SMEs | Excellent for startups & classroom use |
| **Drawback** | Ignores production | Resource intensive | Time-consuming | Incomplete deployment chain |
| **Key Output** | Process specifications | Production QC plans | Field service & lifecycle plan | Engineering targets only |

## 2.3 High-Yield Formulas & Numeric System (House of Quality)

These formulas are used inside the **single House of Quality** (the most common exam variant).

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
